responses: The ankle has ligaments on the outside which stop excessive movement. An ankle sprain is when these ligaments become overstretched and tear. A ligament injury can also be referred to as a soft tissue injury.The severity of the damage could range from a small tear to a full rupture and the severity will determine the time it takes to make a full recovery.

patterns: What are the symptoms of an ankle sprain?
Tell me about the symptoms of an ankle sprain
Explain the symptoms of an ankle sprain
responses: These vary from person to person and some symptoms may appear several days after the initial injury.People may experience:Swelling and bruising.Pain.Mild heat and redness.Loss of movement and strength around the ankle and foot.Difficulty walking or going up or down stairs.Tingling, numbness, or pins and needles in the affected area.

patterns: What causes a sprained ankle?
Tell me the causes of a sprained ankle
Explain the causes of a sprained ankle
responses: The injury is usually caused when over-stretching the ligaments on the outside part of the ankle. This is sometimes called going over or rolling over your ankle. It could also result from a direct blow during sporting activity or a fall.

patterns: How should I manage my ankle after a sprain?
Tell me how to manage my ankle after a sprain
Explain how to manage your ankle after a sprain
responses: The first thing to do after an ankle sprain is manage the pain and swelling.Reducing the swelling after an ankle sprain helps reduce the pain and increase the range of movement.As soon as possible, gradually start to move the ankle through your normal range of movement. This may be uncomfortable, but it’s important to start as soon as possible to help your recovery.Stop if this becomes too painful for you and get in touch with your healthcare professional.

patterns: What is the rehabilitation for an ankle sprain?
Tell me about rehabilitation of an ankle sprain
Explain rehabilitation for an ankle sprain
responses: The aim of rehabilitation is to improve muscle strength, joint stability, and balance and coordination. If you’re diagnosed with an ankle sprain, your healthcare professional will discuss specific rehabilitation goals with you. However, the general timescales for recovery are as follows: 0-2 weeks, where there should be an improvement in movement and swelling, making standing and walking easier; 2-4 weeks, when walking returns to normal, movement approaches 100%, and swelling reduces; and 8-12 weeks, when your ability to perform day-to-day activities starts to return to normal. Every soft tissue injury is different, so your recovery may take slightly longer or shorter than these timescales, but the most important thing is that progress is being made. Recovery can have setbacks, and it's important to pace your activities to aid the healing process.

patterns: Can I go back to work after an ankle sprain?
Tell me about returning to work after an ankle sprain
Explain returning to work after an ankle sprain
responses: An ankle sprain can sometimes mean you need to take some time off work to help recovery. How long you’re off will depend on the nature of your condition and your role at work.You do not need to be symptom-free before you consider returning to work. Continuing to go to work, or returning to work as soon as is possible for you, will help your recovery. Gradually getting back to your normal daily activities can help to build up your strength and stamina levels.

patterns: What help and support is available for an ankle sprain?
Tell me about help and support for ankle sprains
Explain help and support for ankle sprains
responses: Following this advice, you should see gradual improvements over time.If your symptoms haven’t improved within 8-12 weeks, or if they’ve gotten worse, after following this advice, it’s a good idea to talk to a healthcare professional about your symptoms.Find out how to access MSK services in your area.When dealing with any health condition, it’s important to also look after your mental well-being as this can impact your recovery.

patterns: Tell me about recovery times for ankle avulsion fracture
What are the recovery times for ankle avulsion fracture?
Explain recovery times for ankle avulsion fracture?
responses: Most of the healing happens between 4 to 6 weeks after the fracture. It’s normal to have aches and discomfort beyond this. This often happens when you try activities you haven’t done for a while.It’s also normal for the area to be more sensitive for several months after the injury.

patterns: Tell me about what can affect your recovery?
What can affect your recovery?
Explain what can affect your recovery?
responses: There are some things that may affect your recovery.

patterns: Tell me about what to expect after an ankle avulsion or 5th metatarsal fracture
What should I expect after an ankle avulsion or 5th metatarsal fracture?
Explain what to expect after an ankle avulsion or 5th metatarsal fracture
responses: This type of fracture is a minor fracture. It’s often treated in the same way as a strain or sprain. Some people may be fitted with a boot or plaster. Some might be given crutches.Walking on your foot can help your recovery but may be uncomfortable at first.

patterns: Tell me about pain after a fracture
What is pain after a fracture?
Explain pain after a fracture
responses: It’s normal to have some discomfort in the areas around your fracture, including:Other soft tissues,Nearby joints,Areas that have been immobilized,Pain can change from day to day and it doesn’t always depend on what you’re doing. It’s common to have pain at rest.It’s normal to have some pain even when your fracture has healed. Some people also experience discomfort in the fracture site during colder weather.Your local pharmacy can give you advice on managing pain after a fracture.

patterns: Tell me about swelling
What is swelling?
Explain swelling
responses: Swelling is the start of healing. It’s normal to have it near the fracture first. It often spreads down your leg.The amount of swelling changes with:Your position, Your activity,The time of day, Even when your fracture has healed, it’s normal to have some swelling. This can last for several months.

patterns: Tell me about how can I help swelling?
How can I help swelling?
Explain how I can help swelling?
responses: Having a lot of swelling can become uncomfortable or limit your movement. However, there are some things you can do to help the swelling. You can:Raise your foot above your heart when you’re resting – you can do this by supporting it with pillows.Keep the other joints in your leg moving normally.Avoid too much rest and too much activity.Massage the swelling from your foot towards your groin.

patterns: Tell me about bruising
What is bruising?
Explain bruising
responses: It’s normal to have bruising after a fracture.Bruising can be widespread and may appear a long way from your fracture. It can be very purple to start with and may change color as you recover.

patterns: Tell me about getting back to normal activities
What is getting back to normal activities?
Explain getting back to normal activities
responses: In general, you should gradually pace yourself back to your activities as you feel able.

patterns: Tell me about bone scanning
What is bone scanning?
Explain bone scanning
responses: You may be sent a letter inviting you for a scan of your bone density after a fracture.It’s routine to be assessed for any further risk of fracture, especially if you:are over the age of 50, have increased risk factors for fracture

patterns: Tell me about rehabilitation plan
What is rehabilitation plan?
Explain rehabilitation plan
responses: There are things you can do to help your recovery after an ankle fracture.

patterns: Tell me about help and support
What is help and support?
Explain help and support
responses: If your ankle function hasn’t improved within 6 weeks of following this advice, it’s a good idea to talk to a healthcare professional about your symptoms.

patterns: Tell me about gentle exercises
What is gentle exercises?
Explain gentle exercises
responses: Practise gentle foot and ankle exercises to build up strength and movement.

patterns: Tell me about generalised anxiety disorder
What is generalised anxiety disorder?
Explain generalised anxiety disorder
responses: GAD is a long-term condition. It causes you to feel anxious about a wide range of issues, rather than 1 specific event.People with GAD feel anxious most days. They often struggle to remember the last time they felt relaxed.As soon as 1 anxious thought is resolved, another may appear about a different issue.

patterns: Tell me about symptoms of generalised anxiety disorder
What is symptoms of generalised anxiety disorder?
Explain symptoms of generalised anxiety disorder
responses: GAD can cause a change in your behavior and the way you think and feel about things, as well as physical changes. Symptoms include restlessness, a sense of dread, feeling constantly on edge difficulty concentrating, irritability, dizziness, tiredness, a noticeably strong, fast, or irregular heartbeat (palpitations), muscle aches and tension, trembling or shaking, dry mouth, excessive sweating, shortness of breath, stomach ache, feeling sick, headache, pins and needles, and difficulty falling or staying asleep (insomnia). These symptoms may lead to withdrawal from social contact to avoid feelings of worry and dread, and you may find work difficult and stressful, potentially taking time off sick. These actions can worsen your worries and increase your lack of self-esteem.

patterns: Tell me about anxiety triggers
What is anxiety triggers?
Explain anxiety triggers
responses: If you’re anxious as a result of a specific phobia or because of panic disorder, you’ll usually know what the cause is. For example, if you have claustrophobia (a fear of enclosed spaces), you know that being confined in a small space will trigger your anxiety.However, if you have GAD, it may not always be clear what you’re feeling anxious about. Not knowing what triggers your anxiety can intensify it. You may start to worry that there’ll be no solution.

patterns: Tell me about causes of generalised anxiety disorder
What is causes of generalised anxiety disorder?
Explain causes of generalised anxiety disorder
responses: The exact cause of GAD is not fully understood. It’s likely that a combination of several factors plays a role, like:overactivity in areas of the brain involved in emotions and behaviour, an imbalance of the brain chemicals serotonin and noradrenaline, which are involved in the control and regulation of mood,the genes you inherit from your parents – you’re estimated to be 5 times more likely to develop GAD if a close relative has it, having a history of stressful or traumatic experiences, like domestic violence, child abuse or bullying, having a painful long-term health condition, like arthritis,having a history of drug or alcohol misuse.However, many people develop GAD for no apparent reason.

patterns: Tell me about treating generalised anxiety disorder
What is treating generalised anxiety disorder?
Explain treating generalised anxiety disorder
responses: GAD can have a significant effect on your daily life, but there are several treatments available that can help. These include talking therapies, such as cognitive behavioral therapy (CBT), and medication like selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors (SSRIs), a type of antidepressant. Additionally, there are things you can do yourself to help reduce your anxiety, such as trying a self-help course, exercising regularly, stopping smoking, cutting down on alcohol, and drinking less caffeine. With treatment, many people are able to control their anxiety levels, although some treatments may need to be continued for a long time, and there may be periods where your symptoms worsen.

patterns: Tell me about referral to a specialist
What is referral to a specialist?
Explain referral to a specialist
responses: If the treatments you've tried aren’t helping, you may want to discuss with your GP whether you should be referred to a mental health specialist. You'll usually be referred to your community mental health team, which includes a range of specialists such as psychiatrists, psychiatric nurses, clinical psychologists, occupational therapists, and social workers. A mental health specialist from your local team will reassess your condition by asking about your previous treatments and how effective they were. Based on this, they will be able to create a treatment plan for you, which may include a new treatment you haven't tried before or a combination of psychological treatment and medication.

patterns: Tell me about what are the signs of anxiety in children?
What is what are the signs of anxiety in children??
Explain what are the signs of anxiety in children?
responses: Anxiety can make a child feel scared, panicky, embarrassed, or ashamed. Some signs to look out for include difficulty concentrating, not sleeping or waking up at night, not eating properly, quickly getting angry or irritable, constantly worrying or having negative thoughts, feeling tense and fidgety, or using the toilet often. Other signs are always crying, being clingy, complaining of tummy aches or feeling unwell, being withdrawn, and not wanting to do things they once enjoyed. Your child may not be old enough to understand why they’re feeling this way. The cause of the anxiety (if there is one) can vary depending on the child's age. Younger children may experience separation anxiety, while older children and teenagers tend to worry more about school performance, relationships, or health.

patterns: Tell me about what types of anxiety do children and teenagers experience?
What is what types of anxiety do children and teenagers experience??
Explain what types of anxiety do children and teenagers experience?
responses: The common types of anxiety in children and teenagers are :

patterns: Tell me about when is anxiety a disorder that needs treating?
What is when is anxiety a disorder that needs treating??
Explain when is anxiety a disorder that needs treating?
responses: You should seek help for your child’s anxiety if you feel it’s not getting better or is getting worse, and efforts to tackle it yourself have not worked. It’s important to seek help if you think the anxiety is slowing down their development or significantly affecting their schooling or relationships. If the anxiety happens very frequently or your child feels anxious all the time, or if it’s making them feel physically unwell, like feeling sick or having constant headaches, it's time to seek professional help. Additionally, if your child becomes very withdrawn and is anxious about doing things they used to enjoy, it’s crucial to get support.

patterns: Tell me about how serious can it be?
What is how serious can it be??
Explain how serious can it be?
responses: Long-term anxiety can severely interfere with a child’s personal development, family life, and schooling. Anxiety disorders that start in childhood often persist into the teenage years and early adulthood. Teenagers with an anxiety disorder may also experience clinical depression, feel hopeless, and misuse drugs, further complicating their emotional and psychological well-being.

patterns: Tell me about what can i do to help my child?
What is what can i do to help my child??
Explain what can i do to help my child?
responses: If a child is experiencing anxiety, there’s plenty parents and carers can do to help. First, it’s important to listen to your child about their anxiety or worries, offering support and understanding. Resources like YoungMinds provide helpful advice for parents, while YoungScot offers support specifically for young people, ensuring both parents and children have access to the guidance they need.

patterns: Tell me about where I should go for help?
Where should I go for help?
Where can I go for help with my child's anxiety?
responses: You can talk to your GP or someone at your child’s school, such as the school nurse, about your child’s anxiety. You may choose to do this on your own or with your child, or your child might be able to attend an appointment without you. The healthcare professional should listen to your concerns and offer advice on what to do next. If needed, your child may be referred to the local child and adolescent mental health service (CAMHS), where professionals like psychologists, psychiatrists, nurses, occupational therapists, physiotherapists, and other specially trained experts are available to help. CAMHS services are designed to support both the child and their parents or carers.

patterns: How are anxiety disorders treated in children?
How do you treat anxiety disorders in children?
What are the treatments for anxiety disorders in children?
responses: The type of treatment offered will depend on what is causing your child’s anxiety. It can be helpful for your child to talk in confidence about their worries to a trained professional, especially someone they don’t know. If your child is being seen at CAMHS, they may meet with a child and adolescent psychotherapist or a clinical psychologist. Alternatively, if they’re at a youth counselling service, they might see a trained youth counsellor or psychotherapist. These sessions can help your child understand what is making them anxious and guide them through strategies to manage and work through the situation.

patterns: Why are some children affected by anxiety and others not?
Why do some children have anxiety while others don't?
What makes some children more anxious than others?
responses: Some children are simply born more nervous and anxious, making them less able to cope with stress than other children. Traumatic life events, such as an illness or accident, can also trigger anxiety, even in children who were previously confident. A child’s anxious personality may be partly determined by the genes they inherit from their parents. Parents of anxious children may recognize these signs and remember experiencing similar feelings and behaviors when they were younger.

patterns: What is appendicitis?
Tell me about appendicitis
Explain what appendicitis is
responses: Appendicitis is a painful swelling of the appendix, a small, thin pouch about 5 to 10cm (2 to 4 inches) long, connected to the large intestine, where stools (faeces) are formed. Although the exact purpose of the appendix is unknown, removing it isn’t harmful. Appendicitis typically begins with pain in the middle of your tummy (abdomen) that may come and go. Within hours, the pain shifts to the lower right-hand side, where the appendix is usually located, becoming constant and severe. Pressing on this area, coughing, or walking may worsen the pain. Other symptoms may include loss of appetite, nausea, and occasionally diarrhoea.

patterns: When should I get medical help for appendicitis?
When should I seek medical help for appendicitis?
What are the signs that I need medical help for appendicitis?
responses: If you’re experiencing abdominal pain that’s gradually getting worse, contact your GP or local out-of-hours service immediately. If these options aren’t available, phone NHS 24’s 111 service for advice. You should phone 999 for an ambulance if you have sudden pain that continues to get worse and spreads across your abdomen. These are signs your appendix may have burst, which can lead to potentially life-threatening complications.

patterns: How is appendicitis treated?
Tell me how appendicitis is treated
Explain how appendicitis is treated
responses: In most cases of appendicitis, the appendix needs to be surgically removed as soon as possible. Removal of the appendix, known as an appendectomy or appendicectomy, is one of the most common operations in the UK and its success rate is excellent. The operation is most commonly performed as keyhole surgery (laparoscopy), which involves making several small cuts in your abdomen, through which special surgical instruments are inserted. Open surgery, where a larger, single cut is made in the abdomen, is usually carried out if the appendix has burst or access is more difficult. Most people make a full recovery from an appendectomy in a couple of weeks, although strenuous activities may need to be avoided for up to 6 weeks after open surgery.

patterns: What causes appendicitis?
Tell me what causes appendicitis
Explain the causes of appendicitis
responses: The exact causes of appendicitis are not entirely clear, but most cases are believed to occur when something blocks the entrance of the appendix. For example, a small piece of faeces or a swollen lymph node caused by an upper respiratory tract infection may block the appendix. This blockage leads to inflammation and swelling, and the increased pressure can eventually cause the appendix to burst. Since the causes aren’t fully understood, there is no guaranteed way to prevent appendicitis.

patterns: Who is affected by appendicitis?
Who gets appendicitis?
Tell me who is most affected by appendicitis
responses: Appendicitis is a common condition. It’s estimated around 1 in every 13 people develop it at some point in their life. Appendicitis can develop at any age, but it’s most common in young people aged from 10 to 20 years old.

patterns: What is arterial thrombosis?
Tell me about arterial thrombosis
Explain what arterial thrombosis is
responses: Arterial thrombosis is a blood clot that develops in an artery. It’s dangerous as it can obstruct or stop the flow of blood to major organs, such as the heart or brain. If a blood clot narrows one or more of the arteries leading to the heart, muscle pain known as angina can occur. If a blood clot blocks the arteries leading to part of the heart muscle, it will cause a heart attack. If it blocks an artery in the brain, it will cause a stroke. Symptoms therefore depend on where the blood clot has formed.

patterns: Who is at risk of arterial thrombosis?
Tell me who’s at risk of arterial thrombosis?
What factors increase the risk of arterial thrombosis?
responses: Most cases of arterial thrombosis are caused when a process called atherosclerosis damages an artery. Fatty deposits build up on the walls of the arteries and cause them to harden and narrow.

patterns: How is arterial thrombosis treated?
Tell me about treating arterial thrombosis
What are the treatments for arterial thrombosis?
responses: There are two main types of treatment for arterial thrombosis: medication and surgical intervention. Medication often includes blood thinners, which can prevent the clot from growing or new clots from forming. In more severe cases, surgery may be needed to remove the clot or bypass the blocked artery.

patterns: How can I prevent arterial thrombosis?
What should I do to prevent arterial thrombosis?
Tell me about preventing arterial thrombosis
responses:
If you’ve had a blood clot in an artery, you may need to take medication to prevent it from happening again. It's also important to live a healthy lifestyle, which includes: 
- Stopping smoking (if you smoke)
- Doing regular physical activity
- Reducing the amount of salt and saturated fat that you eat

patterns: Tell me about types of arthritis
What are the types of arthritis?
Explain types of arthritis
responses: The two most common types of arthritis are osteoarthritis and rheumatoid arthritis. Other types include ankylosing spondylitis, gout, psoriatic arthritis, enteropathic arthritis, reactive arthritis, and secondary arthritis. Related conditions include cervical spondylosis, fibromyalgia, lupus, and polymyalgia rheumatica.

patterns: Tell me about arthritis in children
What is arthritis in children?
Explain arthritis in children
responses: Arthritis is often thought of as a condition affecting older people, but it can also affect children. The most common type in children is juvenile idiopathic arthritis (JIA), which causes pain and inflammation in one or more joints for at least six weeks. The exact cause of JIA is unknown, but the symptoms often improve as the child grows, allowing them to lead a normal life.

patterns: Tell me about symptoms of arthritis
What are the symptoms of arthritis?
Explain symptoms of arthritis
responses: Symptoms of arthritis vary depending on the type. Common symptoms include joint pain, stiffness, swelling, and reduced mobility. The severity and type of symptoms depend on the specific arthritis you have.

patterns: Tell me about treatment for arthritis
What is treatment for arthritis?
Explain treatment for arthritis
responses: There’s no cure for arthritis, but many treatments can help manage the condition. The treatment you receive will depend on the type of arthritis you have, and often a combination of treatments is required. Common treatments include painkillers, non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drugs (NSAIDs), corticosteroids, disease-modifying anti-rheumatic drugs (DMARDs), physiotherapy, and regular exercise. In severe cases, surgery may be considered, including joint replacement, joint fusion, or osteotomy (bone cutting and realignment).

patterns: Tell me about occupational therapy
What is occupational therapy?
Explain occupational therapy
responses: An occupational therapist can assist if arthritis affects your ability to move around your home or carry out everyday tasks such as cooking and cleaning. They can also suggest equipment to help you live more independently. Depending on your arthritis type, your GP may refer you to an NHS occupational therapist.

patterns: Tell me how to protect your joints
What is how to protect your joints?
Explain how to protect your joints
responses: If you have arthritis, protecting your joints is crucial to prevent further damage. Strategies include maintaining a healthy weight, using joint protection techniques, and practicing proper posture.

patterns: Tell me how to do tasks at home with arthritis
What is how to do tasks at home?
Explain how to do tasks at home with arthritis
responses: Managing tasks around the home can be challenging if you have arthritis. Practical changes, such as modifying your home and adjusting how you perform tasks, can help make daily chores easier and less painful.

patterns: Tell me about living with arthritis
What is living with arthritis?
Explain living with arthritis
responses: Living with arthritis can be difficult as it often makes everyday tasks painful and challenging. However, there are many ways to manage the condition, including adopting a healthy lifestyle, staying active, and working with healthcare professionals to manage symptoms.

patterns: Tell me about work and arthritis
What is work with arthritis?
Explain how arthritis affects work
responses: Many people with arthritis want to continue working for reasons like financial security and higher self-esteem. While work can be challenging due to the impact of arthritis, your employer should help provide training and support. Discuss your condition with your line manager or HR to receive appropriate assistance. Treatment can also support your return to work. If your arthritis is severe and prevents you from working, help is available, such as Personal Independence Payments.

patterns: Tell me about diet for arthritis
What is a good diet for arthritis?
Explain the diet for managing arthritis
responses: Eating a healthy, balanced diet is crucial for people with arthritis. A proper diet provides the necessary nutrients and helps maintain a healthy weight. A diet for arthritis should include a variety of foods from all five food groups: fruit and vegetables, starchy foods like bread, rice, potatoes, and pasta, meat, fish, eggs, and beans, milk and dairy foods, and foods containing fat and sugar.

patterns: Tell me about managing weight with arthritis
What is managing your weight when you have arthritis?
Explain how to manage your weight if you have arthritis
responses: For individuals with arthritis, managing your weight is essential. Being overweight puts extra pressure on the joints, especially in areas like the hips, knees, ankles, and feet. Losing weight can help reduce pain and prevent further mobility issues related to arthritis.

patterns: Tell me about exercise for arthritis
What is the best exercise for arthritis?
Explain exercise for people with arthritis
responses: Exercise is essential for people with arthritis, even though it can sometimes be painful. Regular activity helps reduce pain and prevents further joint stiffness. Along with a healthy diet, exercise can help with weight loss, which reduces the strain on your joints. It can also improve joint mobility, range of motion, muscle strength, and decrease stiffness, which is beneficial for managing arthritis.

patterns: Tell me about the telecare self-check tool for arthritis
What is the telecare self-check online tool for arthritis?
Explain how the telecare self-check tool can help with arthritis
responses: The Telecare Self-Check online tool is designed to help you find the right support for arthritis in your area.

patterns: Tell me about symptoms of asthma
What are the symptoms of asthma?
Explain symptoms of asthma
responses: The common symptoms of asthma include coughing, wheezing, chest tightness, and breathlessness. These symptoms can vary from person to person and are often worse at night or early in the morning, or triggered by certain factors. Asthma is manageable for most people, and with proper treatment, it should not restrict your daily life, including your sleep.

patterns: What to do if you’re having an asthma attack?
Tell me what to do during an asthma attack
Explain what to do if you're experiencing an asthma attack
responses: If you're having an asthma attack, immediately take 1 to 2 puffs of your reliever inhaler (usually blue). Use a spacer if you have one. If you don't start feeling better, take 1 puff every minute, up to a total of 10 puffs. If symptoms do not improve, phone 999. If an ambulance does not arrive within 10 minutes and you still feel unwell, continue taking puffs of your reliever inhaler. Even if symptoms improve, it’s essential to see a doctor within 24 hours.

patterns: Tell me about diagnosing asthma
What is involved in diagnosing asthma?
Explain the process of diagnosing asthma
responses: To diagnose asthma, your GP may ask about the timing of your symptoms, how often they occur, any triggers you have noticed, and whether you have other conditions like eczema or hay fever. Your doctor may also perform tests like a peak flow test or spirometry to confirm the diagnosis.

patterns: Tell me about treatment for asthma
What is treatment for asthma?
Explain treatment for asthma
responses: There is no cure for asthma, but there are treatments that can help prevent future symptoms and attacks, as well as relieve your symptoms. Most people with asthma will take medications once or twice daily, usually using an inhaler. Your nurse, doctor, or pharmacist will teach you how to properly use your inhaler. Finding and avoiding asthma triggers is also important. Some treatments may have side effects.

patterns: Tell me about what causes asthma?
What causes asthma?
Explain what causes asthma?
responses: The exact cause of asthma is unclear, but it is likely to be a combination of genetic and environmental factors. You're more likely to have asthma if you have allergies such as eczema, allergic rhinitis, or hay fever, or if asthma runs in your family. Other risk factors include childhood lung infections like bronchiolitis, exposure to tobacco smoke (especially during pregnancy), premature birth, or a low birth weight.

patterns: Tell me about symptoms of ataxia
What are the symptoms of ataxia?
Explain the symptoms of ataxia
responses: People with ataxia often find they’re falling over more or can’t walk in a straight line. Other symptoms include becoming more clumsy, slurred speech, problems with swallowing (which can cause choking or coughing), tremors or shaking, fatigue, and problems with sight like blurred or jumpy vision caused by difficulty controlling eye movements.

patterns: Tell me about types of ataxia
What are the types of ataxia?
Explain types of ataxia
responses: Some types of ataxia are inherited, meaning they're caused by genes passed on by parents. Other types of ataxia that aren't inherited can be caused by different factors like brain damage, diet, or side effects from medication.

patterns: Tell me about causes of ataxia
What causes ataxia?
Explain causes of ataxia
responses: Ataxia is most commonly caused by damage to the cerebellum (the part of the brain that controls coordination), the spinal cord, or nerves. The cerebellum controls functions like eye movements, speech, balance, and the coordination of arms and legs.

patterns: Tell me about diagnosing ataxia
What is diagnosing ataxia?
Explain diagnosing ataxia
responses: Diagnosing ataxia can be challenging due to the similarity of symptoms to other conditions. A neurologist may need to perform multiple tests, and the diagnosis process can take some time. If the ataxia is acquired due to a serious underlying condition, hospitalization may be required. Otherwise, a referral to a neurologist for further tests is common.

patterns: Tell me about treatment for ataxia
What is treatment for ataxia?
Explain treatment for ataxia
responses: There is no cure for ataxia, but if the underlying cause is known, treatments can sometimes improve symptoms or prevent the condition from worsening. Symptomatic treatments can help manage the effects of ataxia.

patterns: Tell me about atopic eczema
What is atopic eczema?
Explain atopic eczema
responses: Atopic eczema, also known as atopic dermatitis, is the most common form of eczema. It typically affects children, though adults can get it too. Eczema causes the skin to become itchy, red, dry, and cracked. It’s a chronic condition for most people but can improve over time. It can affect areas like the back of knees, elbows, hands, neck, and scalp.

patterns: Tell me about what causes atopic eczema?
What causes atopic eczema?
Explain the causes of atopic eczema
responses: The exact cause of atopic eczema is not fully understood, but it's believed to result from a combination of genetic and environmental factors. Eczema often occurs in people who have allergies, like hay fever or asthma. Certain triggers, such as soaps, stress, food allergies, and weather changes, can make symptoms worse.

patterns: Tell me about treating atopic eczema
What is treating atopic eczema?
Explain treating atopic eczema
responses: There’s no cure for atopic eczema, but treatments can help relieve symptoms. Self-care techniques, such as reducing scratching, avoiding triggers, and using emollients (moisturizing treatments) daily, can manage the condition. Topical corticosteroids may also be used during flare-ups to reduce swelling, redness, and itching.

patterns: Tell me about who is affected by atopic eczema
Who is affected by atopic eczema?
Explain who is affected by atopic eczema
responses: Atopic eczema affects about 1 in 5 children in the UK, with most cases developing before the age of 5. In many cases, it improves or even clears as children grow older. However, it can persist into adulthood, and some adults may develop eczema for the first time.

patterns: Tell me about risks of atrial fibrillation
What is risks of atrial fibrillation?
Explain risks of atrial fibrillation
responses: For some people, atrial fibrillation could create blood clots in the heart that may lead to a stroke. The risk of this is influenced by other factors, including age (being older), whether you have high blood pressure, heart failure, diabetes, a previous history of blood clots, and lifestyle factors. Making lifestyle changes and receiving treatment can reduce this risk.

patterns: Tell me about symptoms of atrial fibrillation
What is symptoms of atrial fibrillation?
Explain symptoms of atrial fibrillation
responses: Some people with atrial fibrillation have no symptoms and may not even be aware that they have an irregular heartbeat. It’s sometimes only discovered during routine tests or investigations for another condition. The most obvious symptom of atrial fibrillation is palpitations, which can feel like your heart is pounding, fluttering, or beating irregularly. You may feel these sensations in your chest, throat, or neck. In addition, you may experience tiredness, breathlessness, dizziness, feeling faint, or chest discomfort or pain.

patterns: Tell me about types of atrial fibrillation
What is types of atrial fibrillation?
Explain types of atrial fibrillation
responses: Atrial fibrillation can be classified into several types: intermittent (paroxysmal), where it lasts for a few minutes or hours; persistent, where the episode lasts for more than 7 days; long-term persistent, which is continuous and can last over a year; and permanent, where the condition does not go away.

patterns: Tell me about diagnosing atrial fibrillation
What is diagnosing atrial fibrillation?
Explain diagnosing atrial fibrillation
responses: Atrial fibrillation can sometimes be detected through a pulse check or with a single lead ECG device, such as a sensor attached to the chest or contained in a watch strap. If your doctor suspects atrial fibrillation, you may be referred for other tests, including a 12-lead ECG, which is a more detailed recording of the heart's rhythm from 12 sensors attached to the chest, or an echocardiogram. Treatment may not be necessary if your doctor assesses your health and finds your heart rate returns to normal. The decision to proceed with treatment depends on your specific situation.

patterns: Tell me about treatment for atrial fibrillation
What is treatment for atrial fibrillation?
Explain treatment for atrial fibrillation
responses: Anticoagulant medicines are used to prevent harmful blood clots from forming and are sometimes referred to as blood thinners, although they do not actually thin the blood. They increase the time it takes for blood to form a clot. People with atrial fibrillation who are at risk of a stroke may be prescribed anticoagulant medication to reduce the risk of clots forming in the heart. Other treatments for atrial fibrillation aim to control the rate and rhythm of the heart. These treatments include medications like beta-blockers and anti-arrhythmic drugs, procedures such as ablation, the fitting of a pacemaker, and cardioversion.

patterns: Tell me about symptoms of ADHD in children and teenagers
What is symptoms of ADHD in children and teenagers?
Explain symptoms of ADHD in children and teenagers
responses: Symptoms of ADHD tend to be noticeable at an early age and may become more apparent when a child’s circumstances change, such as when they start school. The main symptoms of ADHD in children and teenagers include inattention, impulsivity, and hyperactivity. Children with ADHD may struggle with tasks that require focus, following instructions, sitting still, and controlling their impulses.

patterns: Tell me about symptoms of ADHD in adults
What is symptoms of ADHD in adults?
Explain symptoms of ADHD in adults
responses: If you’re an adult with ADHD, you may find it difficult to stay focused and may struggle with paying attention to details, especially for tasks you find boring. You may also have trouble listening to others and often interrupt them or finish their sentences. Adults with ADHD can also find it hard to follow instructions, organize themselves, and complete tasks. Other symptoms include impulsivity, forgetfulness, irritability, difficulty with stress, restlessness, and trouble handling situations without thinking things through.

patterns: Tell me about causes of ADHD
What is causes of ADHD?
Explain causes of ADHD
responses: The exact cause of ADHD isn’t fully understood. However, it is believed that a combination of genetic factors and environmental influences contribute to the development of the disorder. ADHD can run in families, but it cannot be predicted. It is possible for children without any family history of ADHD to be diagnosed with the condition.

patterns: Tell me about diagnosing ADHD
What is diagnosing ADHD?
Explain diagnosing ADHD
responses: If you think that you or your child may have ADHD, it is important to speak to a GP. For children, it may also help to consult with teachers to gather information about any concerns they might have regarding your child’s behavior. A GP cannot formally diagnose ADHD, but they can discuss your concerns and refer you for a specialist assessment if needed.

patterns: Tell me about treating ADHD
What is treating ADHD?
Explain treating ADHD
responses: ADHD can be treated with a combination of medication and therapy. Treatment is often arranged by a specialist, such as a pediatrician or psychiatrist. Medications commonly used to treat ADHD include stimulants and non-stimulants. Behavioral therapy and strategies to help improve organizational skills and control impulsive behavior are also frequently used.

patterns: Tell me about further support
What is further support?
Explain further support
responses: The Scottish ADHD Coalition provides information about local support groups in Scotland. Procrastination Station is a Community Interest Company that offers advice and support to adults with ADHD in Scotland.

patterns: Tell me about characteristics of autism
What is characteristics of autism?
Explain characteristics of autism
responses: Autistic people often have a unique way of interacting with the world. They may experience sights, sounds, smells, tastes, and textures differently from others. They can also find unfamiliar or unpredictable situations more stressful. Many autistic individuals thrive in a routine, and their communication and social interactions might differ from neurotypical individuals. It's important to note that autism is a spectrum, and every autistic person is different in how they experience the world.

patterns: Tell me about autism is different for everyone
What is autism is different for everyone?
Explain autism is different for everyone
responses: Autism is a spectrum disorder, meaning it affects people in different ways. While there are common traits, each autistic person experiences the condition uniquely. Some individuals might need more support than others to live the lives they want. The way autism affects an individual can change with age and the environments they experience.

patterns: Tell me about talking about autism
What is talking about autism?
Explain talking about autism
responses: Most autistic individuals prefer the term ‘autistic’ to describe themselves, which is known as identity-first language (e.g., ‘I’m autistic’). Some people still prefer person-first language, such as ‘a person with autism’. It’s always best to ask an autistic person which term they prefer.

patterns: Tell me about causes of autism
What is causes of autism?
Explain causes of autism
responses: Autism is believed to be primarily genetic, involving multiple genes. It can run in families, but it cannot be predicted, and it can also develop in individuals without a family history of autism.

patterns: How common is autism?
What is the prevalence of autism?
How often is autism diagnosed?
responses: At least 1 in 100 people in Scotland are autistic. The true number may be higher. For example, statistics from the Scottish Government suggest 4.28% of children attending school are autistic. Autism can often be recognized and diagnosed in childhood, but people might not be diagnosed until adulthood. Research suggests that women, those assigned female at birth, and minority ethnic groups are more likely not to be diagnosed until later in life. Early support can be helpful for autistic people. But, even if you’re not diagnosed until adulthood, getting a diagnosis may help you identify your strengths and the things you struggle with. It may also help you find support.

patterns: What other conditions are common with autism?
What conditions often co-occur with autism?
Can autism be linked to other conditions?
responses: Autistic people are more likely to have another neurodevelopmental condition like Attention Deficit Hyperactivity Disorder (ADHD). They may also have difficulties with language development, have a learning disability, or both. People with one diagnosis may also have traits of other neurodevelopmental conditions. Autism can be associated with some genetic conditions like Down’s syndrome and Fragile X. Autistic people are more likely to experience low mood or anxiety, which can include specific anxiety-related conditions like situational mutism. They can also experience eating disorders like anorexia nervosa and avoidant restrictive food intake disorder (ARFID).

patterns: What causes sudden or worsening back pain?
Why does back pain sometimes worsen suddenly?
Can back pain get worse suddenly?
responses: Back pain can sometimes be so sudden and severe that you end up on the floor and in too much pain to move. If this happens, there are techniques you can use to get back on your feet safely and in relative comfort. Only attempt this if you feel well. Additional symptoms like stomach or abdominal pain can indicate other conditions, which should be discussed with a Healthcare Professional.

patterns: Can back pain cause pain elsewhere?
Does back pain affect other parts of the body?
Can back pain spread to other areas?
responses: Your back problem may cause hot, burning, shooting, or stabbing pains into one or both of your legs. You may also get pins and needles. This can be due to nerve irritation.

patterns: What causes back problems?
Why do back problems happen?
What are the reasons for back pain?
responses: Although most back problems start for no obvious reason, back pain can be influenced by staying in one position too long, lifting something awkwardly, a flare-up of an existing problem, or doing more activity than you normally would.

patterns: How can I help myself with back pain?
What self-help techniques are there for back pain?
How can I manage back pain on my own?
responses: Keeping active is an essential part of your treatment and recovery and is the single best thing you can do for your health. Exercising can really help your back and reduce the pain you feel. Read more about keeping active.

patterns: Can I go back to work with back pain?
Should I work if I have back pain?
Is it okay to work while recovering from back pain?
responses: It’s recommended you stay at or return to work as quickly as possible during your recovery. You don’t need to be pain and symptom-free to return to work.

patterns: Where can I find help for my back pain?
What support is available for back pain?
How can I get support for my back problems?
responses: Following this advice, you should see gradual improvements over time. You should see the biggest change in your symptoms within the first couple of weeks. Most problems should have improved within 6 weeks. If your back problem hasn’t improved within 6 weeks of following this advice, it’s a good idea to talk to a healthcare professional about your symptoms. Find out how to access MSK services in your area.

patterns: What are the symptoms of bacterial vaginosis?
How can I tell if I have bacterial vaginosis?
What symptoms are linked to bacterial vaginosis?
responses: Often there are no symptoms of bacterial vaginosis. Some women may notice a change in the normal discharge from the vagina. This discharge will usually be white or grey, thin or watery, and have a strong, unpleasant fishy smell. This can be more noticeable during and after sex, and during periods. Bacterial vaginosis does not usually cause itching or irritation.

patterns: What should I do if I think I have bacterial vaginosis?
How should I treat bacterial vaginosis?
What can I do if I suspect I have bacterial vaginosis?
responses: Check if you could be triggering it with toiletries. Consider over-the-counter treatments from your pharmacy. Contact your GP practice for an appointment or book an appointment at your local sexual health service.

patterns: What is the treatment for bacterial vaginosis?
How is bacterial vaginosis treated?
What medications are used to treat bacterial vaginosis?
responses: Bacterial vaginosis is treated with antibiotics. Antibiotics must be prescribed by a doctor or nurse. You may be given an antibiotic cream or gel to use in your vagina, instead of antibiotic tablets by mouth. While you’re there, make sure you tell the doctor or nurse if you’re pregnant, think you might be pregnant, or are breastfeeding, as these may affect the type of treatment you’re given. You can still have sex while being treated for bacterial vaginosis as it’s not sexually transmitted. However, antibiotics and creams can affect condoms and other forms of contraception. Speak to your doctor or pharmacist for further information. Partners do not usually need treatment. Non-antibiotic treatment is also available to buy in pharmacies.

patterns: What causes bacterial vaginosis?
How does bacterial vaginosis occur?
What are the factors that contribute to bacterial vaginosis?
responses: Bacteria called lactobacilli naturally live in your vagina and stop other bacteria from growing there. Sometimes the balance of these bacteria changes, which can lead to bacterial vaginosis. Bacterial vaginosis can’t be passed on but is more common in sexually active people. Other factors that may increase the risk of getting it include having a new sexual partner, multiple sexual partners, smoking, using scented soaps or perfumed bubble baths, putting antiseptic liquids in the bath, douching (washing or cleaning out the vagina with water or other fluids), using vaginal washes or deodorants, using strong detergents to wash your underwear, and receiving oral sex.

patterns: How can I prevent bacterial vaginosis?
What can I do to prevent bacterial vaginosis?
How do I lower the risk of bacterial vaginosis?
responses: The causes of bacterial vaginosis are not fully understood, so it may not be possible to completely prevent it. However, you may be able to lower your risk of developing it by following our genital washing advice.

patterns: Tell me about benign prostate enlargement
What is benign prostate enlargement?
Explain benign prostate enlargement
responses: Benign prostate enlargement (BPE), also known as benign prostatic hyperplasia (BPH), is a condition that affects older men and anyone with a prostate. It’s particularly common in men and anyone with a prostate over 50 years of age and isn’t usually a serious threat to health.

patterns: Tell me about the prostate gland
What is the prostate gland?
Explain the prostate gland
responses: The prostate is a small gland, located in the pelvis, between the penis and bladder. It’s involved in the production of semen. The prostate produces a thick, white fluid that’s made into a thinner liquid by a protein called prostate-specific antigen (PSA). The liquid is then mixed with sperm, produced by the testicles, to create semen. If the prostate becomes enlarged, it can place pressure on the bladder and urethra (the tube through which urine passes). This can affect how you pass urine and may cause difficulty starting urination, a frequent need to urinate, or difficulty fully emptying the bladder. In some men and anyone with a prostate, the symptoms are mild and don’t require treatment. In others, the symptoms can be very troublesome and have a major impact on a person’s quality of life. Many people worry that having an enlarged prostate means they have an increased risk of developing prostate cancer. This isn’t the case. The risk of prostate cancer is no greater for people with an enlarged prostate than it is for those without an enlarged prostate.

patterns: What causes benign prostate enlargement?
Tell me what causes benign prostate enlargement?
Explain the causes of benign prostate enlargement
responses: The cause of prostate enlargement is unknown, but most experts agree that it’s linked to hormonal changes that occur as a man gets older.

patterns: How is benign prostate enlargement diagnosed?
What tests are used to diagnose benign prostate enlargement?
Explain how benign prostate enlargement is diagnosed
responses: If your GP suspects that you have an enlarged prostate, you’ll be asked to complete a questionnaire to assess your symptoms. Each question has 5 possible answers that carry a score, and your overall score indicates the severity of your symptoms. Your GP will also want to rule out other conditions that cause similar symptoms to prostate enlargement. You may have a number of standard tests, such as urine tests, plus some more specific tests, such as a blood test that measures PSA.

patterns: Tell me about treating benign prostate enlargement
What is treating benign prostate enlargement?
Explain treating benign prostate enlargement
responses: Treatment for an enlarged prostate is determined by the severity of your symptoms. If you have mild to moderate symptoms, you won’t receive any immediate medical treatment, but you’ll have regular check-ups to carefully monitor your prostate. You’ll probably also be advised to make lifestyle changes, such as limiting your caffeine and alcohol intake, and exercising regularly, to see if they improve your symptoms. As well as lifestyle changes, medication is usually recommended to treat moderate to severe symptoms of benign prostate enlargement. Finasteride and dutasteride are medications that are commonly used. They block the effects of a hormone called dihydrotestosterone (DHT) on the prostate gland, which can reduce the size of the prostate and improve associated symptoms. Alpha blockers may also be prescribed. They help to relax your bladder muscles, making it easier to pass urine. Tamsulosin and alfuzosin are two alpha blockers commonly used to treat benign prostate enlargement. Surgery is usually only recommended for moderate to severe symptoms of benign prostate enlargement that have failed to respond to medication.

patterns: Tell me about complications of benign prostate enlargement
What is complications of benign prostate enlargement?
Explain complications of benign prostate enlargement
responses: Benign prostate enlargement can sometimes lead to complications such as a urinary tract infection (UTI) or acute urinary retention. Serious complications are rare.

patterns: Tell me about how common is benign prostate enlargement?
What is how common is benign prostate enlargement?
Explain how common is benign prostate enlargement?
responses: Benign prostate enlargement is a condition associated with ageing and is common in men and anyone with a prostate over 50 years of age.

patterns: Tell me about bile duct cancer
What is bile duct cancer?
Explain bile duct cancer
responses: Bile duct cancer (cholangiocarcinoma) is a rare but aggressive type of cancer. The bile duct system, or ‘biliary’ system, is made up of a series of tubes that begin in the liver and end in the small intestine. Bile is a fluid the digestive system uses to help break down fats and digest foods.

patterns: Tell me about symptoms of bile duct cancer
What are the symptoms of bile duct cancer?
Explain symptoms of bile duct cancer
responses: In most cases, there are no signs of bile duct cancer until it reaches the later stages, when symptoms can include jaundice – yellowing of the skin and the whites of the eyes, itchy skin, pale stools, and dark-colored urine, unintentional weight loss, and abdominal pain. Speak to your GP if you have signs of jaundice or are worried about other symptoms. While it is unlikely you have bile duct cancer, it is best to get it checked.

patterns: Tell me about why bile duct cancer happens
What causes bile duct cancer?
Explain why bile duct cancer happens
responses: The exact cause of bile duct cancer is unknown. However, some things may increase your chances of developing the condition. The most common include being over 65 years old or having a rare chronic liver disease called primary sclerosing cholangitis (PSC).

patterns: Tell me about types of bile duct cancer
What are the types of bile duct cancer?
Explain the types of bile duct cancer
responses: There are 2 main types of bile duct cancer, depending on where the cancer begins: cancer that starts in a part of the bile duct inside the liver is known as intrahepatic bile duct cancer, and cancer that starts in part of the bile duct outside the liver is known as extrahepatic bile duct cancer.

patterns: Tell me about diagnosis
What is diagnosis?
Explain diagnosis
responses: Cancer of the bile duct can be difficult to diagnose, so you may need several tests, including blood tests, ultrasound scans, computerized tomography (CT) scans, magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) scans. For some of these tests, you may need to be injected with a special dye that highlights your bile ducts. You may also need a biopsy, which involves removing a small sample of tissue so it can be studied under a microscope. However, in some cases, your surgeon may prefer to remove the suspected tumour based on the results of your scans alone.

patterns: Tell me about how bile duct cancer is treated
What is how bile duct cancer is treated?
Explain how bile duct cancer is treated
responses: Cancer of the bile duct can usually only be cured if cancerous cells haven’t spread. If this is the case, some or all of the bile duct may be removed. Only a small proportion of bile duct cancer cases are diagnosed early enough to be suitable for surgery. This is because symptoms usually develop at a late stage. Despite this, treatment such as chemotherapy can relieve the symptoms of bile duct cancer and improve the quality of life of people in the advanced stages of the condition.

patterns: Tell me about who is affected
What is who is affected?
Explain who is affected
responses: Most cases of bile duct cancer occur in people over the age of 65. The condition affects men and women almost equally.

patterns: Tell me about can bile duct cancer be prevented?
What is can bile duct cancer be prevented?
Explain can bile duct cancer be prevented?
responses: There are no guaranteed ways to avoid getting bile duct cancer, but you can reduce your chances of developing it. The most effective ways of achieving this are reducing your alcohol intake, as cirrhosis is a risk factor, and trying to ensure that you don’t become infected with hepatitis B or hepatitis C.

patterns: Tell me about what is bingeing
What is bingeing?
Explain what is bingeing
responses: Signs that an episode of overeating is a binge include eating much faster than normal, eating until you feel uncomfortably full, regularly eating a large amount of food when you’re not hungry, eating alone or secretly due to being embarrassed or ashamed about the amount of food you’re consuming, and having feelings of guilt, shame, or disgust during or after a binge.

patterns: Tell me about symptoms of BED
What are the symptoms of BED?
Explain symptoms of BED
responses: The main signs of BED are regularly eating a large quantity of food over a short time (bingeing), feeling out of control of your eating and feeling distressed, restricting your eating or putting strict rules around eating, fasting for long periods of time before or after a binge, hoarding food, feeling ashamed and hiding evidence of eating from others – close family and friends may be unaware of the distress and binges, planning the day around food by trying to restrict how much food you eat and then fasting which may lead to more binges, having low self-esteem and feeling shame, being preoccupied about weight and body shape.

patterns: Tell me about treatment for BED
What is treatment for BED?
Explain treatment for BED
responses: There are treatments available for BED. Treatment does not directly focus on weight loss. It focuses on addressing things like relationships, feelings, and thoughts that result in a person having a difficult relationship with food.

patterns: Tell me about long-term physical effects of binge eating
What are the long-term physical effects of binge eating?
Explain long-term physical effects of binge eating
responses: Binge eating often leads to weight gain. Being overweight or obese can put you at risk of a number of physical health problems, some of which can be life-threatening. These include high cholesterol, high blood pressure, diabetes, osteoarthritis, and some types of cancer. If you think you might have a binge eating problem, help is available. You can get support to tackle both the physical and mental effects of binge eating.

patterns: Tell me about getting help for binge eating disorder
What is getting help for binge eating disorder?
Explain getting help for binge eating disorder
responses: If you have eating problems or think you may have an eating disorder, it’s important to seek help as soon as possible.
You could:
talk to someone you trust, like a friend or member of your family
see your GP – you may find it helpful to bring someone with you for support
contact an anonymous helpline like Beat’s helpline

patterns: Tell me about further support
What is further support?
Explain further support
responses: Find information and services that can offer advice and support about eating disorders

patterns: Tell me about symptoms of bipolar disorder
What is symptoms of bipolar disorder?
Explain symptoms of bipolar disorder
responses: Symptoms of bipolar disorder depend on which mood you’re experiencing.

patterns: Tell me about how to get urgent mental health support
What is how to get urgent mental health support?
Explain how to get urgent mental health support
responses: Support is available if you or someone you know is having a mental health crisis or emergency, no matter what you’re going through.
Get urgent mental health support

patterns: Tell me about diagnosing bipolar disorder
What is diagnosing bipolar disorder?
Explain diagnosing bipolar disorder
responses: Bipolar disorder is common and anyone can develop it. You should speak to your GP practice if you’re worried you could have symptoms of bipolar disorder. They may refer you to a psychiatrist (a qualified mental health specialist). If you have an appointment with a psychiatrist, you can take someone you trust with you. The psychiatrist will ask about the type of symptoms you have, when you first experienced symptoms, how you’ve felt recently, your medical background, and your family history. They’ll also ask if you’ve had thoughts about harming yourself. This will help the psychiatrist decide what treatments are best for you.

patterns: Tell me about treatment for bipolar disorder
What is treatment for bipolar disorder?
Explain treatment for bipolar disorder
responses: Most people have a combination of treatments to manage bipolar disorder. This can include medication, talking therapy, and making lifestyle changes like exercising regularly and improving your diet. Treatment aims to reduce the severity and number of bipolar episodes you have.

patterns: Tell me about what causes bipolar disorder?
What is what causes bipolar disorder?
Explain what causes bipolar disorder?
responses: It’s not known exactly what causes bipolar disorder. Many factors may work together to make you more vulnerable to it. This includes chemical imbalances in the brain, genetics – it can run in families, and stressful life events – like the breakdown of a relationship, problems with money, or physical illness.

patterns: Tell me about pregnancy and bipolar disorder
What is pregnancy and bipolar disorder?
Explain pregnancy and bipolar disorder
responses: Speak to your healthcare team if you have bipolar disorder and are trying to get pregnant. They’ll help you stay well before, during, and after the pregnancy. Tommy’s has further information on bipolar disorder and planning a pregnancy and pregnancy.

patterns: Tell me about support with bipolar disorder
What is support with bipolar disorder?
Explain support with bipolar disorder
responses: Some people with bipolar disorder can talk to family and friends. Other people find it easier to turn to charities and support groups. Many organisations have support groups. They can put you in touch with other people with the condition. This’ll help you share ideas and may let you see that you’re not alone. Some organisations also provide online support in forums and blogs.

patterns: Tell me about looking after your health
What is looking after your health?
Explain looking after your health
responses: Looking after your overall health can help you get more control over your recovery from bipolar disorder.

patterns: Tell me about living with or caring for a friend or family member
What is living with or caring for a friend or family member?
Explain living with or caring for a friend or family member
responses: Living with or caring for someone with bipolar disorder can be challenging. The person’s behaviour may change, and relationships can feel the strain. You may find it helpful to talk to a support group. You can also contact a counsellor for specialist support, if needed. Get further information and support from Bipolar Scotland and Scottish Action for Mental Health (SAMH).

patterns: Tell me about bladder cancer
What is bladder cancer?
Explain bladder cancer
responses: Bladder cancer is where a growth of abnormal tissue, known as a tumour, develops in the bladder lining. In some cases, the tumour spreads into the surrounding muscles. The most common symptom of bladder cancer is blood in your urine, which is usually painless. If you notice blood in your urine, even if it comes and goes, you should speak to your GP, so the cause can be investigated.

patterns: Tell me about types of bladder cancer
What are the types of bladder cancer?
Explain types of bladder cancer
responses: Once diagnosed, bladder cancer can be classified by how far it has spread. If the cancerous cells are contained inside the lining of the bladder, doctors describe it as non-muscle-invasive bladder cancer. This is the most common type of bladder cancer, accounting for 7 out of 10 cases. Most people don’t die as a result of this type of bladder cancer. When the cancerous cells spread beyond the lining, into the surrounding muscles of the bladder, it’s referred to as muscle-invasive bladder cancer. This is less common, but has a higher chance of spreading to other parts of the body and can be fatal. If bladder cancer has spread to other parts of the body, it’s known as locally advanced or metastatic bladder cancer.

patterns: Tell me about why does bladder cancer happen?
What is why does bladder cancer happen?
Explain why does bladder cancer happen?
responses: Most cases of bladder cancer appear to be caused by exposure to harmful substances, which lead to abnormal changes in the bladder’s cells over many years. Tobacco smoke is a common cause, and it’s estimated that half of all cases of bladder cancer are caused by smoking. Contact with certain chemicals previously used in manufacturing is also known to cause bladder cancer. However, these substances have since been banned.

patterns: Tell me about treating bladder cancer
What is treating bladder cancer?
Explain treating bladder cancer
responses: In cases of non-muscle-invasive bladder cancer, it’s usually possible to remove the cancerous cells while leaving the rest of the bladder intact. This is done using a surgical technique called transurethral resection of a bladder tumour (TURBT). This is followed by a dose of chemotherapy medication directly into the bladder, to reduce the risk of the cancer returning. In cases with a higher risk of recurrence, a medication known as Bacillus Calmette-Guérin (BCG) may be injected into the bladder to reduce the risk of the cancer returning. Treatment for high-risk non-muscle-invasive bladder cancer, or muscle-invasive bladder cancer may involve surgically removing the bladder in an operation known as a cystectomy. When the bladder is removed, you’ll need another way of collecting your urine. Possible options include making an opening in the abdomen so urine can be passed into an external bag, or constructing a new bladder out of a section of bowel. This will be done at the same time as a cystectomy. If it’s possible to avoid removing the bladder, or if surgery is not suitable, a course of radiotherapy and chemotherapy may be recommended. Chemotherapy may sometimes be used on its own before surgery or before being combined with radiotherapy. After treatment for all types of bladder cancer, you’ll have regular follow-up tests to check for signs of recurrence.

patterns: Tell me about who is affected?
What is who is affected?
Explain who is affected?
responses: Bladder cancer is more common in older adults, with more than half of all new cases diagnosed in people aged 75 and over. It is also more common in men than in women, possibly because in the past, men were more likely to smoke and work in the manufacturing industry.

patterns: Tell me about what is sepsis?
What is what is sepsis?
Explain what is sepsis?
responses: Sepsis is a serious complication of an infection. Without quick treatment, sepsis can lead to multiple organ failure and death.

patterns: Tell me about what causes a sepsis infection?
What is what causes a sepsis infection?
Explain what causes a sepsis infection?
responses: The immune system usually keeps an infection limited to one place. This is called a localised infection. To do this, the body produces white blood cells that travel to an infection site to destroy the germs causing the infection. This triggers tissue swelling, known as inflammation, which helps to fight the infection and prevent it from spreading. However, an infection can spread to other parts of the body if the immune system is weak or the infection is severe. Widespread inflammation can damage tissue and interfere with blood flow. When blood flow is interrupted, blood pressure can drop dangerously low, preventing oxygen from reaching the organs and tissues.

patterns: Tell me about sources of infection
What is sources of infection?
Explain sources of infection
responses: Sepsis can be triggered by an infection in any part of the body. The most common sites of infection that lead to sepsis are the lungs, urinary tract, tummy (abdomen), and pelvis. However, sometimes the specific infection and source of sepsis can’t be identified.

patterns: Tell me about tests to diagnose sepsis
What is tests to diagnose sepsis?
Explain tests to diagnose sepsis
responses: Sepsis is often diagnosed by testing your temperature, heart rate, breathing rate, and blood. Other tests can help determine the type of infection, where it’s located, and which parts of the body have been affected. These include urine or stool samples, a wound culture (a small sample of tissue, skin, or fluid taken from the affected area for testing), respiratory secretion testing (taking a sample of saliva, phlegm, or mucus), blood pressure tests, and imaging studies such as an X-ray, ultrasound scan, or computerised tomography (CT) scan.

patterns: Tell me about who’s at risk of sepsis?
What is who’s at risk of sepsis?
Explain who’s at risk of sepsis?
responses: Anyone can develop sepsis after an injury or minor infection. However, some people are more vulnerable, including those who are very young, very old, pregnant, have had a urinary catheter fitted, have a long-term health condition like diabetes, have recently had surgery, are genetically prone to infections, are already in hospital with a serious illness, have to stay in hospital for a long time, have wounds or injuries from an accident, have a medical condition that weakens the immune system (like HIV or leukaemia), are receiving medical treatment that weakens the immune system (like chemotherapy or long-term steroids), or are on mechanical ventilation (a machine used to help you breathe).

patterns: Tell me about recovering from sepsis
What is recovering from sepsis?
Explain recovering from sepsis
responses: Some people make a full recovery from sepsis fairly quickly. The amount of time it takes to fully recover depends on the severity of the sepsis, the person’s overall health, how much time was spent in hospital, and whether treatment was needed in an intensive care unit (ICU).

patterns: Tell me about long term effects of sepsis
What is long term effects of sepsis?
Explain long term effects of sepsis
responses: Some people with sepsis will experience long-term physical and psychological problems, which is known as post-sepsis syndrome. Symptoms of post-sepsis syndrome include feeling lethargic or excessively tired, muscle weakness, swollen limbs, joint pain, chest pain, and breathlessness.

patterns: Tell me about about bone cancer
What is about bone cancer?
Explain about bone cancer
responses: Primary bone cancer is a rare type of cancer that begins in the bones. This is different from secondary bone cancer, which occurs when cancer spreads to the bones from another part of the body. These pages refer only to primary bone cancer. For more information about secondary bone cancer, the Macmillan Cancer Support website can provide further details.

patterns: Tell me about signs and symptoms of bone cancer
What is signs and symptoms of bone cancer?
Explain signs and symptoms of bone cancer
responses: Bone cancer can affect any bone, but it most commonly develops in the long bones of the legs or upper arms. The main symptoms include persistent bone pain that worsens over time and continues into the night, swelling and redness (inflammation) over a bone that can make movement difficult if the affected bone is near a joint, a noticeable lump over a bone, and a weak bone that breaks (fractures) more easily than normal. If you or your child are experiencing persistent, severe, or worsening bone pain, it’s important to visit your GP for further investigation, as it’s unlikely to be bone cancer but still requires attention.

patterns: Tell me about types of bone cancer
What is types of bone cancer?
Explain types of bone cancer
responses: Some of the main types of bone cancer are osteosarcoma, which is the most common type and affects mostly children and young adults under 20, Ewing sarcoma, which most commonly affects people between the ages of 10 and 20, and chondrosarcoma, which typically affects adults over 40. Young people can be affected because the rapid growth spurts during puberty may lead to the development of bone tumors. These types of bone cancer affect different types of cells, and the treatment and outlook will depend on the specific type of bone cancer.

patterns: Tell me about what causes bone cancer?
What is what causes bone cancer?
Explain what causes bone cancer?
responses: In most cases, the cause of bone cancer is unknown. However, you're more at risk if you have had previous exposure to radiation during radiotherapy, have a condition called Paget’s disease of the bone (though only a small number of people with Paget’s disease will develop bone cancer), or have a rare genetic condition called Li-Fraumeni syndrome, which involves a faulty gene that normally helps prevent the growth of cancerous cells.

patterns: How is bone cancer treated?
Tell me about the treatment for bone cancer
What are the treatments for bone cancer?
How is bone cancer usually treated?
Explain the treatment options for bone cancer
responses: Treatment for bone cancer depends on the type of bone cancer you have and how far it has spread. Most people have a combination of: surgery to remove the section of cancerous bone – it’s often possible to reconstruct or replace the bone that’s been removed, but amputation is sometimes necessary; chemotherapy – treatment with powerful cancer-killing medication; radiotherapy – where radiation is used to destroy cancerous cells. In some cases of osteosarcoma, a medication called mifamurtide may also be recommended.

patterns: What is the outlook for bone cancer?
Tell me about the prognosis of bone cancer
Explain the outlook for bone cancer
How is the outlook for bone cancer determined?
responses: The outlook for bone cancer depends on factors such as your age, the type of bone cancer you have, how far the cancer has spread (the stage), and how likely it is to spread further (the grade). Generally, bone cancer is much easier to cure in otherwise healthy people whose cancer hasn’t spread.

patterns: How are bowel polyps caused?
Tell me how bowel polyps form
What causes bowel polyps?
Explain the causes of bowel polyps
responses: Bowel polyps are caused by an abnormal production of cells. The lining of the bowel constantly renews itself, and a faulty gene can cause the cells in the bowel lining to grow more quickly. There may be a family tendency towards developing bowel polyps or bowel cancer.

patterns: What are the symptoms of bowel polyps?
Tell me about the symptoms of bowel polyps
How do I know if I have bowel polyps?
Explain the symptoms of bowel polyps
responses: Most people with polyps won’t be aware of them as they produce no symptoms and are often discovered by accident. However, some larger polyps can cause: a small amount of rectal bleeding (blood in your stool), mucus to be produced when you open your bowels, diarrhoea or constipation, abdominal pain.

patterns: How are bowel polyps discovered?
What is the method to discover bowel polyps?
Tell me how bowel polyps are found
Explain how bowel polyps are detected
responses: Bowel polyps are usually found as a result of a bowel investigation for another reason, such as a sigmoidoscopy (examination of the last part of the bowel) or during screening for bowel cancer. If polyps are found, a colonoscopy or CT colonography is needed to view the whole of the large bowel and remove any polyps.

patterns: How are bowel polyps treated?
What treatments are available for bowel polyps?
Tell me about treatment options for bowel polyps
How do doctors treat bowel polyps?
responses: There are several methods for treating polyps, but the most common procedure involves snaring the polyp during a colonoscopy. Snaring is like cutting the polyp off with cheese wire and is painless. Both of the above methods involve passing a flexible instrument called a colonoscope through your bottom and up into your bowel. The colonoscope has a wire with an electric current to either cauterise (burn off) or snare the polyp. In rare cases, polyps may need to be treated by surgically removing part of the bowel. This is usually only done when the polyp has some cell changes or is particularly large. After the polyp or polyps have been removed, they are sent to specialists in a laboratory, who will inform your consultant if: the polyp has been completely removed, there is any risk of it regrowing, there is any cancerous change in the polyp. If there is a cancerous change in the polyp, you may need further treatment (depending on the degree and extent of change). Your specialist will be able to advise you on this.

patterns: What is the outlook for bowel polyps?
Tell me about the outlook for bowel polyps
What is the future outlook for someone with bowel polyps?
Explain the outlook for bowel polyps
responses: Some people will need further colonoscopies because polyps can recur. Polyps can sometimes run in families. This is uncommon, but means you’ll need colonoscopy checks at regular intervals. You might be asked to have repeat examinations at intervals of around 3 to 5 years to catch any further polyps that may develop and potentially turn into bowel cancer.

patterns: How is death confirmed?
What is the process for confirming death?
Tell me how brain death is confirmed
How do doctors confirm brain death?
