responses: If you are well enough to be treated at home, there are certain measures you can take to help relieve your symptoms and prevent spreading the infection to others. These measures include maintaining good hygiene practices and following any other advice given by your healthcare provider.

patterns: Tell me about how you get C. difficile
What is how you get C. difficile?
Explain how you get C. difficile
responses: C. difficile bacteria naturally exist in the digestive systems of about 1 in 30 healthy adults, where they are usually controlled by other bacteria. However, taking antibiotics can disrupt this balance, causing C. difficile to multiply and release toxins that lead to illness. The infection spreads through diarrhoea, and the bacteria form spores that can survive for long periods on surfaces, hands, objects, and clothing. These spores can cause infections in others if they are ingested. A person with a C. difficile infection remains contagious until at least 48 hours after their symptoms have cleared.

patterns: Tell me about how to stop C. difficile spreading
What is how to stop C. difficile spreading?
Explain how to stop C. difficile spreading
responses: C. difficile can be easily spread, but practicing good hygiene can help reduce the risk of both contracting and spreading the infection. Measures to prevent the spread include staying at home until at least 48 hours after your symptoms have cleared, washing your hands regularly with soap and water, especially after using the toilet and before eating, cleaning surfaces with bleach-based cleaners, and washing contaminated clothes separately at high temperatures. When visiting someone in the hospital, follow all visiting guidelines and wash your hands thoroughly, as alcohol hand gels are not effective against C. difficile.

patterns: Tell me about coeliac disease
What is coeliac disease?
Explain coeliac disease
responses: Coeliac disease is a common autoimmune condition where the immune system in a person’s intestine reacts to gluten, leading to chronic inflammation. This can affect the absorption of nutrients and cause a range of symptoms.

patterns: Tell me about what is gluten?
What is gluten?
Explain what gluten is
responses: Gluten is a protein found in wheat, barley, and rye. It is present in many food and drink items, including bread, pasta, cakes, biscuits, crackers, breakfast cereals, pastry, certain sauces, ready meals, most beers, and some fruit squashes.

patterns: Tell me about how many people have coeliac disease?
What is how many people have coeliac disease?
Explain how many people have coeliac disease?
responses: Coeliac disease affects 1 in every 100 people, but only about one third of those are diagnosed. The condition can be diagnosed at any age, and reported cases are twice as high in women compared to men.

patterns: Tell me about symptoms
What are the symptoms?
Explain symptoms of coeliac disease
responses: Coeliac disease can cause a wide range of symptoms, both in the digestive system and throughout the body. Symptoms can develop at any age, though the condition is most commonly diagnosed between the ages of 40 and 60.

patterns: Tell me about the causes of coeliac disease
What causes coeliac disease?
Explain the causes of coeliac disease
responses: The exact cause of coeliac disease is unknown, but it is more common in people with certain conditions and in relatives of individuals who have the disease. It is considered an autoimmune disorder triggered by gluten in genetically predisposed individuals.

patterns: Tell me about diagnosis
What is diagnosis?
Explain diagnosis
responses: Most people with coeliac disease have antibodies in their blood. The first stage in diagnosis is a simple blood test, and if needed, a biopsy may be performed as the second stage. Diagnosis helps confirm the presence of coeliac disease.

patterns: Tell me about treatment
What is treatment?
Explain treatment
responses: Coeliac disease is effectively treated with a gluten-free diet. By adhering to this diet and making necessary lifestyle changes, individuals can manage their condition and significantly improve their quality of life.

patterns: Tell me about complications
What are the complications?
Explain complications
responses: If coeliac disease is not treated, it can lead to complications such as osteoporosis and an increased risk of certain types of cancer later in life. Proper treatment and dietary management are key to preventing these risks.

patterns: Tell me about living well with coeliac disease
What is living well with coeliac disease?
Explain living well with coeliac disease
responses: Living with coeliac disease can be challenging, but with the right support, education, and a gluten-free diet, it is completely manageable. Many individuals live full, healthy lives by following these guidelines.

patterns: Tell me about symptoms of cold sores
What are the symptoms of cold sores?
Explain symptoms of cold sores
responses: You may not experience symptoms when you first become infected with the herpes simplex virus (HSV). However, cold sores may appear later during a recurrent infection. If symptoms do occur, they can be quite severe and often involve painful blisters or sores around the mouth.

patterns: Tell me about when to get medical advice
What is when to get medical advice?
Explain when to get medical advice
responses: You can usually get advice and treatment for cold sores from a pharmacy. If necessary, your pharmacist may recommend that you speak to your GP for further treatment.

patterns: Tell me about what causes cold sores
What causes cold sores?
Explain what causes cold sores
responses: Cold sores are primarily caused by the herpes simplex virus type 1 (HSV-1). The virus is typically contracted during childhood, often through casual contact like being kissed by someone with an active cold sore. Once contracted, the virus remains dormant in the body until triggered by certain factors.

patterns: Tell me about preventing infection
What is preventing infection?
Explain preventing infection
responses: It’s impossible to completely prevent infection with the herpes simplex virus, but you can reduce the spread of cold sores. Cold sores are most contagious when they rupture, but can remain contagious until fully healed. Avoid close contact with others during an outbreak, although there's no need to miss work or school as long as you're not actively contagious.

patterns: Tell me about complications of cold sores
What are complications of cold sores?
Explain complications of cold sores
responses: Cold sores are usually mild but may cause complications in rare cases, particularly in people with weakened immune systems due to illness or treatments like chemotherapy. Dehydration can occur if drinking becomes painful, especially in young children. Other complications include skin infections (often when the virus contacts broken skin), herpetic whitlow (painful blisters on the fingers), and herpetic keratoconjunctivitis (inflammation and sores on the eye area).

patterns: Tell me about what is a coma?
What is what is a coma?
Explain what is a coma?
responses: A coma is a state of unconsciousness where a person shows minimal brain activity and cannot be woken up. They are alive but unresponsive to their environment, including sound or pain. Basic reflexes like coughing or swallowing may be reduced, and some individuals may need assistance breathing. Over time, a person may gradually regain consciousness, though some may progress into a vegetative or minimally conscious state.

patterns: Tell me about caring for and monitoring a person in a coma
What is caring for and monitoring a person in a coma?
Explain caring for and monitoring a person in a coma
responses: Doctors monitor a person’s level of consciousness using the Glasgow Coma Scale, which evaluates eye opening, verbal response, and voluntary movements. A score below eight indicates severe brain injury. In the short term, intensive care supports the person’s vital functions, while long-term care involves providing nutrition, preventing infections, and managing physical therapy. Treatment and monitoring depend on the person’s recovery progress.

patterns: Tell me about what you can do as a visitor
What is what you can do as a visitor?
Explain what you can do as a visitor
responses: While in a coma, people may hear or sense their surroundings. Visitors can offer comfort by speaking normally, holding their hand, or playing their favorite music. It’s important to announce yourself when you arrive and offer reassurance with touch or words, as this may have a positive impact on recovery. Research suggests stimulating senses such as hearing and touch can help support the healing process.

patterns: Tell me about recovering from a coma
What is recovering from a coma?
Explain recovering from a coma
responses: Recovery from a coma can vary. Some people regain consciousness gradually, while others may enter a vegetative or minimally conscious state. Recovery might take years or may not occur at all, depending on the severity of the brain injury. Some individuals make a full recovery, while others may experience long-term disabilities, requiring rehabilitation and ongoing care.

patterns: Tell me about further information and support
What is further information and support?
Explain further information and support
responses: For more support and information regarding comas, you can reach out to organizations like the Brain and Spine Foundation, Headway (the brain injury association), or ICUsteps (the intensive care patient support charity). These resources provide guidance for both healthcare professionals and families caring for people in a coma.

patterns: Tell me about symptoms of a cold
What are the symptoms of a cold?
Explain symptoms of a cold
responses: Common cold symptoms include a sore throat, blocked or runny nose, sneezing, cough, hoarse voice, and a general feeling of being unwell. Less common symptoms can include fever (usually 37°C to 39°C), headache, earache (which may indicate a middle ear infection), muscle aches, loss of taste or smell, and eye irritation.

patterns: Tell me about treatment for a cold
What is treatment for a cold?
Explain treatment for a cold
responses: There is no cure for the common cold, but most people will begin to feel better within one to two weeks. Rest, staying hydrated, and managing symptoms with over-the-counter remedies like pain relievers and decongestants can help ease discomfort during recovery.

patterns: Tell me about how do colds spread?
What is how do colds spread?
Explain how do colds spread?
responses: Colds spread through respiratory droplets when an infected person coughs, sneezes, or talks. You can catch a cold by touching contaminated surfaces and then touching your face, or by direct contact with an infected person. The virus can be passed on even before symptoms appear and while they’re still present.

patterns: Tell me about is it a cold or flu?
What is is it a cold or flu?
Explain is it a cold or flu?
responses: It can sometimes be difficult to distinguish between a cold and the flu, as their symptoms can overlap. Flu symptoms tend to come on quickly, often include a headache, high fever, and sore muscles, and can make you feel too unwell to continue daily activities. Cold symptoms usually develop more gradually, mainly affect the nose and throat, and are mild enough for you to still go about your usual routine.

patterns: Tell me about complications of a common cold
What is complications of a common cold?
Explain complications of a common cold
responses: Colds typically resolve on their own without causing major problems, but in some cases, the infection can spread to the chest, ears, or sinuses, leading to further complications.

patterns: Tell me about symptoms of concussion
What are the symptoms of concussion?
Explain symptoms of concussion
responses: Symptoms of a concussion may not appear immediately and can take up to 72 hours to develop. Symptoms include loss of consciousness, confusion, memory issues, dizziness, nausea, and problems with balance. Individuals may also have headaches, sensitivity to light or sound, or difficulty focusing. Severe symptoms may indicate a more serious head injury.

patterns: Tell me about diagnosing concussion
What is diagnosing concussion?
Explain diagnosing concussion
responses: There’s no single test to definitively diagnose a concussion. If someone has a head injury, a doctor will rule out other severe injuries first. If concussion is suspected, but the person has not been to a hospital, it's generally assumed to be a concussion, and the GRAS recovery plan will be recommended.

patterns: Tell me about recovery from concussion
What is recovery from concussion?
Explain recovery from concussion
responses: During recovery from a concussion, rest is essential, and it's important to avoid alcohol and recreational drugs. Over-the-counter pain relievers like paracetamol can help with headaches. Eating well and getting sufficient rest are also crucial for the recovery process.

patterns: Tell me about complications of concussion
What are the complications of concussion?
Explain complications of concussion
responses: Most people recover well from a concussion, returning to normal life and activities. However, there can be complications, and some individuals may experience persistent symptoms or long-term effects, such as post-concussion syndrome or further brain injuries.

patterns: Tell me about symptoms of conjunctivitis
What are the symptoms of conjunctivitis?
Explain symptoms of conjunctivitis
responses: Conjunctivitis causes symptoms in both eyes, including inflammation, redness, itching, a gritty or burning sensation, excessive watering, and sometimes pus that can stick to the eyelashes.

patterns: Tell me about causes of conjunctivitis
What are the causes of conjunctivitis?
Explain causes of conjunctivitis
responses: Conjunctivitis occurs when the conjunctiva, the thin layer of cells covering the front of the eyes, becomes inflamed. The three most common causes are an infection (infective conjunctivitis), an allergic reaction (allergic conjunctivitis), or irritation from something like a loose eyelash (irritant conjunctivitis).

patterns: Tell me about getting advice from a pharmacist
What is getting advice from a pharmacist?
Explain getting advice from a pharmacist
responses: You can speak to a pharmacist for advice on conjunctivitis. They may recommend eye drops or antihistamines to help alleviate your symptoms.

patterns: Tell me about treatments for conjunctivitis
What are treatments for conjunctivitis?
Explain treatments for conjunctivitis
responses: Treatment for conjunctivitis depends on the underlying cause. If it’s a bacterial infection, antibiotics may be prescribed. However, these won't work for viral infections or allergies. Allergic conjunctivitis can be treated with antihistamines, and avoiding the allergen is also helpful. Irritant conjunctivitis usually improves once the irritant is removed. Some sexually transmitted infections (STIs) can cause conjunctivitis, and this may take longer to heal.

patterns: Tell me about the eye problems self-help guide
What is the eye problems self-help guide?
Explain the eye problems self-help guide
responses: You can use our self-help guide to check your symptoms and determine the next steps for treatment.

patterns: Tell me about symptoms
What are the symptoms?
Explain the symptoms
responses: Symptoms of coronavirus can include a high temperature, fever or chills, feeling generally unwell, a continuous cough, loss or change in your sense of taste or smell, shortness of breath, tiredness, muscle aches, lack of appetite, unusual or longer-lasting headache, sore throat, stuffy or runny nose, diarrhea, and feeling sick. You may still have a cough or feel tired after other symptoms improve, but this doesn’t mean you're still infectious.

patterns: Tell me about stay at home advice
What is stay at home advice?
Explain stay at home advice
responses: If you have symptoms or have tested positive, stay at home and avoid contact with others. You can return to normal activities once your fever is gone (without medication) and you're feeling better, usually after 48 hours. If you have respiratory symptoms, avoid contact with vulnerable individuals and do not visit hospitals or care homes. In older adult care homes, residents who test positive should self-isolate for at least 5 days and until they are fever-free for 48 hours.

patterns: Tell me about children and young people aged 18 and under
What about children and young people aged 18 and under?
Explain children and young people aged 18 and under
responses: Respiratory infections, including coronavirus, are common in children and young people, especially in winter. If a child or young person has mild symptoms like a sore throat or slight cough, they can attend school or childcare. If they test positive or show symptoms, they should stay at home until they're fever-free for 48 hours without medication. Most children recover quickly with rest and fluids, and few become seriously unwell.

patterns: Tell me about financial support
What is financial support?
Explain financial support
responses: The Self-Isolation Support Grant ended on January 5, 2023. For more information about other available financial assistance, visit the Scottish Government’s Cost of Living support page.

patterns: Tell me about costochondritis
What is costochondritis?
Explain costochondritis
responses: Costochondritis is the inflammation of the cartilage that connects your ribs to your breastbone (sternum). Cartilage is a tough yet flexible tissue that acts as a shock absorber for joints. This condition typically improves on its own within a few weeks but may take longer. Though it doesn't lead to permanent issues, flare-ups can occasionally occur.

patterns: Tell me about symptoms of costochondritis
What are the symptoms of costochondritis?
Explain symptoms of costochondritis
responses: The primary symptom of costochondritis is chest pain, usually central and not radiating to other parts of the chest or arms. The pain can be aggravated by certain movements or activities such as wearing a seatbelt, hugging, deep breathing, or coughing. Modifying activities to reduce strain on the chest can help manage the symptoms.

patterns: Tell me about causes of costochondritis
What causes costochondritis?
Explain causes of costochondritis
responses: Costochondritis occurs when the cartilage becomes inflamed, though the exact cause isn't always clear. It may be linked to severe coughing, chest injury, physical strain from exercise or sudden exertion, or infections like respiratory tract infections (RTIs).

patterns: Tell me about diagnosing costochondritis
What is diagnosing costochondritis?
Explain diagnosing costochondritis
responses: Diagnosing costochondritis typically requires an evaluation by a healthcare professional. They will ask about your symptoms and perform a physical examination. In some cases, further tests may be needed to rule out other conditions.

patterns: Tell me about treating costochondritis
What is treating costochondritis?
Explain treating costochondritis
responses: Costochondritis often resolves on its own within a few weeks. It is considered a self-limiting condition. Self-care measures such as rest and pain relievers can help manage symptoms. Avoid activities that strain the chest area, such as heavy lifting or intense exercise. Modifying your daily activities can include taking breaks, adjusting sitting positions, and lifting objects close to your body.

patterns: Tell me about work
What is work?
Explain work
responses: Returning to work will depend on your job and your employer's flexibility. You might be able to discuss a phased return or modified duties. A healthcare professional is not required for you to return to work, but it's important to listen to your body and not push yourself too hard.

patterns: Tell me about hobbies and interests
What are hobbies and interests?
Explain hobbies and interests
responses: Continue engaging in activities you can manage, as your pain allows. Gradually increase your activity level over time. While your symptoms may make things more challenging at first, you’ll be able to resume your usual activities as you recover.

patterns: Tell me about help and support
What is help and support?
Explain help and support
responses: If your symptoms don’t improve after 8 weeks, or worsen, it’s advisable to consult with a healthcare professional. You can also find local musculoskeletal (MSK) services. It's important to take care of your mental wellbeing as well, as it can impact your recovery from any health condition.

patterns: Tell me about symptoms
What are the symptoms?
Explain symptoms
responses: The symptoms of Crohn’s disease vary depending on which part of the digestive system is affected. Common symptoms include recurring diarrhea, abdominal pain and cramping (often worsened after eating), extreme tiredness (fatigue), unintended weight loss, and blood or mucus in stools. There may be periods of remission, followed by flare-ups where symptoms worsen. Less common symptoms include fever, nausea, vomiting, joint pain and swelling, eye inflammation (uveitis), red, swollen areas of skin (often on the legs), mouth ulcers, anal pain and discharge, and slower growth in children.

patterns: Tell me about causes
What causes Crohn’s disease?
Explain the causes of Crohn’s disease
responses: The exact cause of Crohn’s disease is unknown, but it's believed to result from a combination of genetic, immune system, and environmental factors. Genetics play a role, as do immune system issues where the body mistakenly attacks healthy bacteria in the gut. Environmental factors, such as air pollution, previous infections, and smoking, also contribute, with smokers being at a higher risk of developing the disease and experiencing more severe symptoms.

patterns: Tell me about diagnosis
What is the diagnosis process?
Explain the diagnosis of Crohn’s disease
responses: Diagnosis begins with your GP asking about your symptoms, diet, recent travel, medications, and family history of bowel conditions. The GP will examine your general health, checking vital signs like pulse, blood pressure, and temperature, and will likely examine your abdomen. Blood tests may be ordered to check for inflammation or anemia, and a stool sample might be taken to check for blood, mucus, or infection.

patterns: Tell me about treatment
What is treatment for Crohn’s disease?
Explain treatment options for Crohn’s disease
responses: There is no cure for Crohn’s disease, but treatment can help manage symptoms and control inflammation. Treatment is often managed by a range of healthcare professionals. Once symptoms are under control, treatment may focus on maintaining remission. If symptoms return, treatment may need to be restarted. Medications, lifestyle changes, and sometimes surgery may be necessary depending on the severity of the disease.

patterns: Tell me about fertility and pregnancy
What is the impact of Crohn’s disease on fertility and pregnancy?
Explain how Crohn’s disease affects fertility and pregnancy
responses: Crohn’s disease does not typically affect a woman’s ability to get pregnant, but surgery for Crohn’s may impact fertility. Most women with Crohn’s can have a normal pregnancy and a healthy baby. However, if you're pregnant or planning to become pregnant, you should consult with your IBD team, as medications may need to be adjusted. During flare-ups, it may be advised to wait until the disease is under control before attempting pregnancy.

patterns: Tell me about complications
What are the complications of Crohn’s disease?
Explain complications related to Crohn’s disease
responses: Over time, untreated inflammation can lead to complications such as bowel narrowing (stricture) and the formation of fistulas, which are abnormal channels that can connect the bowel to other organs or the skin. These complications may require surgical treatment to resolve.

patterns: Tell me about information about you
What is information about you?
Explain information about you
responses: If you have Crohn’s disease, your clinical team will share your information with the National Congenital Anomaly and Rare Diseases Registration Service (NCARDRS). This helps researchers improve treatments and prevention methods for the condition. You can opt out of the register at any time.

patterns: Tell me about cystic fibrosis
What is cystic fibrosis?
Explain cystic fibrosis
responses: Cystic fibrosis is an inherited condition where thick, sticky mucus builds up in the lungs and digestive system, causing breathing and digestion problems from a young age. Over time, the lungs become more damaged. The condition is identified early through newborn screening tests in the UK, with around 1 in 2,500 babies affected. Treatments can help manage symptoms, but life expectancy is typically reduced.

patterns: Tell me about symptoms of cystic fibrosis
What are symptoms of cystic fibrosis?
Explain symptoms of cystic fibrosis
responses: Symptoms of cystic fibrosis usually start in early childhood, though they may develop soon after birth or not be noticed until adulthood. Common symptoms include recurring chest infections, difficulty gaining weight, frequent wet coughs, diarrhea, and occasional wheezing or shortness of breath. People with cystic fibrosis may also experience diabetes, osteoporosis, and liver problems.

patterns: Tell me about causes of cystic fibrosis
What causes cystic fibrosis?
Explain causes of cystic fibrosis
responses: Cystic fibrosis is caused by inheriting a faulty gene from both parents. The gene affects cells' ability to move salt and water across cell walls, leading to thick mucus buildup in various organs. Parents usually don't have the condition but are carriers of one faulty gene. If both parents are carriers, there’s a 25% chance their child will inherit the condition.

patterns: Tell me about screening and testing for cystic fibrosis
What is screening and testing for cystic fibrosis?
Explain screening and testing for cystic fibrosis
responses: Cystic fibrosis is typically detected through the newborn blood spot test, which involves collecting a drop of blood to check for abnormalities. To confirm a diagnosis, additional tests like a sweat test (to measure salt in sweat) or a genetic test (to detect the faulty gene) may be used. These tests are also available for older children and adults showing symptoms. If there's a family history, genetic testing can help determine if someone is a carrier of the faulty gene.

patterns: Tell me about treatments for cystic fibrosis
What are treatments for cystic fibrosis?
Explain treatments for cystic fibrosis
responses: There is no cure for cystic fibrosis, but treatments are available to manage symptoms, prevent complications, and make life easier. These treatments include antibiotics to prevent chest infections, medicines to thin mucus in the lungs, medications to widen airways and reduce inflammation, devices and techniques to help clear mucus from the lungs, and medications to aid food absorption. A special diet and supplements can help prevent malnutrition. In severe cases, a lung transplant may be needed.

patterns: Tell me about the outlook for cystic fibrosis
What is the outlook for cystic fibrosis?
Explain the outlook for cystic fibrosis
responses: Cystic fibrosis is a progressive condition, which can worsen over time and may eventually be fatal if it leads to serious infections or lung failure. However, treatment advancements have improved life expectancy. Currently, about half of individuals with cystic fibrosis live past the age of 40, and those born with the condition today are likely to live longer.

patterns: Tell me about deafblindness
What is deafblindness?
Explain deafblindness
responses: Deafblindness is a combination of sight and hearing loss that significantly affects communication, access to information, and mobility. It’s also called ‘dual sensory loss’ or ‘multi-sensory impairment’. People with deafblindness usually aren’t completely deaf or blind, but both senses are reduced enough to cause significant difficulties in daily life.

patterns: Tell me about signs of deafblindness
What are signs of deafblindness?
Explain signs of deafblindness
responses: Deafblindness can develop gradually, often in older adults, who may not realize their hearing or vision is deteriorating. Signs include needing to increase the volume on the TV or radio, difficulty following conversations, not hearing sounds like a door knock, asking others to speak more clearly, holding books or newspapers very close, or difficulty moving in unfamiliar places. If someone already has a hearing or vision problem, it's important to watch for signs that the other sense may also be worsening.

patterns: Tell me about seeing your GP
What is seeing your GP?
Explain seeing your GP
responses: If you think your hearing or vision may be getting worse, visit your GP. If you're concerned about someone else, encourage them to see their GP. Early diagnosis is important, as treatments for underlying causes of deafblindness are more effective when started early. It also ensures quicker access to local support services.

patterns: Tell me about the causes of deafblindness
What causes deafblindness?
Explain what causes deafblindness
responses: Deafblindness can be caused by several factors. Some babies are born deafblind, while others lose their sight and hearing later in life. Common causes include age-related hearing loss, genetic conditions like Usher syndrome, infections during pregnancy (e.g., rubella), cerebral palsy, and age-related eye problems like cataracts.

patterns: Tell me about symptoms of DVT
What are symptoms of DVT?
Explain symptoms of DVT
responses: Deep vein thrombosis (DVT) often causes swelling, particularly in the back of the leg, but may also be present in other areas. Additional symptoms include tenderness, throbbing pain in the affected area (especially when standing or walking), a heavy ache, redness or darkened skin around the clot, warm skin, and swollen veins that are painful when touched. In some cases, DVT may occur in the arms or abdomen.

patterns: Tell me about risk factors for dvt
What are risk factors for dvt?
Explain risk factors for dvt
responses: Anyone can develop deep vein thrombosis (DVT), but the risk increases with age. Other risk factors include being inactive for long periods (such as after surgery or long journeys), having a history of DVT or blood clots, a family history of DVT or blood clots, having damaged blood vessels (e.g., from injury or varicose veins), being pregnant or recently giving birth, using contraceptives or hormone replacement therapy (HRT), undergoing chemotherapy or radiotherapy, having cancer, having heart or lung disease, smoking, and being overweight or obese. In some cases, DVT can occur without any obvious cause.

patterns: Tell me about diagnosing dvt
What is diagnosing dvt?
Explain diagnosing dvt
responses: To diagnose deep vein thrombosis (DVT), your doctor will conduct tests, including a blood test called D dimer and possibly an ultrasound scan, which can show whether blood is flowing properly through the veins or if there are any clots. In some cases, you may need an X-ray of the vein, called a venogram, which involves injecting a dye to highlight the clot's location.

patterns: Tell me about treatment for dvt
What is treatment for dvt?
Explain treatment for dvt
responses: There are various treatment options for deep vein thrombosis (DVT). The healthcare team will determine the best approach based on your symptoms and condition.

patterns: Tell me about preventing dvt
What is preventing dvt?
Explain preventing dvt
responses: To reduce the risk of deep vein thrombosis (DVT), making certain lifestyle changes can be helpful.

patterns: Tell me about complications of dvt
What are complications of dvt?
Explain complications of dvt
responses: DVT can be very serious because blood clots can travel to the lungs, leading to a pulmonary embolism, which is life-threatening and requires immediate treatment. Additionally, people who have had DVT may develop long-term symptoms such as pain and swelling, a condition known as post-thrombotic syndrome.

patterns: Tell me about what is degenerative cervical myelopathy
What is degenerative cervical myelopathy?
Explain degenerative cervical myelopathy
responses: Degenerative cervical myelopathy is a condition caused by age-related changes in the bones, ligaments, and discs of the neck. These changes can sometimes cause pressure on the spinal cord, affecting nerve function.

patterns: Tell me about symptoms of degenerative cervical myelopathy
What are the symptoms of degenerative cervical myelopathy?
Explain symptoms of degenerative cervical myelopathy
responses: Degenerative cervical myelopathy often presents with one or more symptoms, including pain or stiffness in the neck, tingling or numbness in the arms or legs (often starting in the fingertips or toes), and changes in manual coordination or dexterity (such as difficulty fastening buttons or tying shoelaces). Other common symptoms are pain, weakness, or heaviness in the arms or legs, imbalance or unsteadiness leading to falls, difficulty walking, and changes in bladder or bowel function, such as increased urgency or incontinence.

patterns: Tell me about when to get professional help
When should I get professional help?
Explain when to seek professional help
responses: Many people experience a combination of neck pain, arm pain, numbness, and weakness, which may not require urgent medical attention. However, if symptoms suddenly worsen or if you notice a gradual deterioration over time, it’s important to contact a healthcare professional for advice. Early recognition and treatment can prevent further complications.

patterns: Tell me about diagnosing degenerative cervical myelopathy
What is the process for diagnosing degenerative cervical myelopathy?
Explain how degenerative cervical myelopathy is diagnosed
responses: Diagnosing degenerative cervical myelopathy typically begins with an assessment by a healthcare professional, who will ask about your symptoms and perform a physical examination to assess strength, movement, and nerve function. Based on the findings, an MRI scan may be recommended. In some cases, further tests may be needed to rule out other causes of the symptoms.

patterns: Tell me about causes of degenerative cervical myelopathy
What are the causes of degenerative cervical myelopathy?
Explain causes of degenerative cervical myelopathy
responses: Degenerative cervical myelopathy occurs when pressure is placed on the spinal cord, affecting nerve function. This pressure can be caused by age-related narrowing, significant changes to the bones, ligaments, or discs in the neck, and general aging of the spine. As we age, narrowing of the spinal space occurs, which can lead to compression of the spinal nerves.

patterns: Tell me about treatment of degenerative cervical myelopathy
What is the treatment for degenerative cervical myelopathy?
Explain treatment of degenerative cervical myelopathy
responses: Treatment for degenerative cervical myelopathy depends on an assessment from your healthcare professional. Some people can manage symptoms through self-care and modifying their activities, while others may require surgery. Your healthcare provider will discuss your options based on your specific condition.

patterns: Tell me about work
What is work?
Explain work
responses: A neck problem may require time off work for recovery. The length of time off depends on the nature of your condition and your job role. As degenerative cervical myelopathy is a long-term condition, it’s advisable to discuss with your employer or occupational health the support you may need to return to or remain at work.

patterns: Tell me about further support
What is further support?
Explain further support
responses: When managing any health condition, it’s important to focus on mental well-being as well, as it can significantly impact your recovery. Seeking emotional support and coping strategies is an essential part of managing the condition.

patterns: Tell me about symptoms of dehydration
What are the symptoms of dehydration?
Explain symptoms of dehydration
responses: Symptoms of dehydration include feeling thirsty, having dark yellow or strong-smelling urine, peeing less often than usual (less than 3 or 4 times a day), feeling dizzy or lightheaded, headache, tiredness, dry mouth, lips, and eyes, sunken eyes, and in babies, a sunken soft spot on the head, fewer wet nappies, dark yellow pee, drowsiness, irritability, fast breathing, and cold blotchy hands and feet.

patterns: Tell me about diagnosing dehydration
What is diagnosing dehydration?
Explain diagnosing dehydration
responses: If your GP suspects dehydration, they may perform a blood or urine test to check the balance of salts in your body.

patterns: Tell me about what causes dehydration?
What causes dehydration?
Explain causes of dehydration
responses: Dehydration is caused by not drinking enough fluids or losing more fluids than you take in, through sweat, vomiting, urination, or diarrhoea. Common causes include illness (like gastroenteritis), excessive sweating (from fever, exercise, or heat), alcohol consumption (which increases urination), and conditions like diabetes that cause frequent urination.

patterns: Tell me about overhydration (hyponatremia)
What is overhydration (hyponatremia)?
Explain overhydration (hyponatremia)
responses: Overhydration (hyponatremia) occurs when you drink too much water in a short period, diluting salt levels in the blood. It can happen with excessive sweating and drinking large amounts of water. Symptoms include nausea, vomiting, headache, and in severe cases, confusion, seizures, and coma. Seek medical advice if you’re concerned about overhydration.

patterns: Tell me about treatment for dehydration
What is treatment for dehydration?
Explain treatment for dehydration
responses: For dehydration, drink plenty of fluids like water, diluted squash, or fruit juice. These are better than tea, coffee, or fizzy drinks. If you’re vomiting, try drinking small amounts of fluid more frequently.

patterns: Tell me about symptoms of delirium
What are the symptoms of delirium?
Explain symptoms of delirium
responses: Symptoms of delirium include confusion, increased sleepiness, agitation, a change in sleeping patterns, suspiciousness, and hallucinations. These symptoms may worsen in the evenings or at night.

patterns: Tell me about causes of delirium
What causes delirium?
Explain causes of delirium
responses: Delirium can be triggered by infections (like UTIs and chest infections), prescription medications, dehydration, constipation, injuries, low blood sugar, alcohol withdrawal, surgery, or severe psychological stress.

patterns: Tell me about diagnosing delirium
What is diagnosing delirium?
Explain diagnosing delirium
responses: Delirium is often noticed by relatives or carers. Healthcare professionals diagnose it by assessing core symptoms using tests like the 4AT, which asks orientation questions to detect changes in mental function.

patterns: Tell me about treatment of delirium
What is treatment for delirium?
Explain treatment of delirium
responses: Treatment for delirium depends on the underlying cause, but generally focuses on addressing triggers, managing symptoms, and providing comfort and safety to the patient.

patterns: Tell me about how to support someone with delirium
What is how to support someone with delirium?
Explain how to support someone with delirium
responses: To support someone with delirium, ensure their hearing aids, glasses, and dentures are within reach, approach them gently and reassure them, use short, simple sentences, and keep familiar objects or people nearby. If safe, help with eating and drinking, and make sure they have a clock or calendar visible.

patterns: Tell me about the long-term impact of having delirium
What is the long-term impact of having delirium?
Explain the long-term impact of having delirium
responses: After delirium, there’s a higher risk of developing dementia. Individuals may also experience post-traumatic stress symptoms, including intrusive memories, anxiety, and depression.

patterns: Tell me about delirium when someone’s dying
What is delirium when someone’s dying?
Explain delirium when someone’s dying
responses: Delirium is common in people nearing the end of life, affecting up to half of individuals. Identifying treatable causes is important, but managing symptoms is the focus of care during this time.

patterns: Tell me about the difference between delirium and dementia
What is the difference between delirium and dementia?
Explain the difference between delirium and dementia
responses: Delirium develops suddenly over hours or days, often improving in a few days, while dementia progresses gradually over months or years and is typically irreversible. Delirium symptoms are more likely to include drowsiness, agitation, paranoia, and hallucinations.

patterns: Tell me about dementia
What is dementia?
Explain dementia
responses: Dementia is an umbrella term for conditions that involve brain damage, impacting memory, thinking, speech, and behavior. It also affects emotions and behaviors.

patterns: Tell me about types of dementia
What are the types of dementia?
Explain types of dementia
responses: There are several types of dementia, including Alzheimer's disease, vascular dementia, and frontotemporal dementia, each with distinct effects on the brain.

patterns: Tell me about worried about memory loss
What is worried about memory loss?
Explain worried about memory loss
responses: Memory loss can occur with age due to stress or fatigue, but it’s important to note that dementia is not a normal part of aging. If you’re concerned about dementia, seek advice.

patterns: Tell me about living with dementia
What is living with dementia?
Explain living with dementia
responses: People can live well with dementia for years. As the condition is progressive, it’s important to focus on what still matters to you and adjust to changing needs.

patterns: Tell me about caring for someone with dementia
What is caring for someone with dementia?
Explain caring for someone with dementia
responses: Dementia can affect all aspects of a person’s life, as well as those around them. But, it’s important to remember that you’re not alone. There are many places to get advice and support.

patterns: Tell me about support with dementia
What is support with dementia?
Explain support with dementia
responses: There are many organisations that can help if you or someone else is affected by dementia. This includes: Alzheimer Scotland, Age Scotland, Alzheimer’s Research UK, Care Information Scotland, Carers Trust. You can get advice on dementia support groups through: Meeting Centres, The Dementia Friendly Communities Network, Age Scotland, Alzheimer Scotland, Carers Scotland forum.

patterns: Tell me about mental health support
What is mental health support?
Explain mental health support
responses: Being diagnosed with dementia can be challenging for your mental health and wellbeing, and can impact those close to you. You can phone the Alzheimer Scotland dementia helpline for support on 0808 808 3000. This helpline is free and is open 24 hours a day. NHS inform has general advice on managing your mental health and wellbeing, including breathing and relaxation exercises, mindfulness for mental wellbeing, and getting help with your mental health. You can also get support through mental health services at NHS 24 and Mind to Mind.

patterns: Tell me about give feedback about this page
What is give feedback about this page?
Explain give feedback about this page
responses: Let us know what you liked, what you didn’t or what you think is missing by emailing nhs.inform@nhs24.scot.nhs.uk. Please don’t include personal information like your name, location or any personal health conditions.

patterns: Tell me about what causes diarrhoea?
What is what causes diarrhoea?
Explain what causes diarrhoea?
responses: There are many different causes of diarrhoea. A bowel infection (gastroenteritis) is a common cause in both adults and children. Gastroenteritis can be caused by: a virus – such as norovirus or rotavirus, bacteria – such as campylobacter and Escherichia (E. coli), which are often picked up from contaminated food, a parasite – such as the parasite that causes giardiasis, which is spread in contaminated water. These infections can sometimes be caught during travelling abroad, particularly to areas with poor standards of public hygiene, which is known as travellers’ diarrhoea. Diarrhoea can also be the result of anxiety, a food allergy, medication, or a long-term condition, such as irritable bowel syndrome (IBS).

patterns: Tell me about what to do if you have diarrhoea
What is what to do if you have diarrhoea?
Explain what to do if you have diarrhoea
responses: Most cases of diarrhoea clear up after a few days without treatment. You should drink plenty of fluids to avoid dehydration. Drink small sips of water often. It’s very important that babies and children do not become dehydrated. Your pharmacist may suggest you use an oral rehydration solution (ORS) if you or your child are particularly at risk of dehydration. You should eat solid foods as soon as you feel able to. If you’re breastfeeding or bottle feeding your baby and they have diarrhoea, you should try to feed them as normal. Stay at home until at least 48 hours after the last episode of diarrhoea to prevent spreading any infection to others. Medications to reduce diarrhoea, such as loperamide, are available. However, these are not usually necessary. Most types of medication should not be given to children.

patterns: Tell me about preventing diarrhoea
What is preventing diarrhoea?
Explain preventing diarrhoea
responses: Diarrhoea is often caused by an infection. You can reduce your risk by making sure you maintain high standards of hygiene.

patterns: Tell me about what happens at your GP appointment
What is what happens at your GP appointment?
Explain what happens at your GP appointment
responses: Your GP will ask about your symptoms and may check if you have vertigo. They’ll also want to know: whether the dizziness started for no reason, or if it followed an illness; whether you have repeated episodes of dizziness and, if so, when you experience these; how long the dizziness lasts. If you’re dizzy when you’re lying down, it’s usually caused by a viral ear infection. This can’t be treated with antibiotics. If you feel dizzy when you’re upright, it’s probably not related to your ears.

patterns: Tell me about treatments for dizziness
What is treatments for dizziness?
Explain treatments for dizziness
responses: Dizziness usually goes away on its own. But there are things you can do to take care of yourself while you’re feeling dizzy.

patterns: Tell me about common causes of dizziness
What is common causes of dizziness?
Explain common causes of dizziness
responses: The most common causes of dizziness are: an inner ear infection that affects your hearing and balance – labyrinthitis, migraine, stress or anxiety, low blood sugar level (hypoglycaemia) – which usually happens in people with diabetes, a sudden fall in blood pressure when you suddenly sit or stand (postural hypotension), dehydration or heat exhaustion, decreased blood flow in the back of the brain, which may be caused by blocked blood vessels (vertebrobasilar insufficiency).

patterns: Tell me about living with Down’s syndrome
What is living with Down’s syndrome?
Explain living with Down’s syndrome
responses: There are increasing opportunities and support in education, work and housing for people with Down’s syndrome. People with Down’s syndrome can live healthy and fulfilling lives as part of their families and communities. Some may: live independently, have jobs, be in relationships, socialise with minimal support. Evidence suggests most families of people with the condition feel satisfied with their lives. Many children with Down’s syndrome attend mainstream school and receive additional support if they need it.

patterns: Tell me about dysphagia
What is dysphagia?
Explain dysphagia
responses: Dysphagia is the medical term for swallowing difficulties. Some people with dysphagia have problems swallowing certain foods or liquids, while others can’t swallow at all. Other signs include coughing or choking when eating or drinking, bringing food back up (sometimes through the nose), a sensation that food is stuck in the throat or chest, and persistent drooling. Over time, dysphagia can cause weight loss and repeated chest infections. It's important to see your GP if you have swallowing difficulties.

patterns: What causes dysphagia?
Explain the causes of dysphagia
responses: Dysphagia is usually caused by conditions like stroke, head injury, dementia, cancer (e.g., mouth or oesophageal cancer), or gastro-oesophageal reflux disease (GORD). It can also occur in children with developmental or learning disabilities. Dysphagia may be due to problems with the mouth or throat (oropharyngeal or ‘high’ dysphagia) or the oesophagus (oesophageal or ‘low’ dysphagia).

patterns: How is dysphagia treated?
Explain how dysphagia is treated
responses: Treatment for dysphagia depends on the underlying cause and the type of dysphagia. After tests, treatment might include speech and language therapy, changing the consistency of food and liquids, tube feeding, or surgery to widen the oesophagus. While many cases improve with treatment, a full cure isn’t always possible.

patterns: What are the complications of dysphagia?
Explain complications of dysphagia
responses: Dysphagia can lead to complications like choking, which may result in fear of eating and drinking, leading to malnutrition and dehydration. Some people develop chest infections, such as aspiration pneumonia. Dysphagia can also reduce the quality of life by making meals and social occasions difficult.

patterns: What are the symptoms of dystonia?
Explain symptoms of dystonia
responses: Symptoms of dystonia include uncontrolled muscle spasms, twisting of body parts (e.g., neck pulled to the side or feet turning inwards), shaking (tremors), and excessive blinking. Symptoms can be continuous or intermittent and may be triggered by voluntary movements.

patterns: What are the types of dystonia?
Explain types of dystonia
responses: There are different types of dystonia, including primary and secondary dystonia, each with different underlying causes.

patterns: What causes dystonia?
Explain causes of dystonia
responses: Dystonia is typically caused by issues in the part of the brain that controls movement. Primary and secondary dystonia may have different causes, such as genetic factors or brain injury.

patterns: How is dystonia diagnosed?
Explain how dystonia is diagnosed
responses: To diagnose dystonia, a GP may refer you to a neurologist. Tests, including blood tests or scans, may be needed to confirm the type of dystonia, whether primary or secondary.

patterns: How is dystonia treated?
Explain how dystonia is treated
responses: Treatment for dystonia involves managing involuntary movements and spasms, and may include medication, physical therapy, or other approaches. It might take some time to find the best treatment for each individual case.

patterns: What is earache self-help guide?
Explain earache self-help guide
responses: Complete the self-help guide to check your symptoms and determine the next steps for treatment.

patterns: When should I get medical advice for earache?
Explain when to seek medical advice for earache
responses: Earache usually improves on its own within a few days. However, speak to your pharmacist for advice, or consult your GP if necessary.

patterns: Is ebola still a risk?
Explain if ebola is still a risk
responses: Ebola is still present in some countries in Africa, but the risk for travelers is minimal. The primary risk remains for those who care for infected people or handle bodily fluids. The risk of an outbreak in the UK is very low.

patterns: What are the symptoms of Ebola?
Explain symptoms of Ebola
responses: Ebola symptoms begin suddenly and can include fever, headache, muscle pain, sore throat, and weakness, followed by vomiting, diarrhea, rash, and sometimes internal bleeding.

patterns: What to do if you become ill after traveling?
Explain what to do if you become ill
responses: Seek medical advice immediately if you become ill after traveling abroad, especially in regions where Ebola is present. Contact your GP and mention your travel history.

patterns: How is Ebola virus spread?
Explain how Ebola virus is spread
responses: Ebola spreads through contact with bodily fluids, including blood, urine, vomit, and feces of an infected person or animal, especially during active infection or after death.

patterns: What is treatment for Ebola?
Explain treatment for Ebola
responses: Currently, there is no licensed treatment for Ebola. Treatment focuses on supporting the body while it fights the virus, including hydration and managing oxygen levels. New vaccines and treatments are under development.

patterns: How can I prevent Ebola?
Explain how to prevent Ebola
responses: To prevent Ebola, follow health guidelines, avoid contact with infected persons, and be cautious in areas with active outbreaks.

patterns: What are the recovery times for a radial head or neck fracture?
Explain recovery times for radial fractures
responses: A radial head or neck fracture usually heals in 3 to 6 weeks. After this time, mild discomfort and sensitivity may persist.

patterns: What can affect recovery from a radial head or neck fracture?
Explain factors affecting recovery
responses: Recovery can be affected by factors such as the severity of the fracture, how well you follow rehabilitation recommendations, and individual health conditions.

patterns: Tell me about what to expect after an elbow fracture
What is what to expect after an elbow fracture?
Explain what to expect after an elbow fracture
responses: After an elbow fracture, your joint will feel stiff and painful at first, but it’s important to keep moving it, even if it hurts. You may be given a collar and cuff or sling for pain relief, but you should reduce its use as your pain improves to prevent your elbow from becoming stiff. The goal is to stop using this support as soon as possible, ideally within a week.

patterns: Tell me about pain after a fracture
What is pain after a fracture?
Explain pain after a fracture
responses: It’s normal to experience discomfort in areas around the fracture, including nearby joints and immobilized areas. Pain may vary day to day and can happen even at rest. Some discomfort may persist even after the fracture heals, and some people feel more pain in the fracture area during colder weather. Your pharmacist can help with managing pain.

patterns: Tell me about swelling
What is swelling?
Explain swelling
responses: Swelling is a natural part of healing and can spread down your arm from the fracture site. The swelling will vary depending on your position, activity, and the time of day. Even after the fracture heals, some swelling may persist for several months.

patterns: Tell me about how to reduce swelling
What is how to reduce swelling?
Explain how to reduce swelling
responses: To reduce swelling, you can raise your arm above your heart when resting, support your elbow with pillows, keep the joints in your hand or arm moving, avoid too much rest or excessive activity, and gently massage the swelling.

patterns: Tell me about bruising
What is bruising?
Explain bruising
responses: Bruising is common after a fracture and may appear far from the fracture site. Initially, it can be purple and will change color as healing progresses.

patterns: Tell me about getting back to normal activities
What is getting back to normal activities?
Explain getting back to normal activities
responses: When returning to normal activities, consider your recovery and avoid overstraining your elbow. It's important to ease back into activities to prevent further injury and support the healing process.

patterns: Tell me about bone scanning
What is bone scanning?
Explain bone scanning
responses: After a fracture, you may receive an invitation for a bone density scan to assess your risk of further fractures. This is especially common if you're over the age of 50 or have other increased fracture risk factors.

patterns: Tell me about rehabilitation plan
What is rehabilitation plan?
Explain rehabilitation plan
responses: A rehabilitation plan will include movements and exercises designed to support each stage of your recovery and improve your function after the injury.

patterns: Tell me about how to increase movement after injury
What is how to increase movement after injury?
Explain how to increase movement after injury
responses: Start exercises as soon as possible after your injury to aid recovery and regain normal function. Be sure to move all parts of your arm, including fingers, wrist, and shoulder. Exercises should be done for 3 to 5 minutes every hour, rather than once a day for longer periods, to avoid stiffness.

patterns: Tell me about help and support
What is help and support?
Explain help and support
responses: If your elbow function hasn’t improved within 6 weeks of following this advice, it’s a good idea to talk to a healthcare professional about your symptoms.

patterns: Tell me about exercises for your elbow
What is exercises for your elbow?
Explain exercises for your elbow
responses: The elbow allows for 4 main movements: turning your hand palm up and palm down, straightening, and bending so that your hand touches your shoulder. You should try exercises that focus on these movements.

patterns: Tell me about what is edwards’ syndrome?
What is what is edwards’ syndrome??
Explain what is edwards’ syndrome?
responses: Edwards’ syndrome occurs when a baby inherits an extra copy of chromosome 18, which can be present in some or all of their cells, leading to health problems. There are 3 different forms of Edwards’ syndrome, and these extra chromosomes can affect the baby in various ways.

patterns: Tell me about why does edwards’ syndrome happen?
What is why does edwards’ syndrome happen??
Explain why does edwards’ syndrome happen?
responses: Edwards' syndrome typically results from a random change in the egg or sperm of healthy parents. It’s not caused by anything the parents did before or during pregnancy.

patterns: Tell me about effects of edwards’ syndrome on babies
What is effects of edwards’ syndrome on babies?
Explain effects of edwards’ syndrome on babies
responses: Babies with Edwards' syndrome are likely to have associated health conditions, which may range from mild to severe. The impact on the baby will depend on the form of Edwards' syndrome they have.

patterns: Tell me about living with edwards’ syndrome
What is living with edwards’ syndrome?
Explain living with edwards’ syndrome
responses: Each child with Edwards’ syndrome is unique, and the condition will affect them differently. Babies with full-form Edwards' syndrome are considered 'medically fragile' and often have difficulty feeding, breathing, and are at risk of infections. Despite these challenges, many children show progress in development, communicate their needs, and some are able to sit, stand, and attend school. Children with Edwards' syndrome can have a good quality of life and are valued members of their families.

patterns: Tell me about congenital and rare condition register
What is congenital and rare condition register?
Explain congenital and rare condition register
responses: Babies with Edwards’ syndrome are included in the secure Congenital and Rare Condition (CARDRISS) register, which is maintained by Public Health Scotland. The register tracks the number of babies with the condition and supports health, care, and public service planning. You can read more about CARDRISS.

patterns: Tell me about further information and support
What is further information and support?
Explain further information and support
responses: You can find out more information about Edwards’ syndrome, including stories from parents of a child with the syndrome, at SOFT UK (Support Organisation for Trisomy 13/18).

patterns: Tell me about symptoms of endometriosis
What is symptoms of endometriosis?
Explain symptoms of endometriosis
responses: Symptoms can vary from person to person and month to month. Some people may not have any symptoms at all.

patterns: Tell me about what causes endometriosis?
What is what causes endometriosis??
Explain what causes endometriosis?
responses: The exact cause of endometriosis is not known. It is sometimes believed to be genetic, meaning if someone in your family has it, you may be more likely to develop it as well.

patterns: Tell me about diagnosing endometriosis
What is diagnosing endometriosis?
Explain diagnosing endometriosis
responses: Diagnosing endometriosis can be difficult because symptoms can vary widely, and many other conditions cause similar symptoms.
If you suspect you have endometriosis, talk to your doctor. Share as much information as you can during your first visit to help speed up the diagnosis.
It may help to track your period dates and symptoms using a calendar, diary, or app. You can share this information with your doctor to help them understand your experience.

Make sure to let your doctor know about: 
- Pain during your period or at other times of the month and where it occurs 
- Pain or bleeding when you urinate or have a bowel movement, especially during your period 
- The frequency, duration, and pattern of your periods 
- The heaviness of your period flow 
- Whether you have had a recent smear test (if over 25) 
- Any treatments you've already tried for your symptoms
The doctor will likely perform a pelvic examination, using a speculum and possibly an internal exam to check for tenderness. A pelvic ultrasound may also be recommended.
If symptoms persist, you may be referred to a gynaecologist for further evaluation.

patterns: Tell me about treatment for endometriosis
What is treatment for endometriosis?
Explain treatment for endometriosis
responses: There is no cure for endometriosis, but treatments are available to help manage and control symptoms.

patterns: Tell me about adhesions and ovarian cysts
What are adhesions and ovarian cysts?
Explain adhesions and ovarian cysts
responses:
Endometriosis can cause: 
- Adhesions, which are scar tissue that can cause organs to stick together 
- Ovarian cysts, which are fluid-filled sacs in the ovaries that can sometimes grow large and cause pain
Both adhesions and ovarian cysts can be treated with surgery, though they may return if the endometriosis itself recurs.

patterns: Tell me about endometriosis and fertility
What is endometriosis and fertility?
Explain endometriosis and fertility
responses: Many people with endometriosis can get pregnant naturally, though some may find it more difficult.
If you're struggling with fertility, your doctor may suggest additional support or treatments to help with conception.

patterns: Tell me about support groups
What are support groups?
Explain support groups
responses: Sometimes discussing your experiences with others can be helpful. There may be support groups in your area where you can hear stories from other women with endometriosis and share your own experiences.

patterns: Tell me about symptoms of epilepsy
What are symptoms of epilepsy?
Explain symptoms of epilepsy
responses: The main symptom of epilepsy is repeated seizures. Seizures vary depending on the part of the brain they affect.

The brain’s neurons conduct electrical signals and communicate using chemical messengers. During a seizure, there are abnormal bursts of electrical activity in the brain, which can cause unusual body movements or loss of consciousness.
Seizures can range in severity. Some people may experience just an odd feeling with no loss of awareness, while others may lose consciousness and have convulsions.

patterns: Tell me about what to do if someone has a seizure
What should I do if someone has a seizure?
Explain what to do if someone has a seizure
responses:
If you see someone having a seizure, you can help by following these steps: 
- Protect them from injury by removing any nearby hazards 
- Cushion their head with your hands or soft material 
- Do not try to restrain them or move them, unless they are in immediate danger 
- Never put anything in their mouth 
- Stay calm and stay with them until they regain consciousness 
- Once the convulsions stop, place them in the recovery position and stay with them until they recover.

patterns: Tell me about causes of epilepsy
What are the causes of epilepsy?
Explain causes of epilepsy
responses: In some cases of epilepsy, a cause cannot be found. If there’s an identifiable cause, it usually involves the brain being affected by a condition. The brain is a delicate mix of nerve cells, electrical impulses, and chemicals, known as neurotransmitters. Any damage can disrupt the workings of the brain and cause seizures. The categories of epilepsy can be separated by their causes, which might include:

patterns: Tell me about diagnosing epilepsy
What is diagnosing epilepsy?
Explain diagnosing epilepsy
responses: Epilepsy is usually difficult to diagnose quickly. In most cases, it cannot be confirmed until you have had more than one seizure. It can be difficult to diagnose because many other conditions, like fainting, migraines, and panic attacks, can cause similar symptoms. If you’ve had a seizure, you’ll be referred to a specialist in epilepsy, usually a neurologist (a doctor who specializes in conditions affecting the brain and nervous system).

patterns: Tell me about treating epilepsy
What is treating epilepsy?
Explain treating epilepsy
responses: Most people with epilepsy can be successfully treated with anti-seizure medications (ASMs). ASMs do not cure epilepsy, but they can prevent seizures from occurring. There are many different ASMs. They work by changing the levels of the chemicals in your brain that conduct electrical impulses, reducing the chance of a seizure. The type of ASM recommended for you will depend on factors such as: the type of seizures you have, your age, whether there are any concerns about certain ASMs interacting with other medications (like the contraceptive pill), and whether you’re thinking of having a baby. Examples of commonly used ASMs include sodium valproate, carbamazepine, lamotrigine, and levetiracetam.

patterns: Tell me about living with epilepsy
What is living with epilepsy?
Explain living with epilepsy
responses: Regular exercise and a healthy diet are recommended for everyone. They can help prevent many conditions, including heart disease and various forms of cancer. Try to eat a balanced diet, containing all the food groups, to give your body the nutrition it needs. Exercising regularly can also increase the strength of your bones, relieve stress, and reduce fatigue.

patterns: Tell me about sudden unexpected death in epilepsy (SUDEP)
What is sudden unexpected death in epilepsy (SUDEP)?
Explain sudden unexpected death in epilepsy (SUDEP)
responses: When someone with epilepsy dies and there’s no clear cause, it’s known as sudden unexpected death in epilepsy (SUDEP). The risk of SUDEP for someone with epilepsy is low. The exact causes of SUDEP are unknown, and it’s not possible to predict who it will affect. One theory is that seizures could affect the person’s breathing and heartbeat. Things that may increase the risk of SUDEP include: having seizures that cause loss of consciousness and the body to go stiff and jerk (tonic-clonic seizures), poorly controlled epilepsy, such as not using ASMs prescribed to control seizures, having sudden and frequent changes to ASMs, being a young adult (especially male), having sleep seizures, having seizures when alone, and drinking large amounts of alcohol. If you’re worried that your epilepsy is poorly controlled, contact your epilepsy specialist. It may be possible to refer you to a specialist epilepsy center for further treatment. A charity called SUDEP Action can offer advice and support on SUDEP. They have a helpline for people who have lost a loved one as a result of epilepsy.

patterns: Tell me about erectile dysfunction
What is erectile dysfunction?
Explain erectile dysfunction
responses: Erectile dysfunction (ED), also known as impotence, is the inability to get and maintain an erection. It is a very common condition, particularly in older men. It is estimated that half of all men between the ages of 40 and 70 will experience it to some degree.

patterns: When should I see my GP about erectile dysfunction?
Tell me when to see a GP about erectile dysfunction
When is it time to see your GP about erectile dysfunction?
responses: See your GP if you have erectile dysfunction for more than a few weeks. They will assess your general state of health because the condition can be a sign of more serious health issues, such as heart disease (when the heart’s blood supply is blocked or interrupted).

patterns: Why does erectile dysfunction happen?
Tell me why erectile dysfunction occurs
What causes erectile dysfunction?
responses: Erectile dysfunction can have both physical and psychological causes. Physical causes include narrowing of the blood vessels going to the penis, often associated with high blood pressure, high cholesterol, or diabetes; hormonal problems; and surgery or injury. Psychological causes include anxiety, depression, and relationship problems. Sometimes, erectile dysfunction occurs only in certain situations. For example, you may be able to get an erection during masturbation, or you may find that you sometimes wake up with an erection but are unable to get an erection with your sexual partner. If this happens, it’s likely that the underlying cause is psychological (stress-related). If you are unable to get an erection under any circumstances, it’s likely that the cause is physical. Erectile dysfunction can also be a side effect of certain medications.

patterns: How is erectile dysfunction diagnosed?
Tell me about diagnosing erectile dysfunction
What is the diagnosis for erectile dysfunction?
responses: Although you may feel embarrassed, it’s important to get a diagnosis so the cause can be identified. Your GP will usually diagnose erectile dysfunction. This will involve answering questions about your symptoms, a physical examination, and some simple tests.

patterns: How is erectile dysfunction treated?
Tell me how erectile dysfunction is treated
What treatments are available for erectile dysfunction?
responses: Erectile dysfunction is primarily treated by addressing the underlying cause, whether physical or psychological. For instance, the narrowing of the arteries (atherosclerosis) is a common cause of ED. In such cases, your GP may suggest lifestyle changes, such as losing weight, to reduce your risk of cardiovascular disease. This could relieve your symptoms and improve your overall health. You may also be prescribed medications like cholesterol-lowering statins or drugs to reduce your blood pressure. Various treatments for erectile dysfunction have been successful. Medications like sildenafil (Viagra) can help manage the condition in at least two-thirds of cases. Vacuum pumps that encourage blood flow to the penis are also effective in about 90% of cases. Psychological treatments, such as cognitive behavioral therapy (CBT) and sex therapy, can also be beneficial. Overall, treatments for erectile dysfunction have improved significantly in recent years. Most men are eventually able to regain sexual function.

patterns: What are the symptoms of E. coli O157 infection?
Tell me about symptoms of E. coli O157
Explain symptoms of E. coli O157 infection
responses: Symptoms include diarrhoea, stomach cramps, and occasionally fever. About half of people with the infection will experience bloody diarrhoea. People usually notice symptoms 3 to 4 days after infection, though symptoms can start anytime between 1 and 14 days later. These symptoms can last up to 2 weeks.

patterns: How is E. coli O157 treated at home?
Tell me about caring for E. coli O157 at home
What should I do to treat E. coli O157 infection at home?
responses: There is no specific treatment for E. coli O157 infection. People usually recover at home without medical treatment. It’s important to drink plenty of fluids to avoid dehydration due to diarrhoea. Antibiotics are not recommended as they may increase the risk of complications. Anti-diarrhoea medications like loperamide (Imodium) should be avoided as they can prolong exposure to the toxin.

patterns: How can I prevent E. coli O157 from spreading at home?
Tell me how to stop E. coli O157 from spreading at home
How do I prevent the spread of E. coli O157 infection?
responses: Strict hygiene measures are essential to prevent the infection from spreading.

patterns: Tell me about returning to work or school
What is returning to work or school?
Explain returning to work or school
responses: Anyone who has had an E. coli O157 infection should stay away from work or school until they have been completely free of symptoms for 48 hours. Most people are no longer infectious after about a week, although some people, particularly children, may carry E. coli O157 for several months after they have got better. Some people need to take special care before returning to work or school. If you work in health or social care, or your work involves handling food, you should ask your local authority environmental health officers about when it is safe to return to work. This advice applies to both people who have been infected and those who live in the same household as someone who has. If you have a child under 5 years of age who has had E. coli, or lives with someone who has, you should talk to your GP about when it is safe for your child to return to school or nursery. Children under 5 years of age who have had an E. coli O157 infection should not swim in public swimming pools, or share paddling pools with others, until they have had test results showing that they are no longer an infection risk to others.

patterns: Tell me about sarcomas
What is sarcomas?
Explain sarcomas
responses: A sarcoma is a type of cancer that develops in the body’s supporting tissues. Bone sarcomas and soft tissue sarcomas are the 2 main types. Ewing sarcoma usually occurs in bone. It most commonly develops in the pelvis bone (hips), thigh bone (femur), or shin bone (tibia). However, the condition can also develop in the soft tissues surrounding the bone or joint. This type of Ewing sarcoma is known as extraosseous, which means outside the bone.

patterns: Tell me about symptoms of ewing sarcoma
What is symptoms of ewing sarcoma?
Explain symptoms of ewing sarcoma
responses: The symptoms of Ewing sarcoma will depend on the size of the cancer and where it is in the body. The main symptoms are pain in the affected area that gets progressively worse, and swelling and tenderness. Other possible, but less common, symptoms may include a high temperature (fever), severe tiredness, and weight loss. In some cases of Ewing sarcoma, there may not be any symptoms at all. However, as the tumour grows, it could weaken the affected bone, increasing the risk of fracture.

patterns: Tell me about what causes ewing sarcoma?
What is what causes ewing sarcoma?
Explain what causes ewing sarcoma?
responses: Like other types of bone cancer, the exact cause of Ewing sarcoma is unknown. As the condition tends to predominantly affect older children and teenagers, one theory is that it may be related to fast-growing bones. The rapid growth spurt that happens during puberty may, in some way, make bone tissue more vulnerable to cancer. Research has also found babies born with an umbilical hernia are 3 times more likely to develop Ewing sarcoma. However, the increased risk is still small, as only 1 in 110,000 children with an umbilical hernia will go on to develop the condition. The Cancer Research UK website has more information about the risks and causes of bone cancer.

patterns: Tell me about diagnosing ewing sarcoma
What is diagnosing ewing sarcoma?
Explain diagnosing ewing sarcoma
responses: When you visit your GP because of bone pain, they’ll ask you about your symptoms and examine the affected area. Your GP may refer you for an X-ray to check for anything abnormal. If abnormalities are found, you’ll be referred to an orthopaedic surgeon (a bone specialist). They may recommend that you have a magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) scan to examine the area in closer detail. A bone biopsy may be carried out to confirm whether cancer is present. A fine needle is used to remove a sample of bone marrow from the affected area so it can be examined under a microscope. It can either be carried out using a general anaesthetic or a local anaesthetic. The Cancer Research UK website has more information about tests for bone cancer and the stages of bone cancer.

patterns: Tell me about treating ewing sarcoma
What is treating ewing sarcoma?
Explain treating ewing sarcoma
responses: Like all types of cancer, the earlier Ewing sarcoma is diagnosed, the easier it is to treat. Ewing sarcoma is usually treated with a combination of chemotherapy, surgery and radiotherapy. Chemotherapy may be used before surgery to shrink the tumour and make it easier to remove. Ewing sarcoma also responds well to radiotherapy. It can be used to shrink the tumour after chemotherapy, and before or after surgery to lower the risk of the cancer returning. In some cases, surgically removing Ewing’s sarcoma can be difficult – for example, if it develops in the pelvis. In this case, radiotherapy may be used as the main treatment.

patterns: Tell me about living with bone cancer
What is living with bone cancer?
Explain living with bone cancer
responses: If you’re diagnosed with bone cancer, it can be upsetting and frightening. Knowing as much about the type of cancer you have and the best treatment options will help you make decisions. The majority of people who have surgery for bone cancer have a limb-sparing procedure. However, for some people, having their limb amputated may be the best option. This can be a very difficult situation to cope with and you may experience emotions such as grief and bereavement. It’s important to seek help and talk about how you’re feeling. You could talk to your partner, family, friends or the healthcare professionals in charge of your care. Talking to others will help you come to terms with your condition and treatment. There are also a number of practical issues you’ll need to deal with, such as financial matters and coping with day-to-day activities such as school, college or work. The Cancer Research UK website has more information on coping with bone cancer.

patterns: Tell me about information about you
What is information about you?
Explain information about you
responses: If you or your child has Ewing sarcoma, your clinical team will pass information about you/your child on to the National Congenital Anomaly and Rare Diseases Registration Service (NCARDRS). This helps scientists look for better ways to prevent and treat this condition. You can opt out of the register at any time. Find out more about the register.
