Some people, particularly older adults and young children, may also develop a rash on their head, neck, palms, and soles of the feet.

patterns: Tell me about get help from a pharmacist
What is get help from a pharmacist?
Explain get help from a pharmacist
responses: Scabies is not usually a serious condition, but it does need to be treated. A pharmacist will recommend a cream or lotion that you apply over your whole body, and it’s important to read the instructions carefully. Let the pharmacist know if you’re breastfeeding or pregnant. You’ll need to repeat the treatment one week later. Everyone in your home needs to be treated at the same time, even if they do not have symptoms. Do not use pharmacy treatments on children under 2 years old; they will need to see a GP. Anyone you've had sexual contact with in the past 8 weeks should also be treated.

patterns: Tell me about get help from a sexual health clinic
What is get help from a sexual health clinic?
Explain get help from a sexual health clinic
responses: You should contact your local sexual health clinic if you think you have genital scabies or if your partner has been diagnosed with genital scabies. When you get to the clinic, you’ll be asked for some personal details and your sexual history. You’ll then receive an examination and, if necessary, treatment. You should avoid having sex and close bodily contact until you and your partner have completed a full course of treatment. You may also be offered tests for STIs.

patterns: Tell me about treatment for scabies
What is treatment for scabies?
Explain treatment for scabies
responses: The two most common treatments for scabies are permethrin cream and malathion lotion, both of which contain insecticides that kill the scabies mite. Permethrin cream is usually recommended as the first treatment, while malathion lotion is used if permethrin isn’t effective. To help reduce the itchiness, you may also be given steroid cream, menthol cream or gel, or antihistamines. You may experience itchiness for a couple of weeks after treatment, as your immune system continues to react to the dead mites and their droppings. Contact your GP if the itchiness persists 6 weeks after completing your treatment. You can return to school or work after the first treatment has been completed.

patterns: Tell me about how is scabies passed on
What is how is scabies passed on?
Explain how is scabies passed on
responses: The two most common treatments for scabies are permethrin cream and malathion lotion, both of which contain insecticides that kill the scabies mite. Permethrin cream is usually recommended as the first treatment, while malathion lotion is used if permethrin isn’t effective. To help reduce the itchiness, you may also be given steroid cream, menthol cream or gel, or antihistamines. You may experience itchiness for a couple of weeks after treatment, as your immune system continues to react to the dead mites and their droppings. Contact your GP if the itchiness persists 6 weeks after completing your treatment. You can return to school or work after the first treatment has been completed.

patterns: Tell me about complications of scabies
What is complications of scabies?
Explain complications of scabies
responses: Scabies can sometimes lead to a secondary skin infection, where your skin becomes irritated and inflamed due to excessive itching. Crusted scabies is a rare but more severe form of scabies, where a large number of mites are present in the skin. This can develop in older people and those with a weakened immune system and is highly infectious. Unlike normal scabies, a crusted scabies rash doesn’t usually itch. Instead, the increase in the number of mites causes thick, warty crusts to form on the skin.

patterns: Tell me about scarlet fever symptoms
What is scarlet fever symptoms?
Explain scarlet fever symptoms
responses: Scarlet fever usually follows a sore throat or a skin infection, such as impetigo, caused by particular strains of Streptococcus bacteria. The symptoms of scarlet fever typically develop 2 to 5 days after infection, though the incubation period (the time between exposure to the infection and the appearance of symptoms) can range from as short as one day to as long as 7 days.

patterns: Tell me about when to seek medical advice
What is when to seek medical advice?
Explain when to seek medical advice
responses: If you think you or your child may have scarlet fever, see your GP for a proper diagnosis and appropriate treatment. It usually clears up after about a week. Your GP should be able to diagnose scarlet fever by examining the distinctive rash and asking about other symptoms. They may also take a sample of saliva from the back of the throat to be tested in a laboratory to confirm the diagnosis. There’s no evidence to suggest that catching scarlet fever during pregnancy will put your baby at risk. However, if you’re heavily pregnant, be sure to inform your doctors and midwives if you've been in contact with someone who has scarlet fever.

patterns: Tell me about how scarlet fever spreads
What is how scarlet fever spreads?
Explain how scarlet fever spreads
responses: Scarlet fever is very infectious and can be caught by breathing in bacteria from airborne droplets when an infected person coughs or sneezes, touching the skin of someone with a Streptococcal skin infection, such as impetigo, or sharing contaminated towels, baths, clothes, or bed linen. It can also be transmitted from people who carry the bacteria in their throat or on their skin but don’t show any symptoms.

patterns: Tell me about who’s affected by scarlet fever
What is who’s affected by scarlet fever?
Explain who’s affected by scarlet fever
responses: Most cases of scarlet fever occur in children under 10, usually between 2 and 8 years of age. However, people of any age can get the illness. As it's easily spread, scarlet fever is likely to affect anyone in close contact with a person who has a sore throat or skin infection caused by Streptococcus bacteria. Outbreaks can occur in nurseries and schools where children are in close contact with each other. The symptoms of scarlet fever only develop in people who are susceptible to the toxins produced by the Streptococcus bacteria, and most children over 10 years old will have developed immunity to these toxins. While it’s possible to have scarlet fever more than once, this is rare.

patterns: Tell me about treating scarlet fever
What is treating scarlet fever?
Explain treating scarlet fever
responses: Most cases of scarlet fever clear up after about a week without treatment. However, your GP may recommend treatment as it reduces the length of time you're infectious, speeds up recovery, and lowers the risk of complications. With treatment, most people recover in about 4 to 5 days and can return to nursery, school, or work 24 hours after starting antibiotic treatment. Without treatment, you’ll remain infectious for 1 to 2 weeks after symptoms appear.

patterns: Tell me about complications of scarlet fever
What is complications of scarlet fever?
Explain complications of scarlet fever
responses: Most cases of scarlet fever don’t cause complications, particularly if the condition is properly treated. However, there’s a small risk of the infection spreading to other parts of the body and causing more serious infections, like an ear infection, a throat abscess (painful collection of pus), sinusitis (inflammation of the sinuses), or pneumonia (inflammation of the lungs). Very rare complications that can occur at a later stage include bacteraemia (an infection of the bloodstream), septic arthritis, meningitis, necrotising fasciitis (a severe infection involving the death of areas of soft tissue below the skin), and Streptococcal toxic shock syndrome (rapidly progressive symptoms with low blood pressure and multi-organ failure).

patterns: Tell me about preventing scarlet fever from spreading
What is preventing scarlet fever from spreading?
Explain preventing scarlet fever from spreading
responses: There’s currently no vaccine for scarlet fever. If your child has scarlet fever, keep them away from nursery or school for at least 24 hours after starting treatment with antibiotics. Adults with the illness should also stay off work for at least 24 hours after starting treatment. GPs, schools, and nurseries should be aware of the current high levels of scarlet fever and inform local health protection teams if they become aware of cases, particularly if more than one child is affected.

patterns: Tell me about schizophrenia
What is schizophrenia?
Explain schizophrenia
responses: Schizophrenia is a long-term mental health condition that causes a range of different psychological symptoms, including hallucinations (hearing or seeing things that do not exist), delusions (unusual beliefs not based on reality that often contradict the evidence), muddled thoughts based on hallucinations or delusions, and changes in behaviour. Doctors often describe schizophrenia as a psychotic illness, meaning that sometimes a person may not be able to distinguish their own thoughts and ideas from reality. Read more about the symptoms of schizophrenia.

patterns: Tell me about why does schizophrenia happen?
What is why does schizophrenia happen??
Explain why does schizophrenia happen?
responses: The exact cause of schizophrenia is unknown. However, most experts believe the condition is caused by a combination of genetic and environmental factors. It is thought that certain things make you more vulnerable to developing schizophrenia, and specific situations can trigger the condition.

patterns: Tell me about who is affected by schizophrenia?
who is affected by?
Explain who is affected?
responses: Schizophrenia is one of the most common serious mental health conditions, affecting about 1 in 100 people in their lifetime, with many continuing to lead normal lives. It is most often diagnosed between the ages of 15 and 35, and men and women are affected equally. There is no single test for schizophrenia, but it is typically diagnosed after an assessment by a mental health care professional, such as a psychiatrist. Early diagnosis is important because the chances of recovery improve significantly with early treatment.

patterns: Tell me about how is schizophrenia treated?
how is schizophrenia treated??
Explain how is schizophrenia treated?
responses: Schizophrenia is usually treated with a combination of medication and therapy tailored to each individual, typically involving antipsychotic medicines and cognitive behavioural therapy (CBT). People with schizophrenia often receive ongoing support from a community mental health team (CMHT), which provides day-to-day care and treatment. While many people recover from schizophrenia, they may experience periods of relapse when symptoms return. Continued support and treatment can help reduce the impact of the condition on daily life.

patterns: Tell me about living with schizophrenia
What is living with schizophrenia?
Explain living with schizophrenia
responses: If schizophrenia is well managed, it is possible to reduce the chances of severe relapses. This includes recognizing the signs of an acute episode, taking medication as prescribed, and talking to others about the condition. There are many charities and support groups that offer help and advice on living with schizophrenia, and most people find it comforting to talk to others who have a similar experience.

patterns: Tell me about what is sciatica?
what is sciatica??
Explain what is sciatica?
responses: Sciatica is a common condition, also known as nerve root or radicular pain. It describes pain felt in the leg, often originating from the lower back. The sciatic nerve starts in the lower back and extends down the back of the leg into the foot. Symptoms can occur along any part of the nerve, and they may have a sudden or slow onset, ranging from mild to severe.

patterns: Tell me about symptoms of sciatica
What is symptoms of sciatica?
Explain symptoms of sciatica
responses: Sciatica can have a range of symptoms, including lower back pain, leg pain (which often feels worse than back pain), and changes in sensation in the leg or foot. Other symptoms can include muscle spasms in the back and weakness in the leg or foot. Changes in sensation in the leg or foot may include pins and needles, numbness, hot or cold feelings, burning sensations, shooting sensations, or other sensations that can be difficult to describe.

patterns: Tell me about what causes sciatica?
What is the causes sciatica??
Explain what causes sciatica?
responses: Sciatica can be caused by irritation of the nerves that travel from the spine down the legs. This irritation may result from inflammation around the nerves or, in some cases, from pressure, often due to disc-related changes. Most symptoms of sciatica will improve with time, as nerves are resilient. In most cases, inflammation and any disc changes resolve, allowing the nerves to recover.

patterns: Tell me about diagnosing sciatica
What is diagnosing sciatica?
Explain diagnosing sciatica
responses: To diagnose sciatica, you'll need an assessment from a healthcare professional. They will first ask you questions about your back and leg pain to understand your symptoms. After that, they’ll conduct a physical examination, which may involve checking your range of movement and strength in the affected areas. In some cases, additional tests or examinations might be necessary to rule out other potential health conditions that could be causing similar symptoms.

patterns: Tell me about how long does sciatica last?
how long does sciatica last??
Explain how long does sciatica last?
responses: Sciatica typically improves within 12 weeks, although the recovery time can vary significantly from person to person. For some individuals, symptoms may take up to a year to resolve, and they may come and go during this period. It's important to keep in mind that several factors can influence the symptoms, such as poor sleep, stress, and emotional wellbeing.

patterns: Tell me about when to get professional advice
when to get professional advice?
Explain when to get professional advice
responses: Rarely, sciatica can cause a condition called Cauda equina syndrome to develop.

patterns: Tell me about treatment of sciatica
What is treatment of sciatica?
Explain treatment of sciatica
responses: Most people recover from sciatica with a combination of time, modifying their activity, and pacing themselves to allow the nerves to adapt. Pain medication, staying active, and engaging in gentle exercise can help with recovery. Since the nerve can remain sensitive, it's recommended to gradually build up your tolerance to activities. The little and often approach to movement is advised to prevent overstraining the affected area while supporting recovery.

patterns: Tell me about work
What is work?
Explain work
responses: A back problem may require some time off work to aid recovery. The duration of time off depends on the nature of your condition and your job responsibilities. You don’t need to be completely symptom-free before considering returning to work. In fact, continuing to work, or returning as soon as possible, can support your recovery. Gradually resuming your normal daily activities will help build your strength and stamina.

patterns: Tell me about help and support fir sciatica
responses: By following this advice, you should notice gradual improvements over time. The most significant change in your symptoms should occur within the first couple of weeks, and most issues should improve within 6 weeks. If your sciatica hasn’t improved within 6 weeks or if your symptoms worsen at any point, consult a healthcare professional. Additionally, it's important to consider your mental wellbeing, as this can affect your recovery. You can also find information about access to MSK services in your area for further support.

patterns: Tell me about symptoms of seasonal_affective_disorder (sad)
What is symptoms of seasonal_affective_disorder (sad)?
Explain symptoms of  seasonal_affective_disorder (sad)
responses: The symptoms of seasonal affective disorder (SAD) can vary from person to person and differ throughout the year. Typically, the symptoms begin mildly in the autumn and may worsen as winter progresses, particularly as sunlight decreases. However, the severity and nature of these symptoms will differ for each individual. People with SAD may experience depressive symptoms such as low mood, loss of interest in everyday activities, irritability, feelings of despair, guilt, and worthlessness, low self-esteem, indecisiveness, tearfulness, stress or anxiety, and a reduced sex drive. Additionally, you may feel less active than usual, experience excessive tiredness and sleep more (hypersomnia), feel lethargic, have difficulty concentrating, and eat more than normal (hyperphagia). For many, SAD can significantly affect daily life. Some individuals with SAD experience recurring episodes of unipolar depression, while others may experience mild episodes of bipolar depression, where depressive periods are followed by manic periods with feelings of happiness, increased energy, and sociability.

patterns: Tell me about what causes sad?
What is what causes sad??
Explain what causes sad?
responses: The exact cause of seasonal affective disorder (SAD) is not fully understood, but it is believed that the symptoms of depression are linked to reduced exposure to sunlight. One theory suggests that light stimulates the hypothalamus, a part of the brain that controls mood, sleep, and appetite, which influences how we feel. In people with SAD, a lack of sunlight and potential issues with certain chemicals in the brain can cause the hypothalamus to function improperly. This lack of light is thought to impact the production of melatonin, a hormone that makes you sleepy, which may be produced in higher levels in those with SAD. It can also affect the production of serotonin, a hormone that influences mood, appetite, and sleep, where lower levels of serotonin are associated with feelings of depression. Additionally, the reduced sunlight can disrupt the body's internal clock (circadian rhythm), which uses light to regulate important biological functions, such as wakefulness, potentially contributing to SAD.

patterns: Tell me about diagnosing sad
What is diagnosing sad?
Explain diagnosing sad
responses: SAD is often difficult to diagnose because the there are many other types of depression that have similar symptoms. It may take several years before you and your GP realise that your symptoms are forming a regular pattern.

patterns: Tell me about treating sad
What is treating sad?
Explain treating sad
responses: Your GP will recommend the most suitable treatment option for you, and this may involve using a combination of treatments to achieve the best results. Treatment options may include cognitive behavioural therapy (CBT), which is a talking therapy that helps you change how you think and react to situations that make you unhappy; counselling, another form of talking therapy where you talk to a trained counsellor about your worries and problems; and psychodynamic psychotherapy, where you discuss how you feel about yourself, others, and experiences from your past.

patterns: Tell me about who is more at risk of developing septic shock?
What is who is more at risk of developing septic shock??
Explain who is more at risk of developing septic shock?
responses: People with a weakened immune system have an increased risk of developing septic shock. This includes newborn babies, elderly people, pregnant women, individuals with long-term health conditions like diabetes, cirrhosis, or kidney failure, and those with lowered immune systems, such as people with HIV or AIDS or those undergoing chemotherapy.

patterns: Tell me about treating septic shock
What is treating septic shock?
Explain treating septic shock
responses: You’ll usually be admitted to an intensive care unit (ICU) so your body’s functions and organs can be supported while the infection is treated. In some cases, treatment may start in the emergency department. Treatment may include oxygen therapy, fluids given directly through a vein (intravenously), medication to increase your blood flow, antibiotics, and surgery in some cases.

patterns: Tell me about complications of septic shock
What is complications of septic shock?
Explain complications of septic shock
responses: The chances of surviving septic shock will depend on the cause of the infection, the number of organs that have failed, and how soon treatment is started. Complications of septic shock can include respiratory failure, where the lungs are unable to take in enough oxygen, heart failure, where the heart cannot pump enough blood around the body, kidney failure or injury, and abnormal blood clotting. These are serious health conditions that will need to be treated urgently. Septic shock can be fatal due to complications like these.

patterns: Tell me about symptoms for sever head injury
What is symptoms for severe head injury?
Explain symptoms for severe head injury
responses: The symptoms of a severe head injury can vary from person to person.

patterns: Tell me about diagnosing a severe head injury
What is diagnosing a severe head injury?
Explain diagnosing a severe head injury
responses: A person with a severe head injury should be seen in an A&E (accident and emergency) department. The healthcare professionals treating you will prioritize any life-threatening injuries. Once you're in a stable condition, they'll ask some questions, which can help with the diagnosis and treatment of your injury. If someone witnessed your accident, it's helpful for them to come to the hospital with you to describe what happened.

patterns: Tell me about treating a severe head injury
What is treating a severe head injury?
Explain treating a severe head injury
responses: Depending on your specific injury and examination results, you may be: admitted to the hospital for further testing and treatment, allowed to go home after examination, or sent home with advice to attend follow-up appointments at your local neurological center or head injury clinic.

patterns: Tell me about admission to hospital for severe head injury
What is admission to hospital for sever head injury?
Explain admission to hospital for severe head injury
responses: If you’re admitted to hospital, the treatment you receive will depend on your injuries.

patterns: Tell me about complications of severe_head_injury
What is complications of severe_head_injury?
Explain complications of severe_head_injury
responses: Because severe head injuries can cause such damage, serious complications can happen. These can sometimes be permanent. Complications can include: brain damage, memory loss, changes in behavior or personality, loss of motor skills, difficulty speaking, hearing or seeing problems, and problems with coordination or balance.

patterns: Tell me about going home when i have head injury
When i go back home when i have head injury?
responses: When you’ve recovered enough to go home after a severe head injury (tag: severe_head_injury_going_home), you’ll need someone to drive you. Do not drive yourself until you are fully recovered. If you go home less than 24 hours after your injury, it’s important that someone stays with you to keep an eye on your condition. You may also be advised to pause your blood-thinning medication for a short time during your recovery.

patterns: Tell me about follow-up appointments and rehabilitation for  head injury
Explain follow-up appointments and rehabilitation about head injury
responses: After a severe head injury (tag: severe_head_injury_follow-up_appointments_and_rehabilitation), you may need to see your GP the week after you’re discharged to check how you're doing. Additionally, a follow-up appointment at a head injury clinic is often recommended, where a specialist will help with your recovery. Depending on your injury, you may be offered rehabilitation treatments such as physiotherapy for physical issues, occupational therapy for difficulties with daily tasks, speech therapy, or psychological therapy to support your mental health.

patterns: Tell me about getting support for severe head injury
What is getting support for severe head injury?
Explain getting support for severe head injury
responses: After a severe head injury (tag: severe_head_injury_getting_support), it’s helpful to seek further information on rehabilitation and the effects of brain injury. Many organizations can offer support, such as the Brain and Spinal Injury Centre (BASIC), Brain and Spine Foundation, Child Brain Injury Trust, and Headway. These groups provide resources and assistance to help you through your recovery.

patterns: Tell me about symptoms of shingles
What is symptoms of shingles?
Explain symptoms of shingles
responses: The main symptom of shingles is pain, followed by a rash that develops into itchy blisters, which resemble chickenpox. Sometimes, shingles causes symptoms a few days before the painful rash appears, including a headache, burning, tingling, numbness, or itchiness of the skin in the affected area, a general feeling of being unwell, and a high temperature (fever).

patterns: Tell me about causes of shingles
What is causes of shingles?
Explain causes of shingles
responses: When you catch chickenpox, the virus stays in your body and can become active again later if your immune system is weakened, causing shingles. Your immune system can be lowered by factors like stress, other illnesses or conditions, and treatments like chemotherapy. While it's possible to have shingles more than once, it is very rare to get it more than twice.

patterns: Tell me about treating shingles
What is treating shingles?
Explain treating shingles
responses: There’s no cure for shingles, but there are ways to ease your symptoms until the condition improves, which usually takes 2 to 4 weeks. It's important to speak to your GP or pharmacist as soon as you notice symptoms of shingles, as early treatment may help reduce the severity of the condition and prevent complications.

patterns: Tell me about preventing the spread of shingles
What is preventing the spread of shingles?
Explain preventing the spread of shingles
responses: You can't give shingles to other people, but others can catch chickenpox from you if they haven't had it before. If you have shingles, you’re contagious until the last blister has dried and scabbed over. To help prevent the virus from being passed on, it's important to take precautions, such as avoiding close contact with people who have not had chickenpox, especially pregnant women, newborns, and those with weakened immune systems.

patterns: Tell me about complications of shingles
What is complications of shingles?
Explain complications of shingles
responses: Complications can sometimes occur as a result of shingles, especially if your immune system is low or if you're elderly. While shingles is rarely life-threatening, complications can make it serious, with around 1 in every 1,000 cases in adults over the age of 70 being fatal. Possible complications include postherpetic neuralgia (ongoing pain after the rash has healed), eye problems, Ramsay Hunt syndrome, bacterial infections of the rash, white patches or scarring in the area of the rash, and rare complications like inflammation of the lungs (pneumonia), liver (hepatitis), brain (encephalitis), spinal cord (transverse myelitis), or the protective membranes around the brain and spinal cord (meningitis).

patterns: Tell me about what causes sudden shortness of breath?
What is what causes sudden shortness of breath??
Explain what causes sudden shortness of breath?
responses: There are different health conditions that can cause sudden and unexpected breathlessness.

patterns: Tell me about what causes long-term breathlessness?
What is what causes long-term breathlessness??
Explain what causes long-term breathlessness?
responses: Long-term breathlessness is usually caused by:
obesity
being unfit
poorly controlled asthma
chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD)
anaemia
heart failure
a problem with your heart rate or rhythm
More unusual causes of long-term breathlessness are:
bronchiectasis
a blockage in a blood vessel in the lung (pulmonary embolism)
partial collapse of your lung caused by lung cancer
excess fluid next to the lung (pleural effusion)
narrowing of the main heart valve, restricting blood flow to the rest of the body
having panic attacks often

patterns: Tell me about about sickle cell disease
What is about sickle cell disease?
Explain about sickle cell disease
responses: Sickle cell disease is the name for a group of blood disorders. The most severe is sickle cell anaemia. These disorders are inherited, meaning they are passed on through your genes.
You can have a blood test at any time to find out if you carry the gene for sickle cell disease, or if you have sickle cell disease.

Sickle cell disease affects how your body produces red blood cells. Normal red blood cells are round – red blood cells affected by sickle cell disease harden and become sickle-shaped, like a crescent moon. This causes them to die too quickly and block blood vessels, leading to symptoms that are often painful.

People from particular ethnic backgrounds are more likely to have sickle cell disease or carry the gene. You are more likely to have sickle cell disease if you are from one of these ethnic backgrounds:
African
Asian
Caribbean
Eastern Mediterranean
Middle Eastern
There are treatments to manage sickle cell disease, but no cure yet – it’s a lifelong condition.
Find out about symptoms and diagnosis
Find out about treating sickle cell disease
Find out about living with sickle cell disease
Find out about screening to see if you’re a sickle cell carrier

patterns: Tell me about symptoms of sinusitis
What is symptoms of sinusitis?
Explain symptoms of sinusitis
responses: Sinusitis usually occurs after an infection, such as a cold.
Symptoms of sinusitis include:
a green or yellow discharge from your nose
a blocked nose
pain and tenderness around your cheeks, eyes or forehead
a high temperature (fever) of 38°C (100.4°F) or more
toothache
a reduced sense of smell
bad breath (halitosis)
Children with sinusitis may:
be irritable
breathe through their mouth
have difficulty feeding
sound nasal when they speak, as though they have a stuffy cold
The symptoms of sinusitis often clear up within a few weeks (acute sinusitis). Occasionally they can last 3 months or more (chronic sinusitis).

patterns: Tell me about when to get medical advice
What is when to get medical advice?
Explain when to get medical advice
responses: If your symptoms are mild and getting better, you can often treat sinusitis without seeing a GP.

patterns: Tell me about diagnosing sinusitis
What is diagnosing sinusitis?
Explain diagnosing sinusitis
responses: Your GP will usually be able to diagnose sinusitis from your symptoms and by examining the inside of your nose.
If you keep getting sinusitis, or its severe, they may refer you to an ear, nose and throat (ENT) specialist.

patterns: Tell me about treatment for sinusitis
What is treatment for sinusitis?
Explain treatment for sinusitis
responses: Sinusitis usually gets better on its own within 2 or 3 weeks.

patterns: Tell me about what causes sinusitis?
What is what causes sinusitis??
Explain what causes sinusitis?
responses: Sinusitis is usually caused by a cold or flu virus spreading to the sinuses from the upper airways. Only a few cases are caused by bacteria infecting the sinuses.
An infected tooth or fungal infection can also occasionally cause the sinuses to become inflamed.

patterns: Tell me about about sjogren’s syndrome
What is about sjogren’s syndrome?
Explain about sjogren’s syndrome
responses: Sjögren’s (pronounced Show-grin’s) syndrome is an autoimmune disorder. The body’s immune system attacks glands that secrete fluid, such as the tear and saliva glands.The effects of Sjögren’s syndrome can be widespread. Certain glands become inflamed, which reduces the production of tears and saliva, causing the main symptoms of Sjögren’s syndrome, which are dry eyes and dry mouth.In women (who are most commonly affected), the glands that keep the vagina moist can also be affected, leading to vaginal dryness.

patterns: Tell me about what causes sjögren’s syndrome?
What is what causes sjögren’s syndrome??
Explain what causes sjögren’s syndrome?
responses: Sjögren’s syndrome is an autoimmune condition where the immune system mistakenly attacks healthy cells and tissues instead of protecting the body from infections. In this condition, the immune system specifically targets the tear and saliva glands, as well as other secretory glands throughout the body. While the exact cause remains unknown, it is believed to be triggered by a combination of genetic, environmental, and possibly hormonal factors. Some individuals may be more susceptible to the syndrome from birth, and certain events, such as an infection, could trigger immune system issues. Healthcare professionals classify Sjögren’s syndrome as either primary, when it develops on its own, or secondary, when it occurs alongside another autoimmune disorder like lupus or rheumatoid arthritis.

patterns: Tell me about diagnosing sjögren’s syndrome
What is diagnosing sjögren’s syndrome?
Explain diagnosing sjögren’s syndrome
responses: Sjögren’s syndrome can be difficult to diagnose, because it has similar symptoms to other conditions and there is no single test for it.Your doctor will ask about your symptoms and carry out a test to see how dry your mouth and eyes are.

patterns: Tell me about treating sjögren’s syndrome
What is treating sjögren’s syndrome?
Explain treating sjögren’s syndrome
responses: There is no cure for Sjögren’s syndrome, but treatments can help manage the symptoms. Dry eyes and mouth are commonly treated with artificial tears and saliva. Maintaining good eye and mouth hygiene is important, as it reduces the risk of infections and prevents complications like corneal ulcers and tooth decay. In more severe cases, doctors may recommend medication or even surgery to help manage the condition.

patterns: Tell me about complications of sjögren’s syndrome
What is complications of sjögren’s syndrome?
Explain complications of sjögren’s syndrome
responses: Sjögren’s syndrome can sometimes lead to complications, such as permanent damage to your eyesight if the reduced tear production is not treated. It also increases the risk of developing non-Hodgkin lymphoma, a type of cancer affecting the lymph glands, although the chances remain low at around 5%. Women with Sjögren’s syndrome may also have an increased risk of having children with a temporary “lupus” rash or heart abnormalities, and any pregnancy will be closely monitored for potential issues.

patterns: Tell me about who’s affected by sjögren’s syndrome?
What is who’s affected by sjögren’s syndrome??
Explain who’s affected by sjögren’s syndrome?
responses: Sjögren’s syndrome most commonly affects people aged 40-60, with women accounting for about 90% of cases. It's difficult to determine the exact number of people affected by the condition because many individuals do not seek medical attention for their symptoms. For further information on Sjögren’s syndrome, additional resources are available.

patterns: Tell me about information about you
What is information about you?
Explain information about you
responses: If you have Sjögren’s syndrome, your clinical team will pass information about you on to the National Congenital Anomaly and Rare Diseases Registration Service (NCARDRS).This helps scientists look for better ways to prevent and treat this condition. You can opt out of the register at any time.

patterns: Tell me about about melanoma
What is  melanoma?
Explain melanoma
responses: Melanoma is a type of skin cancer that can spread to other organs in the body. The most common sign of melanoma is the appearance of a new mole or a change in an existing mole, which can occur anywhere on the body, though the back, legs, arms, and face are most commonly affected. In most cases, melanomas have an irregular shape, multiple colors, and may be larger than normal moles. They can sometimes be itchy or bleed. An ‘ABCDE checklist’ has been developed to help people differentiate between a normal mole and a melanoma.

patterns: Tell me about nodular melanoma
What is nodular melanoma?
Explain nodular melanoma
responses: Nodular melanoma is a fast-developing type of melanoma, most common in middle-aged people. It may not develop from an existing mole and can appear in areas of skin that aren’t regularly exposed to the sun.

patterns: Tell me about lentigo maligna melanoma
What is lentigo maligna melanoma?
Explain lentigo maligna melanoma
responses: Lentigo maligna melanoma is most common in elderly people and those who have spent a lot of time outdoors. It is common on the face and tends to grow slowly over a number of years.

patterns: Tell me about acral lentiginous melanoma
What is acral lentiginous melanoma?
Explain acral lentiginous melanoma
responses: Acral lentiginous melanoma is a rare type of melanoma that usually appears on the palms of the hands and the soles or big toenails of the feet. This is the most common type of melanoma in people with dark skin.Cancer Research UK has more information about the different types of melanoma.

patterns: Tell me about why does melanoma happen?
how  does melanoma happen??
Explain why does melanoma happen?
responses: Melanoma occurs when some cells in the skin begin to develop abnormally. It is thought that exposure to ultraviolet (UV) light from both natural and artificial sources may play a role in its development. Several factors can increase your chances of developing melanoma, including having a lot of moles or freckles, pale skin that burns easily, red or blonde hair, or a family member who has had melanoma.

patterns: Tell me about diagnosing melanoma
What is diagnosing melanoma?
responses: If you notice any changes to your moles, speak to your GP. They will refer you to a specialist clinic or hospital if they suspect you may have melanoma. In most cases, a suspicious mole will be surgically removed and examined to determine if it is cancerous, a procedure known as a biopsy. Additionally, you may undergo a sentinel node biopsy to check if the melanoma has spread to other areas of your body.

patterns: Tell me about how is melanoma treated?
how is melanoma treated??
Explain how is melanoma treated?
responses: The main treatment for melanoma is surgery, though the specific approach will depend on your individual situation. If melanoma is diagnosed and treated at an early stage, surgery is usually successful. However, if the melanoma is diagnosed at an advanced stage, treatment focuses on slowing the spread of the cancer and alleviating symptoms, often involving medications like chemotherapy. Once you've had melanoma, there's a chance it may return, especially if the cancer was widespread and severe. If the risk of recurrence is significant, your cancer team will likely recommend regular check-ups to monitor your health. Additionally, you will be taught how to examine your skin and lymph nodes to help detect if the melanoma returns.

patterns: Tell me about who is affected by melanoma
who is affected by melanoma?
Explain who is affected
responses: Melanoma is the 5th most common cancer in the UK.More than a quarter of cases are diagnosed in people under 50, which is unusual compared to most other types of cancer. It’s also becoming more common in the UK over time, thought to be caused by increased exposure to UV light from the sun and sunbeds.

patterns: Tell me about can melanoma be prevented?
Explain can melanoma be prevented?
responses: Melanoma is not always preventable, but you can reduce your chances of developing it by limiting your exposure to UV light.You can help protect yourself from sun damage by using sunscreen and dressing sensibly in the sun. Sunbeds and sunlamps should also be avoided.Regularly checking your moles and freckles can help lead to early diagnosis and increase your chances of successful treatment.

patterns: Tell me about light sensitivity symptoms
What is light sensitivity symptoms?
Explain light sensitivity symptoms
responses: Skin light sensitivity occurs when the skin reacts to light exposure, causing symptoms such as a rash, pain, itching, a burning or heat sensation, blistering, fragile skin, and sunburn that happens easily. These symptoms can vary in severity, and some people may also experience general discomfort like headaches or fatigue due to a light sensitivity condition. In rarer cases, individuals may develop unusual freckling or scarring on the skin.

patterns: Tell me about light sensitivity rash
What is light sensitivity rash?
Explain light sensitivity rash
responses: Light sensitivity rashes can occur at any time, not just during summer, and can develop all year round. These rashes can appear even through light clothing, windows, or indoor lighting. Interestingly, areas that naturally have shadows, such as under the chin, the upper eyelids, and behind the ears, may not show a rash. Additionally, the rash may stop at areas covered by clothing or footwear.

patterns: Tell me about diagnosing light sensitivity
What is diagnosing light sensitivity?
Explain diagnosing light sensitivity
responses: If you think you have a light sensitivity condition, you should speak to your GP. If needed, your GP may refer you to a dermatologist for further evaluation. While most people won't need to be referred for light sensitivity testing, if required, your dermatologist may send you to a light testing center, such as NHS Tayside’s Photobiology Unit in Scotland.

patterns: Tell me about treatments for skin light sensitivity
What is treatments for skin light sensitivity?
Explain treatments for skin light sensitivity
responses: A light sensitivity rash can be prevented by using sun protection and avoiding direct sun exposure. Your doctor will guide you on any additional treatments, which may include light-based therapies or immune suppressant treatments. If light sensitivity significantly impacts your daily life, psychological support might be necessary. Additionally, you may need to take a vitamin D supplement if you have a light sensitivity condition. For more information on managing light sensitivity, speak with your healthcare provider.

patterns: Tell me about treatments for a sore throat
What is treatments for a sore throat?
Explain treatments for a sore throat
responses: There are things you can do to help soothe a sore throat.

patterns: Tell me about sore throat self-help guide
What is sore throat self-help guide?
Explain sore throat self-help guide
responses: Complete our self-help guide to check your symptoms and find out what to do next.

patterns: Tell me about causes of a sore throat
What is causes of a sore throat?
Explain causes of a sore throat
responses: The cause of a sore throat isn’t always obvious. But in most cases it’s a symptom of a viral or bacterial infection.

patterns: Tell me about types of spina bidifa
What is types of spina bidifa?
Explain types of spina bidifa
responses: There are 2 types of spina bifida.

patterns: Tell me about symptoms of spina bifida
What is symptoms of spina bifida?
Explain symptoms of spina bifida
responses: Spina bifida can cause a variety of symptoms, including weakness or complete loss of movement in the lower limbs, changes in sensation in the legs and/or torso, and curvature of the spine (scoliosis) due to muscle weakness. It can also lead to urinary issues such as incontinence, retention, and frequent urinary tract infections, as well as kidney problems. Bowel incontinence, constipation, or diarrhea may occur, along with hydrocephalus (excess fluid on the brain), skin problems, and a latex allergy.

patterns: Tell me about causes of spina bifida
What is causes of spina bifida?
Explain causes of spina bifida
responses: It’s not known exactly what causes spina bifida. But, there are some things that can increase the risk of a baby developing the condition.

patterns: Tell me about diagnosing spina bifida
What is diagnosing spina bifida?
Explain diagnosing spina bifida
responses: Spina bifida is often detected during the mid-pregnancy scan, which usually takes place between 18 and 21 weeks of pregnancy. However, in some cases, it may not be diagnosed until after the baby is born.

patterns: Tell me about treating spina bifida
What is treating spina bifida?
Explain treating spina bifida
responses: The treatment of spina bifida will depend on what type you or your child has.

patterns: Tell me about living with spina bifida
What is living with spina bifida?
Explain living with spina bifida
responses: People with spina bifida can live full lives, but they may need help managing their condition. The level of support required will vary depending on how spina bifida affects the individual.

patterns: Tell me about what does the spleen do?
what does the spleen do??
Explain what does the spleen do?
responses: The spleen has a few important functions. It fights invading germs in the blood by containing infection-fighting white blood cells. It also controls the levels of blood cells, including white blood cells, red blood cells, and platelets, which are small cells that form blood clots. The spleen screens the blood and removes any old or damaged red blood cells. If the spleen doesn’t function properly, it may start removing healthy blood cells, which can lead to conditions such as anaemia (due to a reduced number of red blood cells), an increased risk of infection (due to a reduced number of white blood cells), and bleeding or bruising (due to a reduced number of platelets).

patterns: Tell me about splenectomy (having the spleen removed)
What is splenectomy (having the spleen removed)?
Explain splenectomy (having the spleen removed)
responses: An operation to remove the spleen, known as a splenectomy, may be needed if the spleen is damaged, diseased, or enlarged. In some cases, it may be more appropriate to have only part of the spleen removed, which is called a partial splenectomy. If there’s time before the surgery, you’ll be advised to have a series of immunisations to help prevent infections after the procedure.

patterns: Tell me about living without a spleen
What is living without a spleen?
Explain living without a spleen
responses: If your spleen needs to be removed, other organs such as the liver can take over many of the spleen’s functions.This means you will still be able to cope with most infections. However, there is a small risk that a serious infection may develop quickly. This risk will be present for the rest of your life.

patterns: Tell me about how you might be feeling
how you might be feeling?
Explain how you might be feeling
responses: Your emotions will likely be in turmoil, and you might experience a mix of anger, guilt, sadness, loneliness, but also love and pride. There's no right or wrong way to feel in such a situation. If you already have children, it’s important to consider how they might be feeling too and what support they may need. This is a very difficult time for you and everyone close to you, so it's crucial to support each other and seek help if needed.

patterns: Tell me about causes of stillbirth
What is causes of stillbirth?
Explain causes of stillbirth
responses: The stillbirth or death of a newborn baby can occur for many reasons, and doctors don’t yet know all the causes. Some are linked to problems with the placenta or the mother's health, while others are related to the baby's health or development. In some cases, the cause remains unexplained. Although many factors can contribute to stillbirth, you can reduce the risk by maintaining a healthy lifestyle before and during pregnancy. It’s important to read more about living a healthy lifestyle during pregnancy to help ensure the best possible outcomes.

patterns: Tell me about death during pregnancy
What is death during pregnancy?
Explain death during pregnancy
responses: If your baby dies before birth, labor is usually induced. Going through labor and giving birth is the safest option for most women, even though it can be very distressing. It’s natural to want to know why this happened, but sometimes it’s not possible to determine the cause, which can be hard to come to terms with. Your midwife and obstetrician will be there to support you throughout the process. They will explain what to expect and provide you with whatever help you need.

patterns: Tell me about after a stillbirth
What is after a stillbirth?
Explain after a stillbirth
responses: What you choose to do is very personal and individual, as is the way you grieve. Specialist help and support are available for parents and families who have lost a baby, and your midwife, family nurse, or health visitor can help you find the right support locally.

patterns: Tell me about recovering from a stillbirth
What is recovering from a stillbirth?
Explain recovering from a stillbirth
responses: You will need care from your midwife and doctor to make sure your body is recovering. Your midwife will assist you through all the decisions you need to make and help you to find the right support. You don’t have to manage alone, and it can sometimes help to talk with others who have been through baby loss. You, your baby’s father or your partner, and your family are likely to need time to grieve.

patterns: Tell me about abdominal pain self-help guide
What is abdominal pain self-help guide?
Explain abdominal pain self-help guide
responses: Complete our self-help guide to check your symptoms and find out what to do next.

patterns: Tell me about stomach cramps with bloating
What is stomach cramps with bloating?
Explain stomach cramps with bloating
responses: Stomach cramps with bloating are often caused by trapped wind. Your pharmacist can recommend treatments to help, such as Buscopan or Mebeverine.

patterns: Tell me about sudden stomach cramps with diarrhoea
What is sudden stomach cramps with diarrhoea?
Explain sudden stomach cramps with diarrhoea
responses: If your stomach cramps have started recently and you also have diarrhoea, the cause may be a tummy bug (gastroenteritis), which is a viral or bacterial infection of the stomach and bowel. It should get better without treatment after a few days. Gastroenteritis may be caused by coming into close contact with someone who’s infected or eating contaminated food (food poisoning). If you have repeated bouts of stomach cramps and diarrhoea, you may have a long-term condition, such as irritable bowel syndrome (IBS).

patterns: Tell me about sudden severe abdominal pain
What is sudden severe abdominal pain?
Explain sudden severe abdominal pain
responses: Sudden agonising pain in a particular area of your abdomen may be a sign of a serious problem and could rapidly get worse without treatment. If there is any possibility that you could be pregnant, it is important to take a pregnancy test.

patterns: Tell me about long-term or recurring abdominal pain
What is long-term or recurring abdominal pain?
Explain long-term or recurring abdominal pain
responses: See your GP if you or your child have persistent or repeated abdominal pain. The cause is often not serious and can be managed. Possible causes in adults include irritable bowel syndrome (IBS), where the pain is often relieved when you go to the toilet, inflammatory bowel disease (IBD) such as Crohn’s disease or ulcerative colitis, endometriosis, a urinary tract infection (UTI) that keeps returning, constipation, period pain (painful muscle cramps in women linked to the menstrual cycle), stomach ulcers, heartburn and acid reflux, and gastritis (inflammation of the stomach lining). In children, possible causes include constipation, a recurring UTI, heartburn and acid reflux, and abdominal migraines, which are recurrent episodes of abdominal pain with no identifiable cause.

patterns: Tell me about about stomach cancer
What is about stomach cancer?
Explain about stomach cancer
responses: Stomach cancer, or gastric cancer, is a relatively uncommon type of cancer. The initial symptoms are vague and can easily be mistaken for less serious conditions, such as persistent indigestion, heartburn, trapped wind, frequent burping, feeling very full or bloated after meals, and persistent stomach pain. As the cancer advances, additional symptoms may appear, including blood in your stools or black stools, loss of appetite, and weight loss. Since the early symptoms of stomach cancer are similar to those of many other conditions, the cancer is often in an advanced stage by the time it's diagnosed. Therefore, it is important to get any possible symptoms checked by your GP as soon as possible.

patterns: Tell me about who’s affected
What is who’s affected?
Explain who’s affected
responses: The exact cause of stomach cancer is still unclear, but there are several factors that can increase the risk of developing it. You are more likely to develop stomach cancer if you are male, 55 years of age or older, smoke, have a diet low in fiber and high in processed food or red meat, have a diet that contains a lot of salted and pickled foods, or have a stomach infection caused by *Helicobacter pylori* (H. pylori) bacteria.

patterns: Tell me about types of stomach cancer
What is types of stomach cancer?
Explain types of stomach cancer
responses: There are several different types of stomach cancer, with more than 95% of cases developing in the cells of the stomach lining, known as adenocarcinomas. Less common types include lymphoma of the stomach, which develops in the lymphatic tissue (tissue that drains fluid and helps fight infection), and gastrointestinal stromal tumors (GISTs), which develop in the muscle or connective tissue of the stomach wall.

patterns: Tell me about how stomach cancer is treated
What is how stomach cancer is treated?
Explain how stomach cancer is treated
responses: Many cases of stomach cancer can't be completely cured, but it is possible to relieve symptoms and improve quality of life with treatments like chemotherapy, and in some cases, radiotherapy and surgery. If the cancer is operable, surgery can potentially cure the cancer as long as all the cancerous tissue is removed. Surgery to remove part or all of the stomach is called a gastrectomy. While you can still eat normally after a gastrectomy, you may need to adjust the size of your portions. Chemotherapy can also be used before surgery to shrink the tumor and sometimes after surgery to help prevent the cancer from returning.

patterns: Tell me about living with stomach cancer
What is living with stomach cancer?
Explain living with stomach cancer
responses: Living with stomach cancer and the effects of surgery can be tough, but there are a range of services that can provide social, psychological and financial support.

patterns: Tell me about about stomach ulcers
What is about stomach ulcers?
Explain about stomach ulcers
responses: Stomach ulcers (gastric ulcers) are open sores that develop on the lining of the stomach. Ulcers can also occur in part of the intestine just beyond the stomach. These are called duodenal ulcers.Stomach and duodenal ulcers are sometimes called peptic ulcers. This information applies to both.

patterns: Tell me about symptoms of stomach ulcers
What is symptoms of stomach ulcers?
Explain symptoms of stomach ulcers
responses: The most common symptom of a stomach ulcer is a burning or gnawing pain in the centre of the abdomen, although not all stomach ulcers are painful. Some people may also experience indigestion, heartburn, or nausea (feeling sick). If you think you have a stomach ulcer, it's important to speak to your GP for proper diagnosis and treatment.

patterns: Tell me about what causes stomach ulcers?
What is what causes stomach ulcers??
Explain what causes stomach ulcers?
responses: Stomach ulcers occur when the layer protecting the stomach lining from stomach acid breaks down, allowing the stomach lining to become damaged. This is usually caused by an infection with Helicobacter pylori (H. pylori) bacteria or by taking non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drugs (NSAIDs), such as ibuprofen or aspirin, particularly when taken for a long time or at high doses. There is little evidence that stress or certain foods cause stomach ulcers.

patterns: Tell me about treating stomach ulcers
What is treating stomach ulcers?
Explain treating stomach ulcers
responses: If your ulcer was caused by a H. pylori infection, you'll be treated with antibiotics to kill the bacteria and prevent the ulcer from returning. If your ulcer was caused by NSAIDs, you'll be treated with a proton pump inhibitor (PPI), which reduces stomach acid. Your doctor will also discuss whether you should continue using NSAIDs and may recommend alternative medications, such as paracetamol. Most stomach ulcers take a few months to heal after treatment, but they can recur, although this is less likely if the underlying cause, like the H. pylori infection or NSAID use, is addressed.

patterns: Tell me about complications
What is complications?
Explain complications
responses: Complications from stomach ulcers are rare but can be very serious and potentially life-threatening. The main complications include bleeding at the site of the ulcer, perforation where the stomach lining splits open, and gastric obstruction, which blocks the movement of food through the digestive system.

patterns: Tell me about gas infections
What is gas infections?
Explain gas infections
responses: GAS (Group A Streptococcus) bacteria can cause a wide variety of skin, soft tissue, and respiratory tract infections, ranging from mild to life-threatening. These include tonsillitis, sore throat, scarlet fever, impetigo, erysipelas (a type of cellulitis), cellulitis, and pneumonia. In rare cases, patients may go on to develop post-streptococcal complications, such as rheumatic fever and glomerulonephritis, which are heart and kidney diseases caused by an immune reaction to the bacteria.

patterns: Tell me about igas infections
What is igas infections?
Explain igas infections
responses: GAS can very rarely cause more serious conditions, known as invasive group A Streptococcus (iGAS) infections. These can include bacteraemia (an infection of the bloodstream), septic arthritis, meningitis, necrotising fasciitis (a severe infection involving death of areas of soft tissue below the skin), and streptococcal toxic shock syndrome (characterized by rapidly progressive symptoms with low blood pressure and multi-organ failure). iGAS infections tend to occur in the elderly, the very young, or individuals with an underlying risk factor. Groups considered to be at risk for serious GAS infections include people with existing health conditions such as diabetes, cardiovascular disease, or a weakened immune system, people who have recently had flu or chickenpox, newborn babies, women who are pregnant or have recently given birth, and people who are homeless or inject drugs.

patterns: Tell me about treating gas infections
What is treating gas infections?
Explain treating gas infections
responses: Most GAS infections are relatively mild and clear up on their own without the need for antibiotics. Often, symptoms resembling GAS infections, such as sore throats, are more commonly caused by viruses than GAS bacteria. For example, if you or your child has a runny nose with their sore throat, it's likely to be a viral infection. Sore throats caused by viruses typically don't need antibiotics unless there are concerns about complications. Your healthcare professional may consider prescribing antibiotics if they believe you or your child need medication to help you recover or ease symptoms, are at risk of serious complications from a GAS infection, or if you have scarlet fever. Antibiotics may be in liquid or pill form, depending on the situation. It’s also not uncommon for someone to carry GAS bacteria in their throat or on their skin without being ill, and in such cases, they won't need antibiotics. If you're unsure about whether you need antibiotics or are concerned about your symptoms, it's always best to consult with a healthcare professional.

patterns: Tell me about preventing gas infection
What is preventing gas infection?
Explain preventing gas infection
responses: GAS are spread by close contact between individuals, through respiratory droplets (moisture in your breath) and direct skin contact.
To help reduce the risk of picking up or spreading infections:

patterns: Tell me about signs and symptoms of a stroke
What is signs and symptoms of a stroke?
Explain signs and symptoms of a stroke
responses: The main symptoms of a stroke can be remembered with the word FAST, which stands for Face, Arms, Speech, and Time. If a person’s face droops on one side, they may not be able to smile, their mouth or eyelid may droop, or they might be unable to lift both arms due to weakness or numbness. They might slur their speech or be unable to talk, even if awake. If any of these symptoms occur, it's crucial to phone 999 immediately. A stroke might also cause symptoms like complete paralysis on one side of the body, loss or blurring of vision, difficulty understanding others, problems with balance and coordination, a sudden severe headache, or loss of feeling in one side of the body. However, these symptoms may also have other causes.

patterns: Tell me about why do strokes happen?
What is why do strokes happen??
Explain why do strokes happen?
responses: The brain, like all organs, requires oxygen and nutrients from blood to function properly. If blood supply is restricted or stops, brain cells begin to die, potentially leading to brain injury, disability, and even death. There are two main causes of strokes: ischaemic, which accounts for 85% of cases, occurs when a blood clot stops the blood supply, and haemorrhagic, which happens when a weakened blood vessel supplying the brain bursts. There's also a related condition known as a transient ischaemic attack (TIA), which is often referred to as a mini-stroke.

patterns: Tell me about ‘mini-stroke’ or transient ischaemic attack (tia)
What is ‘mini-stroke’ or transient ischaemic attack (tia)?
Explain ‘mini-stroke’ or transient ischaemic attack (tia)
responses: The symptoms of a TIA are the same as a stroke, but they only last between a few minutes and a few hours before disappearing completely.Although the symptoms do improve, you should never ignore a TIA. A TIA is a serious warning sign there’s a problem with the blood supply to your brain. This means you’re at an increased risk of having a stroke in the near future.

patterns: Tell me about who is at risk of a stroke?
What is at risk of a stroke ?
Explain who can be at the risk of a stroke?
responses: There are several factors that can increase the likelihood of having a stroke or TIA, including a poor diet, lack of exercise, obesity, smoking, excessive alcohol consumption, high cholesterol, high blood pressure (hypertension), diabetes, and atrial fibrillation. Lifestyle changes, such as improving diet, increasing physical activity, and managing weight, can help control some of these risk factors. In addition, regular medication may be necessary, such as anticoagulants for those with atrial fibrillation to reduce the risk of stroke. If you've experienced a stroke or TIA, these measures become even more crucial, as you are at a higher risk of having another stroke.

patterns: Tell me about diagnosing a stroke
What is diagnosing a stroke?
Explain diagnosing a stroke
responses: Strokes are typically diagnosed through physical tests and brain scans. To confirm the diagnosis and determine the cause of the stroke, several tests may be conducted, including checking your pulse for an irregular heartbeat, measuring your blood pressure, and performing blood tests to check cholesterol and blood sugar levels.

patterns: Tell me about how strokes are treated
What is how strokes are treated?
Explain how strokes are treated
responses: Effective treatment of stroke can reduce the risk of long-term disability and save lives. Treatment of stroke has two main purposes: immediate treatment within the first few hours to minimize brain damage and reduce long-term effects, which is why it’s critical to get urgent help by phoning 999 or going to A&E if you suspect someone is having a stroke. The second purpose is to reduce the risk of further strokes and prevent complications. The specific treatments depend on the type of stroke, whether it’s caused by a blood clot obstructing blood flow to the brain (ischaemic stroke) or a burst blood vessel bleeding in or around the brain (haemorrhagic stroke). Treatment usually involves medications, and some people may also require surgery. Seeking treatment as soon as possible improves the chances of a good recovery.

patterns: Tell me about life after a stroke
What is life after a stroke?
Explain life after a stroke
responses: The brain injury caused by a stroke can lead to long-lasting problems. Some people who’ve had a stroke need rehabilitation to help them regain as much independence as possible. Rehabilitation often starts in the hospital, at a local clinic, and might continue at home. A team of specialists is available to assist with rehabilitation, including physiotherapists, speech and language therapists, occupational therapists, dietitians, psychologists, specialist nurses, and doctors. These specialists can help you with various aspects of recovery, including mobility, communication, daily living activities, nutrition, emotional well-being, and overall health management.

patterns: Tell me about caring for someone who’s had a stroke
What is caring for someone who’s had a stroke?
Explain caring for someone who’s had a stroke
responses: Around half the people who have a stroke will need some form of care or help with their daily activities. For example, a care worker could come to the person’s home to help with household tasks, or to provide companionship. There are lots of ways you can support a friend or relative who’s had a stroke to speed up their rehabilitation.

patterns: Tell me about sudden infant death syndrome
What is sudden infant death syndrome?
Explain sudden infant death syndrome
responses: SIDS happens most often during sleep at any time, day or night. Doctors don’t yet know what causes it but it’s most likely to happen in the first 6 months. Babies born early and underweight, and twins or multiple babies are more at risk.

patterns: Tell me about sharing a bed with your baby
What is sharing a bed with your baby?
Explain sharing a bed with your baby
responses: Adult beds aren’t designed for babies. Before you bed-share, consider whether you think it’s a safe place for your baby to sleep. Check that: your baby cannot fall out of the bed, your baby cannot get trapped between the mattress and the wall, your adult bedding can be kept away from your baby, and other children and pets will not be in the bed at the same time as your baby. Mothers do sometimes bed-share when breastfeeding, however, without some planning and thought it can be very dangerous. If you are breastfeeding while lying down, make sure your baby cannot roll onto their front. Try and keep your baby on their back, or move them onto their back once they have been fed. If you’re thinking about bed-sharing, talk to your midwife, health visitor, or family nurse about how to reduce the risks for your baby. Baby Sleep Info Source has more information about bed-sharing.

patterns: Tell me about how much sleep’s normal?
What is how much sleep’s normal??
Explain how much sleep’s normal?
responses: There’s no normal amount of sleep and some babies sleep more than others. New babies sleep a lot – sometimes as much as 18 hours a day for the first month or so. But your baby probably won’t sleep for more than a few hours at a time to begin with.

patterns: Why do babies cry?
What causes a baby to cry?
Can you explain why babies cry?
What should I know about babies crying?
Tell me about how babies cry and what it means
responses: When your baby cries and you go to them and comfort them, you’re teaching them the world’s a safe place. This helps them to develop the skills to sleep through the night.How to soothe a crying baby

patterns: Tell me about plagiocephaly
What is plagiocephaly?
Explain plagiocephaly
responses: Sometimes babies develop a flatter area either at the back of or on one side of their head. This is called plagiocephaly.Plagiocephaly usually gets better on its own if your baby’s spending less time lying down.

patterns: Tell me about what to do if you’re sunburnt
What is what to do if you’re sunburnt?
Explain what to do if you’re sunburnt
responses: If you or your child has sunburn, you should get out of the sun as soon as possible – head indoors or into a shady area.You can usually treat mild sunburn at home, although there are some circumstances where you should seek medical advice.The following advice may help to relieve your symptoms until your skin heals.

patterns: What are the dangers of UV rays?
Can you explain the dangers of UV rays?
Why are UV rays harmful?
How do UV rays affect the skin?
Tell me about the risks of UV radiation
responses: The short-term risks of sun exposure include sunburn and sun allergy. Over the long term, repeated exposure to UV rays can lead to actinic (solar) keratoses, which are rough and scaly pre-cancerous spots on the skin, and increase the risk of skin cancer, including both melanoma and non-melanoma types. Prolonged sun exposure can also cause eye problems like photokeratitis (snow blindness) and cataracts, as well as contribute to premature aging of the skin, including wrinkling

patterns: Tell me about preventing sunburn
What is preventing sunburn?
Explain preventing sunburn
responses: Skin should be protected from strong sunlight by covering up with suitable clothing, seeking shade and applying sunscreen.In the UK, the risk of getting sunburnt is highest from March to October, particularly from 11am to 3pm, when the sun’s rays are strongest.There is also a risk of getting sunburn in other weather conditions. For example, light reflecting off snow can also cause sunburn. You can also burn in cloudy and cool conditions.

patterns: Tell me about advice for babies and children
What is advice for babies and children?
Explain advice for babies and children
responses: Children aged under 6 months should be kept out of direct strong sunlight.From March to October in the UK, children should:cover up with suitable clothing spend time in the shade (particularly from 11am to 3pm)wear at least SPF30 sunscreen To ensure they get enough vitamin D, children aged under 5 are advised to take vitamin D supplements even if they do get out in the sun.

patterns: Tell me about what are the lymph glands?
What is what are the lymph glands??
Explain what are the lymph glands?
responses: The lymph glands (or lymph nodes) are small lumps of tissue that contain white blood cells. These help to fight bacteria, viruses and anything else that causes infection. They’re an important part of the immune system and are found throughout the body.

patterns: Tell me about what swollen lymph glands feel like
What is what swollen lymph glands feel like?
Explain what swollen lymph glands feel like
responses: Swollen lymph glands can enlarge by more than a few centimeters in response to infection or disease. These swollen glands typically feel like tender, painful lumps, and you may feel them on each side of the neck, under the chin, in the armpits, or around the groin.

patterns: Tell me about common causes of swollen glands
What is common causes of swollen glands?
Explain common causes of swollen glands
responses: Swollen glands are usually caused by a viral or bacterial infection, such as a cold, tonsillitis, glandular fever, a throat infection, an ear infection, a dental abscess, or cellulitis (a skin infection). The glands in the affected area often become suddenly tender or painful, and you may also experience other symptoms like a sore throat, cough, or fever. These infections typically clear up on their own, and the swollen glands will gradually go down. Swollen lymph glands are rarely caused by anything more serious.

patterns: Tell me about managing swollen glands at home
What is managing swollen glands at home?
Explain managing swollen glands at home
responses: When an infection causes swollen glands you can help to ease symptoms at home.

patterns: Tell me about introduction
What is introduction?
Explain introduction
responses: Self-harm is when someone intentionally damages or injures their body, often as a way of expressing deep emotional feelings like low self-esteem or coping with traumatic events, such as the death of a loved one. If you are self-harming, it's important to seek help from your GP. You can also contact Breathing Space or call the Samaritans at 116 123 for support. While self-harm is an expression of personal distress rather than an illness, it can be linked to other mental health conditions, such as depression.

patterns: Tell me about what is tennis elbow?
What is what is tennis elbow??
Explain what is tennis elbow?
responses: Tennis elbow is a condition that causes pain around the outside bony part of the elbow and down into the forearm. It is also known as lateral epicondylitis or lateral epicondyle tendinopathy. The most commonly affected arm is the dominant side. Tennis elbow is often an overuse injury, typically caused by repetitive strain from tasks and activities that involve repeated gripping and hand movements. Although it is commonly associated with racquet sports, many other activities can also lead to this condition.

patterns: Tell me about symptoms of tennis elbow?
What is symptoms of tennis elbow??
Explain symptoms of tennis elbow?
responses: Tennis elbow causes pain and tenderness on the outside of your elbow that may spread down your forearm. This can lead to difficulty with gripping, difficulty with twisting movements such as opening jars, restriction in movement at the elbow, and tenderness when touching the affected area.

patterns: Tell me about what causes tennis elbow?
What is what causes tennis elbow??
Explain what causes tennis elbow?
responses: Tennis elbow symptoms develop when there’s a problem with the tendons around the elbow, which help lift your hand backwards. Activities that involve the use of these tendons can include gripping, writing, lifting, pulling, and twisting. Repeated use of the muscles that lift your wrist up can cause changes to the tendon, often due to overloading it. Other factors that can affect tendon health include smoking, obesity, high fat diet, high cholesterol levels, and diabetes (Type 1 and 2). Tennis elbow is considered a self-limiting condition, meaning it can improve on its own over time in up to 90% of people with the condition.

patterns: Tell me about tennis elbow diagnosis
What is tennis elbow diagnosis?
Explain tennis elbow diagnosis
responses: In order for tennis elbow to be diagnosed, you’ll need an assessment from a healthcare professional. They’ll initially ask you questions about your elbow issue and then carry out a physical examination. This may include checking your range of movement and strength. You may sometimes need to have a further examination to rule out other possible health conditions.

patterns: Tell me about treatment for tennis elbow
What is treatment for tennis elbow?
Explain treatment for tennis elbow
responses: If your elbow pain is aggravated by certain movements, try to reduce these by modifying your activity. You can modify your activity by: changing how you grip or lift an object, reducing the weight you are carrying, and taking regular breaks while doing aggravating activities. As well as modifying your activities, it’s important to start exercising your elbow.

patterns: Tell me about tennis elbow work
Explain work
responses: An elbow problem can sometimes mean you need to take some time off work to help recovery. How long you’re off will depend on the nature of your condition and your role at work.You do not need to be symptom free before you consider returning to work. Continuing to go to work, or returning to work as soon as is possible for you, will help your recovery. Gradually getting back to your normal daily activities can help to build up your strength and stamina levels.

patterns: Tell me about help and support
What is help and support?
Explain help and support
responses: Following this advice, you should see gradual improvements over time.If your tennis elbow hasn’t improved, or it’s got worse, within 6 weeks of following this advice, it’s a good idea to talk to a healthcare professional about your symptoms.

patterns: Tell me about about testicular cancer
What is about testicular cancer?
Explain about testicular cancer
responses: Cancer of the testicle is one of the less common cancers, primarily affecting men and anyone with testicles between the ages of 15 and 49. The most common symptom is a painless lump or swelling in one of the testicles, which can be the size of a pea or much larger. Other symptoms can include a dull ache in the scrotum and a feeling of heaviness in the scrotum. It’s important to be aware of what feels normal for you, get to know your body, and speak to your GP if you notice any changes.

patterns: Tell me about types of testicular cancer
What is types of testicular cancer?
Explain types of testicular cancer
responses: Testicular cancer is classified based on the type of cells where the cancer begins. The most common type is germ cell testicular cancer, which accounts for around 95% of all cases. Germ cells are the cells the body uses to create sperm. There are two main subtypes of germ cell testicular cancer: seminomas, which have become more common in the last 20 years and now account for 50 to 55% of testicular cancers, and non-seminomas, which make up most of the rest and include teratomas, embryonal carcinomas, choriocarcinomas, and yolk sac tumors. Both types tend to respond well to chemotherapy. Less common types of testicular cancer include Leydig cell tumors (about 1 to 3% of cases), Sertoli cell tumors (around 1% of cases), and lymphoma (about 4% of cases). This topic focuses on germ cell testicular cancer. For more information on Leydig and Sertoli cell tumors, you can contact the Macmillan Support Line at 0808 808 00 00, open Monday to Friday, 8am to 8pm.

patterns: Tell me about how common is testicular cancer?
What is how common is testicular cancer??
Explain how common is testicular cancer?
responses: Testicular cancer is a relatively rare type of cancer.Testicular cancer is unusual compared to other cancers because it tends to affect younger men and anyone with testicles. Although it’s relatively uncommon overall, testicular cancer is the most common type of cancer to affect men and anyone with testicles between the ages of 15 and 49.For reasons that are unclear, white men and anyone with testicles have a higher risk of developing testicular cancer compared with other ethnic groups.The number of cases of testicular cancer that are diagnosed each year in the UK has roughly doubled since the mid-1970s. Again, the reasons for this are unclear.

patterns: Tell me about causes of testicular cancer
What is causes of testicular cancer?
Explain causes of testicular cancer
