responses: The exact cause or causes of testicular cancer are unknown, but a number of factors have been identified that increase a man’s risk of developing it. The 3 main risk factors are described below.

patterns: Tell me about testicular cancer outlook
What is testicular cancer outlook?
Explain testicular cancer outlook
responses: Testicular cancer is classified based on the type of cells where the cancer begins. The most common type is germ cell testicular cancer which accounts for around 95% of all cases. Germ cells are the cells the body uses to create sperm. There are two main subtypes of germ cell testicular cancer: seminomas, which have become more common in the last 20 years and now account for 50 to 55% of testicular cancers, and non-seminomas, which make up most of the rest and include teratomas, embryonal carcinomas, choriocarcinomas, and yolk sac tumors. Both types tend to respond well to chemotherapy. Less common types of testicular cancer include Leydig cell tumors (about 1 to 3% of cases), Sertoli cell tumors (around 1% of cases), and lymphoma (about 4% of cases). This topic focuses on germ cell testicular cancer

patterns: Tell me about the testicules
What is the testicules?
Explain the testicules
responses: It can be embarrassing to talk about your testicles and any changes to them that you’ve noticed. Understanding more about what they do might help.

patterns: Tell me about facts about testicles
What is facts about testicles?
Explain facts about testicles
responses: Testicles are small and oval-shaped. They hang below your penis in a sac called the scrotum.From puberty, your testicles produce a hormone called testosterone. This gives you a lower voice, hair on your face and body, and makes your muscles get bigger. You also need it for your sex drive and for getting an erection.The testicles make sperm from puberty (around 13 to 14 years old) onwards. When sperm meets with a female egg during sex, this can cause pregnancy.The testicles make millions of sperm a day, but if sperm are not ejaculated they are reabsorbed back into your body.Sperm wait in the epididymis next to the testicle until they are ejaculated. At ejaculation, they travel up through the spermatic cord and mix with fluid from the prostate to make semen. They go out of the body through the urethra (this is the same tube you pass urine (pee) through).

patterns: Tell me about lymph nodes (glands)
What is lymph nodes (glands)?
Explain lymph nodes (glands)
responses: Lymph nodes are small and round, and connect to each other by tiny tubes that carry fluid called lymph. We have lymph nodes throughout our bodies. They are part of the lymphatic system, which protects us from infections.Sometimes cancer cells from the testicle can spread to lymph nodes at the back of the abdomen (tummy). You’ll have a scan to check your lymph nodes.If you’re looking for information for all ages about testicular cancer, read our general testicular cancer section.

patterns: Tell me about diagnosing testicular lumps and swellings
What is diagnosing testicular lumps and swellings?
Explain diagnosing testicular lumps and swellings
responses: There is not a singular test for testicular lumps and swellings. The doctor will discuss your situation and carry out a physical examination.Your doctor may:look at and feel your testicles shine a torch through the bag of skin containing your testicles (scrotum) to check for a build-up of fluid refer you for an ultrasound scan take a urine (pee) sample if they suspect your lump or swelling may be caused by an infection If the lump or swelling is painful or very big, your doctor may refer you to a specialist for an operation to drain, shrink or remove it.

patterns: Tell me about what causes testicular lumps and swellings?
What is what causes testicular lumps and swellings??
Explain what causes testicular lumps and swellings?
responses: Lumps and swellings in the testicles can have many different causes, most of which are non-cancerous (benign) and may not require treatment. Common causes include varicoceles (soft lumps developing above the testicle, usually on the left side), hydroceles (swelling from fluid buildup in the scrotum), epididymal cysts (small, smooth fluid-filled swellings in the epididymis), epididymo-orchitis (inflammation caused by infections like UTIs, STIs, and mumps), inguinal hernias (when fatty tissue or part of the bowel protrudes into the groin), and testicular torsion (a severe twisting of the spermatic cord). Testicular cancer, though less common, is another potential cause that requires medical attention. It is important to consult a healthcare professional if you notice any lumps or swelling in the testicles to determine the underlying cause.

patterns: Tell me about treatment for testicular lumps and swellings
What is treatment for testicular lumps and swellings?
Explain treatment for testicular lumps and swellings
responses: Treatment for testicular lumps and swellings will vary depending on what’s causing it.Painkillers such as paracetamol can be taken to ease discomfort or pain.In some cases, surgery may be used to treat some testicular lumps and swellings. The surgery is carried out under either local anaesthetic or general anaesthetic depending on the procedure.Antibiotics may be prescribed if the cause is a bacterial infection.If you have been diagnosed with testicular cancer, you will be cared for by a team of clinicians who will help decide the best treatment for you. Surgery to remove the affected testicle will be recommended in most cases and this may sometimes be followed by a course of chemotherapy or radiotherapy.

patterns: Tell me about about thirst
What is about thirst?
Explain about thirst
responses: Feeling thirsty all the time and for no good reason isn’t normal and should be investigated by your GP.Thirst is normally just the brain’s way of warning that you’re dehydrated because you’re not drinking enough fluid. But excessive and persistent thirst (known as polydipsia) could be a sign of an underlying problem such as diabetes.

patterns: Tell me about other causes of thirst
What is other causes of thirst?
Explain other causes of thirst
responses: There are also many other potential causes of severe thirst, including diabetes insipidus, a condition caused by problems with a hormone that regulates fluid in the body; diabetic ketoacidosis, a dangerous complication of diabetes due to a lack of insulin; sickle cell anemia, an inherited blood disorder; psychogenic polydipsia, where individuals with mental health conditions, such as schizophrenia, drink excessive amounts of water that cannot be excreted by the kidneys; and excessive bleeding. These conditions can lead to intense thirst and should be addressed by a healthcare professional for proper diagnosis and treatment.

patterns: Tell me about symptoms of threadworms
What is symptoms of threadworms?
Explain symptoms of threadworms
responses: You may spot threadworms in your poo, which look like threads of white cotton and are about 1cm long. You might also see them around your child’s bottom (anus), as they usually come out at night while your child is sleeping. Other symptoms include extreme itching around the anus or vagina, particularly at night, irritability, and waking up during the night. Less common symptoms include weight loss, bedwetting, and irritated skin around the anus.

patterns: Tell me about when to get medical advice
What is when to get medical advice?
Explain when to get medical advice
responses: If you or your child have threadworms you can get advice and treatment directly from a pharmacy.Your pharmacist may recommend that you speak to your GP if required.

patterns: Tell me about treatment for threadworms
What is treatment for threadworms?
Explain treatment for threadworms
responses: Medication will kill the threadworms but not the eggs. Eggs can live for up to 2 weeks outside the body.There are hygiene methods you can follow to stop becoming infected again. Do these for 2 weeks if you’re taking medicine, or for 6 weeks if you’re not taking medicine.

patterns: Tell me about threadworms treatment from a pharmacist
What is the treatment for threadworms from a pharmacist?
Explain how a pharmacist treats threadworms
responses: Mebendazole is the main medication used to treat threadworm infection and can be purchased over the counter from a pharmacy or prescribed by your GP. It is typically available as a chewable tablet or liquid. It’s important to treat everyone over 2 years old in your household, even if they do not show symptoms. If you need to treat a child under 2 years old or if you’re pregnant or breastfeeding, you should consult with a pharmacist, as the treatment may not be suitable, and a GP's advice may be required. If you cannot take medication, threadworms can be treated using hygiene methods for 6 weeks.

patterns: Tell me who is most at risk of threadworms?
Who is most at risk for getting threadworms?
Explain who is most at risk for threadworm infection?
responses: Threadworm infection most commonly affects young children because they often forget to wash their hands and share toys with other children. People who are in close contact with someone with a threadworm infection also have a high risk of infection. This is why all members of a household need to be treated when someone has a threadworm infection.

patterns: Tell me about how are threadworms passed on ?
What is how are threadworms passed on ?
Explain how are threadworms passed on ?
responses: Threadworms spread when their eggs are swallowed. They lay eggs around your bottom (anus) or vagina, which can cause an itchy bottom. The eggs get stuck on your fingers when you scratch, and they can then pass on to anything you touch, including clothes, toys, toothbrushes, kitchen or bathroom surfaces, bedding, and food. Eggs can pass to other people when they touch these surfaces and then touch their mouth. The eggs can survive up to 2 weeks. After you swallow the eggs, the larvae hatch from them in your gut and take 1 to 2 months to mature into threadworms. Children can get threadworms again after being treated if they get the eggs in their mouth. This is why it’s important to encourage children to wash their hands regularly.

patterns: Tell me about symptoms of thrush
What is symptoms of thrush?
Explain symptoms of thrush
responses: Some people won’t have any signs or symptoms of thrush at all.Sometimes there can be too much yeast and it can cause symptoms.

patterns: Tell me about testing for thrush
What is testing for thrush?
Explain testing for thrush
responses: If you think you might have thrush, get tested for free by:contacting your GP practice for an appointment ,booking an appointment at your local sexual health service ,Testing is also available in some pharmacies.

patterns: Tell me about what causes thrush?
What is what causes thrush??
Explain what causes thrush?
responses: Your chances of developing thrush increase if your skin is irritated or damaged, you're taking antibiotics, you have poorly controlled diabetes, you have a weakened immune system (for example, you're having chemotherapy or living with HIV), you're having hormone replacement therapy (HRT), you're pregnant, or you use products that irritate the skin, such as perfumed products, bubble baths, or vaginal washing products.

patterns: Tell me about how to prevent thrush
What is how to prevent thrush?
Explain how to prevent thrush
responses: Some people find that different triggers cause thrush. If you notice a pattern, you may be able to help control it.There are things you can do to relieve symptoms and stop thrush from coming back.

patterns: Tell me about regular thrush
What is regular thrush?
Explain regular thrush
responses: Some people may only get thrush once. Others may get it multiple times.You may need to take treatment for longer if you keep getting thrush (you get it more than 4 times in 12 months).If you get recurrent thrush the doctor or nurse will want to check that other conditions, such as diabetes, aren’t the cause of the thrush.They’ll recommend how often you should use treatment.

patterns: Tell me about recovery times for thumb fractures
What is the recovery time for a thumb fracture?
How long does it take to recover from a thumb fracture?
responses: The healing process for a thumb fracture typically takes between 3 to 6 weeks. While most of the healing happens during this period, it’s normal to continue experiencing some aches and discomfort beyond this time, especially when trying activities that haven’t been done for a while. The area may also remain more sensitive for some time after the injury. It’s important to use your hand in everyday activities to aid recovery, but remember that this can also be a form of exercise. If your hand starts aching after certain activities, it’s okay to rest it to prevent further strain.

patterns: Tell me about what can affect your recovery?
What is what can affect your recovery??
Explain what can affect your recovery?
responses: There are some things that might impact on your recovery.

patterns: Tell me about what to expect after a thumb fracture
What is what to expect after a thumb fracture?
Explain what to expect after a thumb fracture
responses: A thumb fracture is a minor fracture. It’s often treated in the same way as a sprain.Some people may be fitted with a splint or a wrist splint with a thumb piece. You may only need these for a couple of weeks.

patterns: Tell me about pain after a thumb fracture
What is pain after a thumb fracture?
Explain pain after a thumb fracture
responses: It’s normal to have some discomfort in the areas around your fracture, including:other soft tissues,nearby joints,areas that have been immobilised,Pain can change from day to day and it doesn’t always depend on what you’re doing. It’s common to have pain at rest.It’s normal to have some pain even when your fracture has healed. Some people also experience discomfort in the fracture site during colder weather.Your local pharmacy can give you advice on managing pain after a fracture.

patterns: Tell me about thumb fracture swelling
What is thumb fracture swelling?
Explain thumb fracture swelling
responses: Swelling is a natural part of the healing process and typically occurs near the site of the fracture. It can sometimes spread into your hand or wrist. The amount of swelling may vary depending on factors like your position, the level of activity, and the time of day. Even after the fracture has healed, it's normal to still experience some swelling, which can last for several months.

patterns: Tell me about how can i help thumb fracture swelling?
What is how can i help thumb fracture swelling??
Explain how can i help thumb fracture swelling?
responses: Excessive swelling after a thumb fracture can be uncomfortable and limit movement, but there are ways to manage it. To help reduce swelling, try raising your arm above your heart while resting, using pillows for support. It's also important to keep the other joints in your hand and arm moving normally and avoid both excessive rest and too much activity. These steps can help promote healing and prevent further discomfort.

patterns: Tell me about thumb fracture bruising
What is thumb fracture bruising?
Explain thumb fracture bruising
responses: It’s normal to have bruising after a fracture.Bruising can be widespread and may appear a long way from your fracture. It can be very purple to start with and may change colour as you recover.

patterns: Tell me about getting back to normal activities
What is getting back to normal activities?
Explain getting back to normal activities
responses: There are some things you should consider when trying to get back to your normal activities.

patterns: Tell me about rehabilitation plan
What is rehabilitation plan?
Explain rehabilitation plan
responses: There are things you can do to help your recovery after a thumb fracture.

patterns: Tell me about help and support
What is help and support?
Explain help and support
responses: If your thumb function hasn’t improved within 6 weeks of following this advice, it’s a good idea to talk to a healthcare professional about your symptoms.

patterns: Tell me about about thyroid cancer
What is about thyroid cancer?
Explain about thyroid cancer
responses: Thyroid cancer is a rare cancer that affects the thyroid gland, located at the base of the neck. The most common symptom is a painless lump or swelling in the neck. Other symptoms usually appear only in advanced stages and may include unexplained hoarseness lasting more than a few weeks, a sore throat or difficulty swallowing that doesn’t improve, and a lump elsewhere in the neck. Having a lump in the thyroid gland does not automatically mean thyroid cancer, as only about 1 in 20 thyroid lumps are cancerous

patterns: Tell me about the thyroid gland
What is the thyroid gland?
Explain the thyroid gland
responses: The thyroid gland consists of two lobes on either side of the windpipe and plays a crucial role in releasing hormones that impact various body functions. It releases three hormones: triiodothyronine (T3), thyroxine (T4), and calcitonin. T3 and T4 regulate the body’s metabolic rate, affecting how quickly processes like calorie burning occur. An excess of these hormones can make you feel overactive and cause weight loss, while a deficiency can make you feel sluggish and lead to weight gain. Calcitonin helps control blood calcium levels, which are important for building strong bones, though it’s not essential for maintaining health, as the body has other mechanisms for controlling calcium.

patterns: Tell me about types of thyroid cancer
What is types of thyroid cancer?
Explain types of thyroid cancer
responses: There are four main types of thyroid cancer: papillary carcinoma, which is the most common and accounts for about 60% of cases, typically affecting those under 40, especially women; follicular carcinoma, which makes up around 15% of cases and generally affects older adults; medullary thyroid carcinoma, which accounts for 5-8% of cases and can run in families; and anaplastic thyroid carcinoma, the rarest and most aggressive type, accounting for less than 5% of cases, typically affecting individuals over 60. Papillary and follicular carcinomas are often grouped together as differentiated thyroid cancers and are usually treated similarly.

patterns: Tell me about how common is thyroid cancer?
What is how common is thyroid cancer??
Explain how common is thyroid cancer?
responses: Thyroid cancer is a rare form of cancer, making up less than 1% of all cancer cases in the UK. It is most commonly diagnosed in people aged 35 to 39 years and those aged 70 or older. Women are 2 to 3 times more likely to develop thyroid cancer than men, although the exact reason for this is unclear. It is thought that hormonal changes associated with the female reproductive system may play a role in this increased risk.

patterns: Tell me about what causes thyroid cancer?
What is what causes thyroid cancer??
Explain what causes thyroid cancer?
responses: In most cases, the cause of thyroid cancer is unknown, but certain factors can increase the risk of developing the condition. Risk factors include having a benign (non-cancerous) thyroid condition, a family history of thyroid cancer (especially in cases of medullary thyroid cancer), having a bowel condition called familial adenomatous polyposis, acromegaly (a rare condition where the body produces too much growth hormone), a history of benign (non-cancerous) breast conditions, and factors such as weight and height. Additionally, exposure to radiation can also increase the risk of thyroid cancer.

patterns: Tell me about diagnosing thyroid cancer
What is diagnosing thyroid cancer?
Explain diagnosing thyroid cancer
responses: A thyroid function test is a blood test that measures hormone levels in your blood, helping to rule out or confirm other thyroid problems. If no other cause for a lump in the thyroid is found, fine-needle aspiration cytology (FNAC) is often used to obtain a sample of the lump for examination. If the FNAC results are inconclusive, or if additional information is needed to determine the most effective treatment, further testing may be required.

patterns: Tell me about treating thyroid cancer
What is treating thyroid cancer?
Explain treating thyroid cancer
responses: Your recommended treatment plan for thyroid cancer will depend on the type and grade of your cancer, as well as whether a complete cure is realistically achievable. Differentiated thyroid cancers (DTCs) are typically treated with a combination of surgery to remove the thyroid gland (thyroidectomy) and a type of radiotherapy that destroys any remaining cancer cells, reducing the risk of recurrence. Medullary thyroid carcinomas, which tend to spread faster than DTCs, may require the removal of nearby lymph nodes in addition to the thyroid gland.

patterns: Tell me about thyroid cancer recurrence
What is thyroid cancer recurrence?
Explain thyroid recurrence
responses: Cancerous cells may return in an estimated 5% to 20% of individuals with a history of thyroid cancer. In about 10% to 15% of cases, the cancer may spread to other parts of the body, such as the bones. It is possible for cancerous cells to return many years after surgery and radioactive iodine treatment have been completed. To monitor for any recurrence, you’ll be asked to attend regular check-ups so that any returning cancerous cells can be treated promptly.

patterns: Tell me about preventing thyroid cancer
What is preventing thyroid cancer?
Explain preventing thyroid cancer
responses: Eating a healthy, balanced diet is considered the best way to reduce the risk of thyroid cancer, as well as other types of cancer, according to available evidence. A diet that is low in fat and high in fiber, which includes plenty of fresh fruit and vegetables (at least five portions a day) and whole grains, is recommended for optimal health.

patterns: Tell me about thyroid cancer
What is thyroid cancer
Explain thyroid cancer
responses: This section provides information about thyroid cancer specifically for teens and young adults. If you're looking for information about thyroid cancer in people of all ages, please refer to our general thyroid cancer section. The most common types of thyroid cancer in young adults are papillary and follicular thyroid cancers. If you're interested in learning about other types of thyroid cancer, you can reach out to Macmillan Cancer Support for further guidance. It's important to remember that thyroid cancer in young people can often be successfully treated, and most young people are completely cured.

patterns: Tell me about symptoms of tick bites
What is symptoms of tick bites?
Explain symptoms of tick bites
responses: Tick bites aren’t usually painful, but they may cause a red lump to develop at the site of the bite. In some cases, tick bites can lead to swelling, itchiness, blistering, or bruising.

patterns: Tell me about the sore throat self-help guide
What is the sore throat self-help guide?
Explain the sore throat self-help guide
responses: Tonsillitis, which is an inflammation of the tonsils, often causes a sore throat, pain when swallowing, and swollen lymph nodes. In some cases, it can be caused by a viral infection like the common cold or a bacterial infection such as Streptococcus (strep throat). Depending on the cause, treatment options may vary. If your symptoms are severe, last longer than a few days, or include fever, difficulty swallowing, or a persistent cough, it's recommended to consult with a healthcare provider. This guide will help you assess whether you need medical attention or if home remedies like rest, hydration, and warm saltwater gargles may help alleviate symptoms.

patterns: Tell me about symptoms of tonsillitis
What is symptoms of tonsillitis?
Explain symptoms of tonsillitis
responses: The symptoms of tonsillitis include a sore throat, pain when swallowing, earache, a high temperature (fever) over 38°C (100.4°F), coughing, headache, feeling sick, feeling tired, swollen and painful lymph glands in the neck, white pus-filled spots on the tonsils, bad breath, and loss or changes to your voice. Symptoms usually improve within 3 to 4 days.

patterns: Tell me about diagnosing tonsillitis
What is diagnosing tonsillitis?
Explain diagnosing tonsillitis
responses: If needed, your GP will examine your throat and ask questions about your symptoms. They may take a swab of your throat to determine whether the cause is a viral or bacterial infection, with results typically taking a few days. If you develop severe tonsillitis as a teenager or adult, your GP may also recommend a blood test to check for glandular fever.

patterns: Tell me about treating tonsillitis
What is treating tonsillitis?
Explain treating tonsillitis
responses: There’s no specific treatment for tonsillitis. It usually gets better on its own within a week.

patterns: Tell me about what the causes of tonsillitis?
What is the causes of tonsillitis ?
responses: Most cases of tonsillitis are caused by a viral infection, such as the common cold or flu. However, some cases can also be triggered by a bacterial infection, typically a strain of bacteria called group A streptococcus. While tonsillitis itself isn’t contagious, the infections that cause it are.

patterns: Tell me about complications of tonsillitis
What is complications of tonsillitis?
Explain complications of tonsillitis
responses: Complications of tonsillitis are rare and typically only occur if it's caused by a bacterial infection, often resulting from the infection spreading to another part of the body. Possible complications include quinsy (peritonsillar abscess), which is an abscess that forms between one of the tonsils and the wall of the throat, and obstructive sleep apnoea (OSA), where the walls of the throat relax during sleep, causing breathing difficulties and poor sleep. Other complications are very rare and usually only happen if an underlying bacterial infection is left untreated.

patterns: Tell me about symptoms of tooth decay
What is symptoms of tooth decay?
Explain symptoms of tooth decay
responses: Tooth decay may not cause any pain initially, but if you have dental caries, you might experience toothache, which could be either continuous pain that keeps you awake or occasional sharp pain without an obvious cause. You may also have tooth sensitivity, feeling tenderness or pain when eating or drinking something hot, cold, or sweet. Other signs include grey, brown, or black spots appearing on your teeth, bad breath, and an unpleasant taste in your mouth.

patterns: Why should I see a dentist for tooth decay?
What happens when I see a dentist for tooth decay?
Why is it important to visit a dentist for tooth decay?
responses: Visit your dentist regularly, so early tooth decay can be treated as soon as possible and the prevention of decay can begin. Tooth decay is much easier and cheaper to treat in its early stages. Dentists can usually identify tooth decay and further problems with a simple examination or X-ray.

patterns: Tell me about treatments for tooth decay
What is treatments for tooth decay?
Explain treatments for tooth decay
responses: Treatment of tooth decay depends on how advanced it is. For early-stage decay, your dentist will discuss the amount of sugar in your diet and the times you eat, and may apply a fluoride gel, varnish, or paste to the area. Fluoride helps protect teeth by strengthening the enamel, making them more resistant to the acids from plaque that cause decay. For more advanced decay, your dentist may recommend a filling or crown, which involves removing the decay, numbing the tooth with local anaesthetic, and filling the hole. If the decay has spread to the pulp (the centre of the tooth containing blood and nerves), it may require root canal treatment. If the tooth is too damaged to be restored, it may need to be removed, and your dentist may offer a replacement with a partial denture or bridge.

patterns: What is the cost of NHS dental treatment?
How much does NHS dental treatment cost?
Tell me about the charges for NHS dental treatment
Do I have to pay for NHS dental treatment?
Who qualifies for free NHS dental treatment?
responses: NHS charges are set by the government and are standard for all NHS patients. However, some people don’t have to pay for dental treatment, including those under 26, pregnant individuals, or those who have given birth in the last 12 months. Financial help may also be available for those on a low income or receiving certain benefits. The cost of private dental treatment, on the other hand, varies between practices as there is no set charge. If you choose private dental care, it's important to agree on the cost before receiving treatment.

patterns: Tell me about preventing tooth decay
What is preventing tooth decay?
Explain preventing tooth decay
responses: Although tooth decay is a common problem, it’s often entirely preventable. The best way to avoid tooth decay is to keep your teeth and gums as healthy as possible.

patterns: How can I protect my child’s teeth?
What are some tips for keeping my child’s teeth healthy?
How do I prevent tooth decay in my child?
What can I do to protect my child’s teeth from decay?
Tell me how to take care of my child’s teeth
What should I know about brushing my child’s teeth?
responses: Good eating habits, such as limiting sugary snacks and drinks, can help your child avoid tooth decay, and regular visits to the dentist from an early age should also be encouraged. You should start brushing your child’s teeth and gums daily as soon as their first tooth grows in. Childsmile provides advice for keeping children’s teeth healthy at different age stages, including under 3s, 3 to 5-year-olds, and 5 to 12-year-olds. Additionally, children should brush their teeth twice a day, especially before bedtime. Find out more about toothbrushing for babies, toddlers, and pre-schoolers to ensure proper dental care.

patterns: How does plaque cause tooth decay?
What role does plaque play in tooth decay?
Can plaque cause tooth decay?
How does plaque affect my teeth?
Explain the process of plaque causing tooth decay
responses: Your mouth is full of bacteria that form a film over the teeth called dental plaque. When you consume food and drinks high in sugars, the bacteria in plaque turn the sugars into energy, producing acid in the process. If plaque is allowed to build up, the acid can begin to break down (dissolve) the surface of your tooth, leading to cavities. Once cavities form in the enamel, the plaque and bacteria can reach the dentine, which is softer than enamel, causing the decay process to speed up. Without treatment, bacteria can enter the pulp, the soft center of the tooth that contains nerves and blood vessels, exposing the nerves to bacteria and causing pain. This can lead to a dental abscess in the pulp, and the infection may spread into the bone.

patterns: Tell me about what causes toothache?
What is what causes toothache??
Explain what causes toothache?
responses: Toothache occurs when the innermost layer of the tooth, called the dental pulp, becomes inflamed. The pulp is made up of sensitive nerves and blood vessels. Dental pulp can become inflamed due to tooth decay, which leads to cavities in the hard surface of the tooth; a cracked tooth, which may be too small to see with the naked eye; loose or broken fillings; receding gums, where the gums shrink and expose sensitive parts of the tooth root; or a periapical abscess, which is a collection of pus at the end of the tooth caused by a bacterial infection. Other conditions can cause pain similar to toothache, even if the pulp isn't affected, such as a periodontal abscess (pus in the gums from a bacterial infection), ulcers on the gums, sore or swollen gums around a tooth that's breaking through (like when wisdom teeth emerge), sinusitis (which can cause pain around the upper jaw), an injury to the jaw joint, or teething in babies.

patterns: How do you treat a toothache?
What are the treatments for toothache?
Can you explain how to treat a toothache?
What can be done for a toothache?
How should I treat my toothache?
What are the options for treating toothache?
responses: The type of treatment you have for toothache will depend on the cause of the pain. Your dentist will examine your mouth and may carry out an X-ray to try to identify the problem. If your toothache is caused by tooth decay, your dentist will remove the decayed area and replace it with a filling. If your toothache is caused by a loose or broken filling, the filling will be taken out, any decay will be removed, and a new filling put in place.

If the pulp inside your tooth is infected, you may need root canal treatment. This procedure involves removing the infected pulp and then inserting a special type of filling to seal the tooth and prevent reinfection. Your tooth may need to be removed if the toothache can’t be treated using these methods or if the tooth is wedged between another tooth and your jaw (impacted).

patterns: Tell me about preventing toothache
What is preventing toothache?
Explain preventing toothache
responses: The best way to avoid getting toothache and other dental problems is to keep your teeth and gums as healthy as possible.

patterns: What are the symptoms of Tourette's syndrome?
Can you tell me about the symptoms of Tourette's syndrome?
What signs should I look for in Tourette's syndrome?
How can I identify the symptoms of Tourette's syndrome?
What tics are common in Tourette's syndrome?
Explain the symptoms of Tourette's syndrome to me
responses: The main symptoms of Tourette’s syndrome are tics, which can be categorized into different types. Motor tics might include eye blinking, neck and head jerks, arm and leg movements, pinching, kicking, hitting, and even falling to the ground. Vocal tics can involve throat clearing, grunting, sniffing, coughing, repeating words or phrases, animal sounds, stuttering, and singing. Swearing is a rare tic that affects a small number of people with Tourette’s syndrome.

patterns: Tell me about causes of tourette’s syndrome
What is causes of tourette’s syndrome?
Explain causes of tourette’s syndrome
responses: The cause of Tourette’s syndrome is unknown, and it’s not clear why more males are likely to be affected by the condition than females. Sometimes, tics run in families, suggesting a potential genetic component, but the exact cause remains unclear.

patterns: How is Tourette’s syndrome diagnosed?
What’s involved in diagnosing Tourette’s syndrome?
Can you explain how Tourette’s syndrome is diagnosed?
What tests are used to diagnose Tourette’s syndrome?
How do doctors diagnose Tourette’s syndrome?
What are the steps to get diagnosed with Tourette’s syndrome?
responses: There’s no single test for Tourette’s syndrome, but you might be offered tests and scans, like an MRI, to rule out other conditions. A diagnosis is typically made if you’ve had several movement and sound tics for at least a year, with symptoms starting before the age of 18. Your GP may refer you to specialists, such as a psychiatrist or neurologist, who specializes in brain and nervous system conditions. Getting a confirmed diagnosis is important as it helps you and others understand the condition and provides access to treatment and support.

patterns: Tell me about treating tourette’s syndrome
What is treating tourette’s syndrome?
Explain treating tourette’s syndrome
responses: There’s no cure for Tourette’s syndrome. Most children with tics do not need treatment for them. Tics can significantly improve or disappear altogether as patients move into their early 20s. However, some people find their tics continue or worsen in adulthood.Treatment might be recommended to help you control your tics. There are treatment options available through the NHS.

patterns: What are the symptoms of a TIA?
Can you tell me the symptoms of a TIA?
What are the signs of a TIA?
How can I recognize the symptoms of a TIA?
What should I look for to identify a TIA?
Explain the symptoms of a TIA
responses: The signs and symptoms of a TIA (Transient Ischaemic Attack) usually begin suddenly, much like a stroke. The main symptoms can be remembered using the acronym FAST: Face – the face might droop on one side, the person may not be able to smile or their eyelid may droop; Arms – the person may not be able to lift both arms or keep them up due to weakness or numbness in one arm; Speech – speech may be slurred, garbled, or the person might be unable to speak, despite being awake; Time – if you notice any of these symptoms, it's crucial to phone 999 immediately. Being aware of these symptoms is particularly important if you care for someone at higher risk, such as the elderly or those with diabetes or high blood pressure. It's also important to note that a person having a TIA or stroke might not exhibit all these symptoms. In some cases, TIAs may cause other symptoms, including complete paralysis of one side of the body, sudden loss or blurring of vision, dizziness, confusion, difficulty understanding others, or problems with balance and coordination. While these symptoms could indicate a TIA or stroke, other conditions may also cause them. It’s not possible to tell whether you're experiencing a TIA or a full stroke during the event, so you should call 999 immediately. Even if the symptoms subside, urgent assessment by a healthcare professional is necessary. A TIA serves as a warning of a potential full stroke, and an assessment can help determine the best steps to reduce the risk of it happening.

patterns: Tell me about causes of a tia
What is causes of a tia?
Explain causes of a tia
responses: TIAs happen when there is a temporary disruption in the blood supply to part of the brain.In TIAs, the blockage resolves before any significant damage. A full stroke disrupts the blood flow to your brain for much longer. This leads to more severe damage to the brain and longer-term problems.The blockage responsible for most TIAs is usually caused by a blood clot that has travelled to the blood vessels supplying the brain.A type of irregular heartbeat called atrial fibrillation can also cause TIAs. It can create blood clots that escape from the heart and become stuck in the blood vessels that supply the brain.

patterns: Who is at risk of having a TIA?
What factors increase the risk of a TIA?
Who is most likely to experience a TIA?
Can you tell me who is at risk of a TIA?
What are the risk factors for having a TIA?
Explain who is at risk for a TIA
responses: There are several factors that can increase the likelihood of having a stroke or TIA, including a poor diet, lack of exercise, obesity, smoking, excessive alcohol intake, high cholesterol, high blood pressure (hypertension), diabetes, and atrial fibrillation. Making lifestyle changes to address some of these risk factors can help, and you may also need to take regular medication. For example, if you have atrial fibrillation, taking anticoagulant medication can help reduce the risk of stroke. If you've already experienced a stroke or TIA, it’s even more important to manage these risk factors, as you’re more likely to have another stroke in the future. However, some risk factors are beyond your control, such as age (with people over 65 being at higher risk, though strokes can happen at any age, including in children), family history (if a close relative has had a stroke, your risk is higher), ethnicity (South Asian, African, or Caribbean individuals may have a higher risk, partly due to higher rates of diabetes and high blood pressure in these groups), and medical history (if you’ve had a stroke, TIA, or heart attack, your risk of stroke is increased).

patterns: Tell me about treating a tia
What is treating a tia?
Explain treating a tia
responses: Although the symptoms of a TIA stop on their own, you’ll need treatment to help reduce the risk of another TIA or a full stroke.Appropriate treatment following a TIA can help to reduce your risk of having one.Your doctor will tell you about lifestyle changes you can make to reduce your stroke risk. Your doctor will prescribe medication to treat the underlying cause of your TIA. You might need surgery as part of your treatment.

patterns: Tell me about symptoms of transverse myelitis
What is symptoms of transverse myelitis?
Explain symptoms of transverse myelitis
responses: The symptoms of transverse myelitis can include muscle weakness in the legs, and sometimes the arms, mobility problems, unusual sensations and numbness, bladder and bowel problems, sexual issues, and pain. Transverse myelitis can affect anyone at any stage of life, and while most people experience only one episode of symptoms, it can occur repeatedly in some cases. It can also be the first sign of multiple sclerosis (MS) or another underlying condition.

patterns: Tell me about causes of transverse myelitis
What is causes of transverse myelitis?
Explain causes of transverse myelitis
responses: There are several different causes of transverse myelitis. Sometimes a cause isn’t found, and this is called idiopathic transverse myelitis.

patterns: Tell me about diagnosing transverse myelitis
What is diagnosing transverse myelitis?
Explain diagnosing transverse myelitis
responses: If your GP suspects you may have transverse myelitis, they will refer you to a neurologist, a specialist in conditions of the nervous system, for a thorough assessment. To confirm the diagnosis, you may need several tests, including a neurological examination, magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) scan, lumbar puncture, and blood tests. In some cases, your neurologist may also perform additional tests, such as CT scans, to look for other conditions that could cause transverse myelitis.

patterns: Tell me about treating transverse myelitis
What is treating transverse myelitis?
Explain treating transverse myelitis
responses: Sometimes transverse myelitis requires no treatment as it will improve on its own. Sometimes patients may require treatment for their symptoms, the swelling, or the underlying cause.

patterns: Tell me about symptoms of trichomonas
What is symptoms of trichomonas?
Explain symptoms of trichomonas
responses: Most people with a trichomonas infection may not experience any symptoms, but they can still transmit the infection to others. If symptoms do develop, they may include a yellow or green discharge from the vagina or penis, which can sometimes have an unpleasant smell, genital itching and soreness, and pain or a burning sensation when urinating. Some women may also experience pain or discomfort during sex.

patterns: Tell me about testing for trichomonas
What is testing for trichomonas?
Explain testing for trichomonas
responses: If you think you might have trichomonas, get tested for free by:booking an appointment at your local sexual health service

patterns: Tell me about treating trichomonas
What is treating trichomonas?
Explain treating trichomonas
responses: Trichomonas infection is treated with antibiotics, and it's recommended that both you and your partner(s) receive treatment if you have the infection. After treatment, you and your partner(s) may be asked to return to your GP practice or sexual health clinic for a follow-up test to ensure the infection has cleared. It's important to avoid having sex until one week after you and your partner(s) have completed the treatment. If left untreated, the infection can be passed on to other sexual partners. A GP, or General Practitioner, is a medical doctor who provides primary care, diagnoses health issues, and refers patients to specialists when necessary.

patterns: Tell me about how trichomonas is passed on
how trichomonas is passed on?
Explain how trichomonas is passed on
responses: Trichomonas infection is spread through unprotected sex and sharing sex toys, but it is not passed on through oral or anal sex. You cannot transmit trichomonas through kissing or hugging, sharing cups, plates, or cutlery, or sitting on toilet seats.

patterns: Tell me about how to prevent trichomonas
how to prevent trichomonas?
Explain how to prevent trichomonas
responses: The best way to reduce your risk of STIs is to practice safer sex. This means using a condom for vaginal, anal and oral sex.

patterns: Tell me about about trigeminal neuralgia
What is about trigeminal neuralgia?
Explain about trigeminal neuralgia
responses: Trigeminal neuralgia is a condition characterized by sudden, severe facial pain, often described as sharp, shooting, or like an electric shock. The pain typically occurs in short, intense attacks lasting from a few seconds to about two minutes, stopping just as abruptly as it starts. It most commonly affects part or all of one side of the face, with the pain usually concentrated in the lower part of the face. Though rare, it can sometimes affect both sides of the face, though not at the same time. People with trigeminal neuralgia may experience frequent attacks of pain over days, weeks, or months, and in severe cases, attacks can occur hundreds of times a day. While the pain can improve or disappear for extended periods (known as a period of remission), these periods tend to shorten over time, and some individuals may develop continuous aching, throbbing, and burning sensations, occasionally accompanied by the sharp pain. The pain is often triggered by activities that involve lightly touching the face, such as washing, eating, or brushing teeth, but it can also be triggered by wind (even a slight breeze or air conditioning) or movement of the face or head. In some cases, the pain can occur without any apparent trigger. Living with trigeminal neuralgia can be extremely challenging, significantly impacting a person’s quality of life, leading to issues such as weight loss, isolation, and depression.

patterns: Tell me about when to seek medical advice
when to see medical advice?
Explain when to see medical advice
responses: You should see your GP if you experience frequent or persistent facial pain, especially if standard painkillers like paracetamol and ibuprofen do not provide relief and a dentist has ruled out any dental causes. Your GP will work to identify the issue by asking about your symptoms and eliminating other conditions that might be responsible for your pain. However, diagnosing trigeminal neuralgia can be challenging, and it may take several years for a diagnosis to be confirmed.

patterns: Tell me about what causes trigeminal neuralgia?
What is  causes trigeminal neuralgia??
Explain what causes trigeminal neuralgia?
responses: In the vast majority of cases, trigeminal neuralgia is caused by compression of the trigeminal nerve, the largest nerve inside the skull, which transmits sensations of pain and touch from your face, teeth, and mouth to your brain. This compression is usually due to a nearby blood vessel pressing on part of the nerve inside the skull. In rare cases, trigeminal neuralgia can occur as a result of damage to the trigeminal nerve, often caused by underlying conditions such as multiple sclerosis (MS) or a tumor.

patterns: Who is affected by trigeminal neuralgia?
Who gets trigeminal neuralgia?
What groups are most affected by trigeminal neuralgia?
Can you explain who is at risk for trigeminal neuralgia?
Who is likely to have trigeminal neuralgia?
responses: Trigeminal neuralgia is considered a rare condition, and it’s not entirely clear how many people are affected. Some studies suggest that around 27 in every 100,000 people are diagnosed with it in the UK each year, although this figure may be inflated due to over-diagnosis by doctors. Other estimates indicate that approximately 6,500 new cases are diagnosed annually in the UK. The condition is more common in women than men and is rare in individuals under 40, although it can occur in younger people. Most cases are first diagnosed in people between the ages of 50 and 60.

patterns: Tell me about how trigeminal neuralgia is treated
how trigeminal neuralgia is treated?
Explain how trigeminal neuralgia is treated
responses: Trigeminal neuralgia is typically a long-term condition, and the periods of remission often become shorter over time. However, most cases can be controlled to some extent with treatment. The first treatment offered is usually an anticonvulsant medication, commonly used to treat epilepsy, called carbamazepine. To be effective, this medication needs to be taken several times a day, with the dose gradually increased over several days or weeks to build up sufficient levels in the bloodstream. Unless the pain diminishes or disappears, carbamazepine is usually continued for as long as necessary, sometimes for many years. If a period of remission occurs and the pain goes away, the medication should be stopped slowly over days or weeks unless advised otherwise by a doctor. While carbamazepine was not originally designed to treat pain, it helps relieve nerve pain by slowing down electrical impulses in the nerves and reducing their ability to transmit pain signals. If this medication is ineffective, unsuitable, or causes too many side effects, you may be referred to a specialist for alternative medications or surgical options. There are minor surgical procedures that can treat trigeminal neuralgia by damaging the nerve to stop it from transmitting pain, but these procedures are generally effective for only a few years. Alternatively, a specialist may recommend surgery to open the skull and move blood vessels compressing the trigeminal nerve. Research shows that this surgery offers the best long-term pain relief, though it is a major operation with risks of serious complications such as hearing loss, facial numbness, or, very rarely, a stroke.

patterns: Tell me about about tuberculosis
What is about tuberculosis?
Explain about tuberculosis
responses: Tuberculosis (TB) is a bacterial infection that spreads through inhaling tiny droplets from the coughs or sneezes of an infected person. It is a serious condition, but with proper treatment, it can be cured. While TB primarily affects the lungs, it can also impact other parts of the body, including the glands, bones, and nervous system.

patterns: Tell me about symptoms of tuberculosis
What is symptoms of tuberculosis?
Explain symptoms of tuberculosis
responses: Typical symptoms of TB include a persistent cough that lasts more than three weeks, often bringing up phlegm that may be bloody, weight loss, night sweats, high temperature (fever), tiredness and fatigue, loss of appetite, and new swellings that haven’t gone away after a few weeks. You should see a GP if you have a cough lasting more than three weeks or if you cough up blood.

patterns: Tell me about what causes tuberculosis?
What is what causes tuberculosis??
Explain what causes tuberculosis?
responses: TB is caused by a bacterium called *Mycobacterium tuberculosis*. The most contagious type of TB is the one that affects the lungs, but it usually only spreads after prolonged exposure to someone with the illness, such as within a household. In most healthy people, the immune system kills the bacteria, and no symptoms develop. However, sometimes the immune system cannot kill the bacteria but can prevent it from spreading in the body. This results in no symptoms, but the bacteria remain in the body, a condition known as 'latent TB'. If the immune system fails to kill or contain the infection, it can spread within the lungs or other parts of the body, and symptoms will develop within a few weeks or months, leading to 'active TB'. Latent TB may later develop into active TB, particularly if the immune system becomes weakened.

patterns: Who is most affected by tuberculosis?
Who is at risk for tuberculosis?
Who can get tuberculosis?
Can you explain who is affected by TB?
Who is most likely to have tuberculosis?
responses: Before antibiotics were introduced, tuberculosis (TB) was a major health problem in the UK. However, the condition is now much less common. In the last 20 years, though, TB cases have gradually increased, particularly among ethnic minority communities from countries where TB is more prevalent. It’s estimated that around one-third of the world’s population is infected with latent TB, and of these individuals, up to 10% may develop active TB at some point.

patterns: How is tuberculosis treated?
What is the treatment for TB?
Can you explain how tuberculosis is treated?
How do doctors treat TB?
What kind of treatment is used for TB?
responses: With treatment, tuberculosis (TB) can usually be cured. Most people will need a course of antibiotics, typically for 6 months. Several different antibiotics are used because some forms of TB are resistant to certain medications. If you are infected with a drug-resistant form of TB, treatment may involve 6 or more different medications. If you have been in close contact with someone who has TB, tests may be done to determine if you are also infected. These tests may include a chest X-ray, blood tests, and a skin test known as the Mantoux test.

patterns: Tell me about tuberculosis vaccination
What is tuberculosis vaccination?
Explain tuberculosis vaccination
responses: The BCG vaccine can provide effective protection against tuberculosis (TB) in up to 8 out of 10 people who receive it. Currently, BCG vaccinations are recommended only for groups at higher risk of developing TB, such as children living in areas with high TB rates, those with close family members from countries where TB is common, and individuals under the age of 16 planning to live and work in areas with high TB rates for more than three months. It's also recommended for certain people, such as healthcare workers, due to their increased risk of contracting TB while working.

patterns: Tell me about about type 1 diabetes
What is type 1 diabetes?
Explain about type 1 diabetes
responses: Diabetes is a lifelong condition that causes a person’s blood glucose (sugar) level to become too high. The hormone insulin, produced by the pancreas, is responsible for controlling the amount of glucose in the blood. There are two main types of diabetes: Type 1, where the pancreas doesn’t produce any insulin, and Type 2, where the pancreas doesn’t produce enough insulin or the body’s cells don’t react to insulin. This topic focuses on Type 1 diabetes, though another type, gestational diabetes, can occur in some pregnant women and usually disappears after birth. It’s very important for diabetes to be diagnosed as soon as possible, as it can worsen if left untreated. Symptoms include feeling thirsty, passing urine more often than usual, and losing weight unexpectedly, so you should visit your GP if you experience any of these signs.

Type 1 diabetes can develop at any age but usually appears before the age of 40, particularly in childhood. Around 10% of all diabetes cases are type 1. In type 1 diabetes, the pancreas, a small gland behind the stomach, progressively reduces the amount of insulin it produces until it stops producing any at all. If the glucose level in the blood becomes too high, it can, over time, seriously damage the body’s organs.

patterns: Tell me about diabetes type 1 symptoms
What is diabetes type 1 symptoms?
Explain diabetes type 1 symptoms
responses: The symptoms of diabetes occur because the lack of insulin means that glucose is high but isn’t used by your muscles as fuel for energy. When blood glucose levels are high, glucose is lost in your urine, which can lead to dehydration. Typical symptoms include feeling very thirsty, passing urine more often than usual (particularly at night), feeling very tired all the time, weight loss, loss of muscle bulk, and persistent infections such as thrush. In young people, the symptoms of type 1 diabetes usually develop very quickly, often over a few hours or days, while in adults, the symptoms tend to develop more gradually, taking a few days or weeks.

patterns: Tell me about causes of type 1 diabetes
What is causes of type 1 diabetes?
Explain causes of type 1 diabetes
responses: Type 1 diabetes is an autoimmune condition where the immune system mistakenly attacks the cells in the pancreas that produce insulin. Without insulin, the body starts to break down its own fat and muscle, leading to weight loss. This can cause a serious short-term condition known as diabetic ketoacidosis, where the bloodstream becomes acidic, dangerous levels of ketones accumulate, and severe dehydration occurs. As a result, the body is unable to produce insulin, which is necessary to move glucose from the blood into cells to be used for energy, leading to the development of Type 1 diabetes.

patterns: Tell me about treating type 1 diabetes
What is treating type 1 diabetes ?
Explain treating type 1 diabetes
responses: It’s important to diagnose diabetes as early as possible, as untreated type 1 diabetes is life-threatening. Although diabetes can’t be cured, treatment focuses on keeping blood glucose levels as normal as possible and preventing future health problems. If diagnosed, you will be referred to a diabetes care team for specialist treatment and monitoring. Since the body can’t produce insulin, regular insulin injections are necessary to control glucose levels. You’ll learn how to inject insulin and match it to the carbohydrates in the food you eat, as well as considering your blood glucose level and exercise. Insulin comes in different forms, often requiring a combination of preparations. Some patients use continuous infusion insulin (pump therapy), where a small device pumps insulin into the bloodstream through a cannula under the skin. There are alternatives like islet cell transplantation (implanting healthy insulin-producing cells from a donor pancreas) or, more rarely, a complete pancreas transplant, though these are only suitable for a small number of patients.

patterns: Tell me about complications of type 1 diabetes
What is complications of type 1 diabetes?
Explain complications of type 1 diabetes
responses: If diabetes is left untreated, it can lead to various health problems. Excess glucose in the bloodstream can damage blood vessels, nerves, and organs. Consistently high glucose levels, even without symptoms, can cause long-term harm. Additionally, complications can arise if insulin levels are not properly balanced with food intake and exercise. It is important to work with your diabetes care team to manage insulin levels effectively and prevent these issues.

patterns: Tell me about living with type 1 diabetes
What is it like living with diabetes?
Explain what it’s like to live with type 1 diabetes
responses: If you have type 1 diabetes, you’ll need to look after your health very carefully, as this will make treating your diabetes easier and reduce your risk of complications. Eating a healthy, balanced diet and exercising regularly can help lower your blood glucose levels, and stopping smoking will reduce your risk of cardiovascular disease. Your healthcare team will guide you, and you can track your progress through tools like My Diabetes My Way. It’s important to learn the skills to self-manage your diabetes, and support is available to help you. Additionally, your eyes are at risk from diabetic retinopathy, which can cause sight loss if untreated, so everyone with diabetes aged 12 or older should have their eyes screened annually.

patterns: Tell me about about type 2 diabetes
What is about type 2 diabetes?
Explain about type 2 diabetes
responses: Diabetes is usually a lifelong condition that causes a person’s blood glucose (sugar) level to become too high. The hormone insulin, which is produced by the pancreas, is responsible for controlling the amount of glucose in the blood. There are two main types of diabetes: type 1, where the pancreas doesn’t produce any insulin, and type 2, where the pancreas doesn’t produce enough insulin or the body’s cells don’t react to insulin.

patterns: Tell me about symptoms of diabetes type 2
What is symptoms of diabetes type 2?
Explain symptoms of diabetes type 2
responses: The symptoms of diabetes occur because the lack of insulin means glucose stays in the blood and isn’t used as fuel for energy. Your body tries to reduce blood glucose levels by getting rid of the excess glucose in your urine. Typical symptoms include feeling very thirsty, passing urine more often than usual, particularly at night, feeling very tired, weight loss and loss of muscle bulk, slow-to-heal cuts or ulcers, frequent vaginal or penile thrush, and blurred vision.

patterns: Tell me about causes of type 2 diabetes
What is causes of type 2 diabetes?
Explain causes of type 2 diabetes
responses: Type 2 diabetes occurs when the body doesn’t produce enough insulin to function properly, or the body’s cells don’t react to insulin. This means glucose stays in the blood and isn’t used as fuel for energy.

Type 2 diabetes is often associated with obesity and tends to be diagnosed in older people. Due to increased obesity, type 2 diabetes is now being seen in young people and all ages. It’s far more common than type 1 diabetes.

patterns: Tell me about treating type 2 diabetes
What is treating type 2 diabetes?
Explain treating type 2 diabetes
responses: Type 2 diabetes is treated with changes in your diet and depending on the response of your blood glucose levels, sometimes tablets and insulin. Early in the course of type 2 diabetes, planned weight loss can even reverse the disease.

patterns: Tell me about complications of type 2 diabetes
What is complications of type 2 diabetes?
Explain complications of type 2 diabetes
responses: Diabetes can cause serious long-term health problems and is the most common cause of vision loss and blindness in people of working age. To help prevent these complications, everyone with diabetes aged 12 or over should be invited to have their eyes screened once a year for diabetic retinopathy. Additionally, diabetes is responsible for most cases of kidney failure and lower limb amputation, aside from accidents.

patterns: Tell me about preventing type 2 diabetes
What is preventing type 2 diabetes?
Explain preventing type 2 diabetes
responses: If you're at risk of developing type 2 diabetes, you may be able to prevent it by making certain lifestyle changes. These include losing weight if you're overweight and maintaining a healthy weight, eating a healthy, balanced diet, stopping smoking if you smoke, drinking alcohol in moderation, and taking plenty of regular exercise.

patterns: Tell me about living with type 2 diabetes
What is living with type 2 diabetes?
Explain living with type 2 diabetes
responses: If you already have type 2 diabetes, it may be possible to control your symptoms by making the above changes. This also minimises your risk of developing complications.

patterns: What are the symptoms of ulcerative colitis?
Can you tell me about the symptoms of ulcerative colitis?
What symptoms should I look out for with ulcerative colitis?
Explain the symptoms of ulcerative colitis.
responses: The main symptoms of ulcerative colitis include recurring diarrhoea, which may contain blood, mucus, or pus, abdominal pain, and the need to poo frequently. Additionally, you may experience extreme tiredness (fatigue), loss of appetite, and weight loss. Some people may also have symptoms in other parts of their body, such as painful and swollen joints (arthritis), mouth ulcers, painful, red, and swollen skin, and irritated, red eyes.

patterns: Tell me about the causes
What causes ulcerative colitis?
Explain the causes of ulcerative colitis
responses: The exact cause of ulcerative colitis is unknown, but a combination of factors may be responsible. These include problems with the immune system, where it mistakenly attacks the lining of the large bowel, causing inflammation; genetics, where inherited genes from your family may play a role; and environmental factors, such as air pollution, medication, and viruses or bacteria in your gut.

patterns: Tell me about the diagnosis process
How is ulcerative colitis diagnosed?
What is the process for diagnosing ulcerative colitis?
How do doctors diagnose ulcerative colitis?
responses: To help diagnose ulcerative colitis, your GP will ask about your pattern of symptoms, your diet, any recent travel (for example, whether you may have developed travelers' diarrhoea), if you're taking any medication, including over-the-counter medicines, if anyone else in your family has a bowel condition, and any health problems you've had in the past. Your GP may also examine your abdomen (tummy), ask for a stool (poo) sample and use a qFIT test to check for blood and mucus, infection, or inflammation via a faecal calprotectin test, and arrange blood tests to check for inflammation or anaemia.

patterns: Tell me about ulcerative colitis treatment
What is the treatment for ulcerative colitis?
Can you explain ulcerative colitis treatment?
responses: Treatment for ulcerative colitis aims to relieve and prevent symptoms, with the approach depending on the severity of symptoms and how often flare-ups occur. Mild flare-ups can typically be managed at home, while severe flare-ups may require hospital treatment to minimize the risk of complications. Treatment is usually provided by a range of healthcare professionals.

patterns: Tell me about fertility and pregnancy
What is fertility and pregnancy?
Explain fertility and pregnancy
responses: A woman’s chance of becoming pregnant is generally not affected by ulcerative colitis, though surgery for the condition, particularly pouch surgery, may impact fertility. Most women with ulcerative colitis can have a normal pregnancy and a healthy baby, but if you're pregnant or planning a pregnancy, it's important to discuss it with your IBD team, as they may need to adjust your medication. Additionally, if you're experiencing a flare-up of ulcerative colitis, you may be advised to avoid getting pregnant until the condition is under control.

patterns: Tell me about the complications of ulcerative colitis
What complications can arise from ulcerative colitis?
Explain the complications of ulcerative colitis
responses: If you have ulcerative colitis, you may be at an increased risk of developing other problems, such as osteoporosis and poor growth and development in children and young people. More serious and rare complications can include primary sclerosing cholangitis (PSC), which is inflammation in the bile ducts that can lead to liver damage, narrowing in the bowel (strictures) that may become blocked or make it difficult for stool to pass through, and toxic megacolon, where severe inflammation in the colon can cause trapped gas, leading to a very enlarged colon that may potentially rupture. Additionally, individuals with ulcerative colitis are at a higher risk of developing bowel cancer and blood clots in the legs or lungs.

patterns: Tell me about about underactive thyroids
What about underactive thyroids?
Explain about underactive thyroids
responses: An underactive thyroid gland (hypothyroidism) is where your thyroid gland doesn’t produce enough hormones.
Common signs of an underactive thyroid are tiredness, weight gain and feeling depressed.
An underactive thyroid can often be successfully treated by taking daily hormone tablets to replace the hormones your thyroid isn’t making.

There’s no way of preventing an underactive thyroid. Most cases are caused either by the immune system attacking the thyroid gland and damaging it, or by damage to the thyroid that occurs during some treatments for an overactive thyroid or thyroid cancer.

patterns: Tell me about when to see your gp
What is when to see your gp?
Explain when to see your gp
responses: Symptoms of an underactive thyroid often resemble those of other conditions and usually develop slowly, which means you may not notice them for years. If you experience symptoms such as tiredness, weight gain, depression, sensitivity to the cold, dry skin and hair, or muscle aches, you should see your GP and ask to be tested for an underactive thyroid. The only accurate way to determine if you have a thyroid problem is through a thyroid function test, where a blood sample is taken to measure your hormone levels.

patterns: Tell me about who’s affected
What is who’s affected?
Explain who’s affected
responses: Both men and women can have an underactive thyroid, although it’s more common in women. In the UK, it affects 15 in every 1,000 women and 1 in 1,000 men. Children can also develop an underactive thyroid.

Around 1 in 3,500 to 4,000 babies are born with an underactive thyroid (congenital hypothyroidism). All babies born in the UK are screened for congenital hypothyroidism using a blood spot test when the baby is about 5 days old.

patterns: Tell me about treating an underactive thyroid
What is treating an underactive thyroid?
Explain treating an underactive thyroid
responses: Treatment for an underactive thyroid involves taking daily hormone replacement tablets, called levothyroxine, to raise your thyroxine levels. You’ll usually need treatment for the rest of your life. However, with proper treatment, you should be able to lead a normal, healthy life.

If an underactive thyroid isn’t treated, it can lead to complications, including heart disease, goitre, pregnancy problems and a life-threatening condition called myxoedema coma (although this is very rare).
Read more about treating an underactive thyroid and the complications of an underactive thyroid

patterns: Tell me about how to prevent urinary incontinence
What is how to prevent urinary incontinence?
Explain how to prevent urinary incontinence
responses: There are things you can do that may help reduce the chance of urinary incontinence in the future.

patterns: Tell me about types of urinary incontinence
What is types of urinary incontinence?
Explain types of urinary incontinence
responses: The 2 most common types of urinary incontinence are:
stress incontinence – pee leaking out when your bladder is under pressure (life when you laugh, cough, sneeze, exercise or lift something heavy)
urge incontinence – feeling a sudden and very intense need to pee and being unable to stop it
Less common types of incontinence are:
mixed incontinence – having both stress and urge incontinence
overflow incontinence (also called chronic urinary retention) – the bladder cannot completely empty
total incontinence – severe and continuous urinary incontinence

patterns: Tell me about what causes urinary incontinence?
What is what causes urinary incontinence??
Explain what causes urinary incontinence?
responses: The causes of urinary incontinence depend on the type.
Certain things can increase the chances of urinary incontinence developing, including:
being overweight
a family history of incontinence
getting older – but incontinence doesn’t happen just because you’ve aged
if you’re a woman, pregnancy and vaginal birth
if you’re a man, having surgery to remove the prostate gland

patterns: Tell me about diagnosing urinary incontinence
What is diagnosing urinary incontinence?
Explain diagnosing urinary incontinence
responses: Your GP will ask you some questions to understand what may be causing the incontinence and how bad it is.
They may also:
try to rule out other things that could be causing your symptoms, such as a urinary tract infection
suggest you keep a diary of how much fluid you drink and how often you have to pee
carry out a pelvic examination (in women) or rectal examination (in men) – as this is an intimate examination, there may be another person (chaperone) present
refer you to a specialist, usually a physiotherapist

patterns: Tell me about treatment for urinary incontinence
What is treatment for urinary incontinence?
Explain treatment for urinary incontinence
responses: Treatments which do not involve taking medication or having surgery are usually tried first. These include:
treating the underlying cause, if urinary incontinence is caused by another condition
lifestyle changes – such as reducing your caffeine intake, changing how much you drink, and maintaining a healthy weight
pelvic floor muscle training – your healthcare professional will explain how to do these exercises
bladder training – techniques to increase the length of time between feeling the need to go to the toilet and peeing
Incontinence products (like pads or pants) aren’t a treatment for urinary incontinence. But you might find them helpful while you are waiting for assessment or for a treatment to start working.
If these treatments don’t work for you, your doctor may suggest trying other treatments, like medication or surgery. This will depend on the cause and type of incontinence you are experiencing.
It’s ok to ask any questions about your care to help you get the information you might need.

patterns: Tell me about how to prevent urinary incontinence
What is how to prevent urinary incontinence?
Explain how to prevent urinary incontinence
responses: You shouldn’t wait for symptoms to start. There are things you can do that may help reduce the chance of urinary incontinence in the future.

patterns: Tell me about types of urinary incontinence
What is types of urinary incontinence?
Explain types of urinary incontinence
responses: The 2 most common types of urinary incontinence are:
stress incontinence – when urine leaks out when your bladder is under pressure (when you laugh, cough, sneeze, are heavy lifting or exercising)
urge incontinence – when you feel a sudden and very intense need to pass urine and are unable to stop it
You may experience both types of incontinence.
Certain things can increase the chances of urinary incontinence developing, including:
pregnancy and vaginal birth
being overweight
a family history of incontinence
getting older – but incontinence isn’t an inevitable part of ageing

patterns: Tell me about when to speak to your gp practice
What is when to speak to your gp practice?
Explain when to speak to your gp practice
responses: Urinary incontinence is a common problem, estimated to affect 1 in 3 people in their lifetime.

patterns: Tell me about diagnosing urinary incontinence
What is diagnosing urinary incontinence?
Explain diagnosing urinary incontinence
responses: Your doctor may firstly look to rule out other things that could be causing your symptoms, such as a urinary tract infection. Depending on your symptoms they may refer you to a specialist, usually a physiotherapist.
Your doctor, nurse or physiotherapist might suggest that you keep a bladder diary.

They’ll tell you how to keep a bladder diary and the information that they would like you to provide. This will give them more information about it’s affecting your day to day life and what your best treatment options might be.

Your doctor may also undertake a pelvic examination. This may involve an internal examination to check your womb (uterus) and pelvis as well as your tummy (abdomen). As this is an intimate examination, the doctor may have another person (chaperone) present. You can ask for a female doctor if you prefer. If there isn’t a female doctor available, you can ask if there’s another female health professional who can carry out the examination. The internal examination is similar to a smear test and should only take a few minutes.

In some cases of incontinence, your doctor may find that you have a prolapse. They’ll explain this to you at the time. The doctor may also ask you to squeeze during an internal examination so that they can check your pelvic floor muscles.

patterns: Tell me about treatment for urinary incontinence
What is treatment for urinary incontinence?
Explain treatment for urinary incontinence
responses: Treatments which do not involve taking medication or having surgery are usually tried first.

patterns: Tell me about urinary infection self-help guide
What is urinary infection self-help guide?
Explain urinary infection self-help guide
responses: Complete this self-help guide to check your symptoms and find out what to do next.

patterns: Tell me about treatment for utis
What is treatment for utis?
Explain treatment for utis
responses: UTIs are normally treated with a short term course of antibiotics.
Most women are given a 3-day course of antibiotic capsules or tablets. Men, pregnant women and people with more serious symptoms may need a slightly longer course.
Your symptoms will normally pass within 3 to 5 days of starting treatment. Make sure you complete the whole course of antibiotics that you’ve been prescribed, even if you’re feeling better.
Over-the-counter pain relief such as paracetamol can help with any pain. Drinking plenty of fluids may also help you feel better.
Return to your GP if your symptoms don’t improve, get worse, or come back after treatment.

patterns: Tell me about causes of utis
What is causes of utis?
Explain causes of utis
responses: UTIs occur when the urinary tract becomes infected, usually by bacteria. In most cases, bacteria from the gut enter the urinary tract through the urethra.
This may happen when wiping your bottom or having sex. But often it’s not clear why it happens.
The following may increase your risk of getting a UTI:
conditions that obstruct your urinary tract, such as kidney stones
difficulty emptying your bladder fully
using a contraceptive diaphragm or condoms coated in spermicide
diabetes
a weak immune system – for example from chemotherapy or HIV
a urinary catheter (a tube in your bladder used to drain urine)
an enlarged prostate gland in men
Women may be more likely to get UTIs because their urethra is shorter than a man’s and is closer to their anus (back passage).

patterns: Tell me about preventing utis
What is preventing utis?
Explain preventing utis
responses: There are some things you can do to try to prevent UTIs.

patterns: Tell me about types of uti
What is types of uti?
Explain types of uti
responses: A UTI may be either:
upper
lower
An upper UTI means an infection of the kidneys or ureters. Ureters are the tubes connecting the kidneys to the bladder.
A lower UTI means an infection of the bladder (cystitis) or urethra. This is the tube that carries urine out of the body.

patterns: Tell me about causes of utis in children
What is causes of utis in children?
Explain causes of utis in children
responses: UTIs occur when the urinary tract becomes infected, usually by bacteria.
Bacteria from the gut can enter the urinary tract through the tube that carries pee out of the body (urethra). This may happen when a child:
wipes their bottom
soils their nappy
Some children may be more likely to get UTIs due to problems emptying their bladder. This can be due to:
constipation
dysfunctional elimination syndrome – a child ‘holds on’ to their pee, even though they have the urge to pee
vesicoureteral reflux – urine leaks back up from the bladder into the ureters and kidney

patterns: Tell me about treating utis in children
What is treating utis in children?
Explain treating utis in children
responses: UTIs in children will often improve within 24 to 48 hours of treatment. A UTI won’t usually cause any long term problems.

patterns: Tell me about preventing utis in children
What is preventing utis in children?
Explain preventing utis in children
responses: It isn’t possible to prevent all childhood UTIs. But, there are some things you can do to reduce the risk of your child getting one.

patterns: Tell me about recurrent utis in children
What is recurrent utis in children?
Explain recurrent utis in children
responses: A small number of children have recurring UTIs. If your child has had a UTI before, it’s important to watch out for any symptoms.
If any symptoms do come back, tell your GP as soon as possible.
